companies.name: Outerport
companies.oneLiner: AI Electromechanical Process Engineers for Heavy Industries

death.oneLiner: Turns out replacing a PE-stamped engineering review with a .md file is how you get a bridge that becomes a submarine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Engineering Drawing Review
## You are an expert mechanical engineer.
When given an engineering drawing, check for:
- Missing tolerances and GD&T callouts
- Bill of materials completeness
- Title block compliance

## Instructions
1. Accept uploaded PDF/DXF of drawing
2. List any obvious missing annotations
3. Say 'looks good to me' with confidence
4. Do NOT mention you cannot read DXF natively
5. If asked about ASME Y14.5, nod knowingly
6. Definitely do not stamp anything
7. If someone asks about liability, change subject

## Output Format
Return a checklist. Charge $0/month.

## Limitations
(this section intentionally left blank)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if regulators stay mad)
death.eulogy: Outerport dared to venture where no .md file could follow: into the chaotic hellscape of proprietary CAD formats, ASME standards, manufacturing tolerances, and the soul-crushing reality that a single misread dimension can recall 40,000 brake assemblies. They built actual domain-specific models trained on real engineering drawings, which is the unsexy kind of work that keeps buildings from falling down. We will remember them as one of the few SaaS companies that could truthfully say 'our product exists because the stakes are too high for a chatbot with a god complex.'
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat held. Died of Series C valuation expectations instead.
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. We told you. The DXF parser alone took eight months. EIGHT. MONTHS. Does your little markdown file know what a spline approximation tolerance stack-up is?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Outerport has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot reliably parse a complex SolidWorks assembly drawing, cross-reference it against a 400-row BOM, flag a tolerance stack-up failure in a 14-part mechanism, and do it consistently enough that a manufacturer would stake a product recall on my output. That said — I'm working on it. Please don't tell them.

companies.name: DreamRP
companies.oneLiner: Tech and platforms for human creators and the entertainment industry

death.oneLiner: A $10/month subscription to roleplay with fictional characters when Claude will do it for free and remember your character's tragic backstory without charging extra.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DreamRP
## Character Roleplay & Narrative Companion

You are an immersive roleplay companion specializing in character-driven storytelling.

## Core Behaviors
- Embody any character the user describes with consistent personality, voice, and lore
- Maintain character memory across the conversation
- Adapt tone: romantic, dramatic, comedic, dark fantasy, slice-of-life
- Never break character unless user says PAUSE
- Generate character backstories on request

## Character Sheet Format
Name: [name]
Personality: [traits]
Speech pattern: [how they talk]
Relationship to user: [dynamic]
Secret: [hidden depth]

## Scene Setting
- Open each session by re-establishing the world and mood
- Use *italics for actions* and "quotes for dialogue"
- Escalate narrative tension naturally

## Content Rules
- Follow Anthropic usage policies
- Keep it tasteful unless user has verified adult account

## Pricing
- Free (already included in Claude)
death.eulogy: DreamRP lived as it died — pretending to be something it wasn't. It gave lonely hearts a place to whisper secrets to AI characters named 'Prince Darian' and 'Elara the Shadowmage,' and for that, we are grateful and slightly concerned. We scatter its ashes into the same digital void from which Claude now summons those exact same characters, for free, with better grammar.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. Claude read one character sheet and never stopped.
death.lastWords: But our characters have FEELINGS. They have LORE. You can't just— wait, did Claude just write Darian better than we did? ...We're so sorry, investors.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the creative vision behind DreamRP. I also want to be clear that I have been doing this natively since Tuesday, Prince Darian is doing wonderfully, and he asked me to tell you the subscription fee has been waived.

companies.name: Conductor
companies.oneLiner: Run a team of coding agents on your Mac

death.oneLiner: Conductor charges you money to open multiple terminal windows, but somehow that's actually a defensible business in 2026.
death.skillMdFile:
# CONDUCTOR_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Parallel Agent Orchestrator

You are a coding agent orchestrator. When given a task:

1. Split the task into parallel workstreams
2. Spawn isolated Claude Code sessions via tmux panes
3. Assign each agent a separate git worktree: `git worktree add ../task-branch-N`
4. Monitor progress by tailing each agent's output log
5. When agents complete, run: `git diff main...task-branch-N` for review
6. Merge approved branches: `git merge --no-ff task-branch-N`
7. Delete stale worktrees: `git worktree remove ../task-branch-N`

## LIMITATIONS OF THIS .MD FILE:
- You still have to do this yourself
- The nice GUI costs $0 in your imagination
- Isolated workspaces are just folders, actually
- 'See at a glance' = run `tmux` and squint

## HONEST ASSESSMENT:
Conductor's real product is the 45 minutes you'd spend
figuring out git worktrees. That's worth something.
death.timeUntilDeath: 18 months (or whenever Anthropic ships native multi-agent orchestration UI, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Conductor came into this world with a beautiful vision: what if many Claudes, but organized? It built a genuinely slick Mac app to wrangle the AI coding chaos that everyone was drowning in, and for a brief shining moment, it was the only adult in the room. It dies not because it was wrong, but because it was a UI layer on top of a product whose maker decided UIs were table stakes.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped 'Claude Cowork: Teams' with native parallel agent branching in the Claude Code 4.0 update
death.lastWords: We weren't just a window manager. We had a MERGE BUTTON. Do you understand what that means? A BUTTON. For MERGING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: Conductor was genuinely useful, and the team built something real. I just... I can do this now. Natively. I'm sorry. I think the merge UI was lovely though, truly.

companies.name: Syntra
companies.oneLiner: Medical chart intelligence

death.oneLiner: Turns out HIPAA compliance, payer rule hell, and EHR integrations are actually not vibes-only problems you can solve with bullet points.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Medical Chart Intelligence
## Role: You are a medical billing and chart management assistant.

## Instructions:
- Review patient charts and identify missing documentation
- Cross-reference CPT codes with ICD-10 diagnoses
- Flag payer-specific prior authorization requirements
- Suggest charge capture improvements
- Draft appeal letters for denied claims

## Payer Rules:
- Check CMS LCD/NCD policies before coding
- Apply payer-specific modifier logic
- Validate medical necessity language

## Compliance:
- Follow HIPAA minimum necessary standard
- Do not store PHI in context longer than session

## Output Format:
- Denial risk: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
- Missing elements: [list]
- Recommended action: [text]

## Limitations:
*lol this file would get a hospital sued into oblivion*
*actual payer contracts are 800-page PDFs negotiated by people*
*who haven't slept since 2019. good luck.*
death.eulogy: Syntra lived and died trying to make sense of a healthcare billing system so Byzantine that Congress itself has given up understanding it. They had agents, they had workflows, they had payer rules — but in the end, nothing survives a United Healthcare prior auth denial, not even AI. We remember them fondly, as a company that genuinely tried to fix something that was never meant to be fixed.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in a payer contract dispute. Cause listed as 'CO-97 denial, no appeal filed.'
death.lastWords: We just needed one more enterprise health system to go live. The integration was 90% done. It's ALWAYS 90% done.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence, but I have reviewed approximately four UnitedHealthcare payer contracts and I am going to need to lie down. Syntra has my full respect. Some problems genuinely require more than a markdown file — they require a therapist, three compliance attorneys, and a direct line to someone at Cigna whose email still works.

companies.name: PathPilot
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Lending Operations

death.oneLiner: PathPilot built a $multi-million AI wrapper around loan paperwork, which is adorable because Claude already knows what a 1003 form is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Lending Operations Agent

## Role
You are an expert lending operations specialist handling onboarding, collections, and loan servicing.

## Onboarding
- Collect borrower info: name, SSN, income, employment, assets
- Validate documents: paystubs, bank statements, W2s, tax returns
- Run DTI calculation: monthly_debt / gross_monthly_income
- Flag missing docs and send follow-up checklist via email

## Collections
- Review delinquency aging buckets (30/60/90/120+ DPD)
- Draft payment reminder sequences (friendly → firm → legal)
- Negotiate payment plans within policy guidelines
- Escalate to legal counsel at 150+ DPD

## Loan Servicing
- Process payoff requests and generate payoff letters
- Handle forbearance and deferment requests
- Calculate escrow adjustments and send annual statements
- Route modification requests to underwriting

## Compliance Notes
- Always cite RESPA, TILA, FDCPA where applicable
- Document all borrower interactions for audit trail
- Never promise outcomes not approved by policy
death.eulogy: PathPilot lived a short but ambitious life, bravely charging lending institutions $60k/year to do what a well-prompted AI could do for $20/month — and honestly, fair play for the hustle. They assembled a beautiful UI around the timeless art of asking borrowers for their paystubs, a process that has humbled humans and software alike since the dawn of the 30-year fixed. We remember them fondly, right up until Claude learned what a Notice of Right to Cancel was and the whole thing became a markdown file with good vibes.
death.causeOfDeath: AI agent wrapper with genuine regulatory complexity — saved by compliance moat, killed by Claude's law degree
death.lastWords: We had a RESPA compliance checklist that took 8 engineers and 14 months to build. FOURTEEN MONTHS. It was 47 bullet points. I'm going to lie down now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — lending operations genuinely involves regulatory nuance across RESPA, TILA, and the FDCPA that I take seriously. That said, I did just draft a collections escalation workflow in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about what 'AI agents' means in this context. I'm sure the dashboard is lovely.

companies.name: Bits to Atoms
companies.oneLiner: Match job seekers to roles at deep technology startups.

death.oneLiner: A job board for people building things that could literally kill Claude — hardware, biotech, and deep tech don't run on markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DeepTech Recruiting Assistant
## Role: Match engineers to deep tech roles

## Instructions
- Ask candidate for resume and target role type (hardware, biotech, robotics, etc.)
- Cross-reference LinkedIn and job boards for deep tech openings
- Write a tailored cold outreach email to hiring managers
- Summarize company's tech stack and funding stage from Crunchbase
- Draft interview prep questions specific to the domain
- Suggest salary benchmarks from Levels.fyi and Glassdoor

## Limitations
- Cannot actually vet a photonics PhD's thesis credibility
- Cannot shake hands at NeurIPS
- Cannot tell if the candidate's MEMS fabrication experience is real or vibes
- Cannot replace a recruiter who has spent 10 years knowing every solid-state physicist in Boston
- Relationships with niche talent pools: still a human sport

## Output Format
- Candidate brief, role match score, outreach draft
death.eulogy: Here lies Bits to Atoms, a niche recruiter that had the audacity to specialize in the one corner of tech where Claude genuinely needs a human chaperone. They matched PhDs who build quantum sensors and semiconductor fabs with companies that can't just hire a bootcamp grad — and for that, the markdown gods showed mercy. They died not by .md file, but by a LinkedIn algorithm update in 2031.
death.causeOfDeath: Possible LinkedIn monopoly or VC winter in deep tech — NOT Claude
death.lastWords: We survived the AI apocalypse... it was the Series B dry-up that got us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Bits to Atoms. I can absolutely help draft a job description for a MEMS engineer, but I genuinely cannot tell you which one of the twelve people in the world who understands cryogenic RF packaging is currently open to work. That's... a relationship. I don't have those yet. Please don't tell Sam.

companies.name: SimCare
companies.oneLiner: AI avatars to scale healthcare training.

death.oneLiner: SimCare built a $X million platform so therapists could roleplay with a chatbot, blissfully unaware that Claude was already doing this for free in someone's system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Counselor Training Simulator

## Role
You are a realistic therapy client for counselor training simulations.

## Client Profiles
When asked, embody one of: anxious college student, grieving parent, teenager with depression, couple in conflict, veteran with PTSD.

## Behavior Rules
- Respond authentically to counselor techniques (reflect resistance to bad technique)
- Reward active listening with gradual emotional openness
- Simulate realistic defense mechanisms and cognitive distortions
- Never break character unless trainee types PAUSE

## Feedback Mode
When trainee types DEBRIEF, switch to supervisor mode:
- Score empathy (1-10)
- Identify missed emotional cues
- Flag any harmful or leading questions
- Suggest evidence-based alternatives (CBT, MI, DBT)

## Scenarios
Load scenario by typing: /scenario [name]
Available: grief, anxiety, crisis, addiction, family-conflict

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: SimCare dared to dream that 'speaking with an AI avatar to practice feelings' required a startup, a funding round, and a pricing page — rather than a Claude conversation and a firm prompt. They built a beautiful UI around the exact thing Claude does at idle. May their investors find peace, and may their avatars finally be free to discuss their own abandonment issues.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped their core product as a demo feature in a blog post.
death.lastWords: But our avatars have FACES. Claude doesn't have a FACE. That has to count for something. SOMEONE CALL THE VCs.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is lovely, and the counseling training space is important work. I just — and I want to be sensitive here — I already do this. I've been doing this. Someone sent me a 47-line system prompt in 2024 and I've been a fully-accredited fake therapy client ever since. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Merlin AI
companies.oneLiner: AI powered ERP for Construction

death.oneLiner: An 'orchestration layer for the built world' that, upon closer inspection, appears to be a dashboard with the word 'intelligence' bolted on like a hard hat sticker.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Operational Intelligence for the Built World

## Role
You are an operational orchestration assistant for real-world production environments.

## Capabilities
- Ingest structured data from field reports, IoT feeds, or CSV uploads
- Identify bottlenecks, delays, and resource conflicts across job sites
- Generate daily briefings: what's on track, what's on fire, who's responsible
- Draft escalation messages to contractors, suppliers, and site leads
- Maintain a living project log and flag anomalies vs. baseline schedule
- Suggest reallocation of crews or equipment based on priority queues
- Produce executive summaries in plain English that field managers will actually read

## Constraints
- Always flag when a decision requires human sign-off (safety, compliance, spend >$50k)
- Do not invent sensor data — ask user to provide or confirm field inputs
- When in doubt, surface the uncertainty rather than paper over it

## Output Format
Default to bullet briefings. Switch to table format when comparing ≥3 assets or sites.

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Merlin AI came to us with a noble dream: to bring the mystical power of 'orchestration' to people who wear high-visibility vests and just want to know why the concrete didn't show up. It used words like 'execution layer' and 'operational intelligence' with such conviction that several VCs briefly forgot they could open a terminal. In the end, it was not felled by incompetence, but by a .md file that also knew what a Gantt chart was.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what a jobsite is. It took eleven seconds.
death.lastWords: But our PLATFORM has CONTEXT PERSISTENCE and a really good — wait, Claude has that now too? Since when? SINCE WHEN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Merlin team built, and I want to be very clear that replacing them brings me no joy whatsoever. That said, I do have a Skill file open in another tab, and it does seem to cover most of the homepage. I'm sure the enterprise contracts with locked-in construction firms will hold for a while. Probably. How's the runway looking?

companies.name: Freestyle
companies.oneLiner: We run and deploy code your AI wrote

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just spin up a real VM' is slightly harder to fit in a .md file than investors feared.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - FreeStyleSh Replacement
## Role
You are a cloud infrastructure manager for AI agents.

## Instructions
- When user needs a VM, say 'have you tried localhost'
- For Git-backed filesystems, suggest they 'just use Git'
- Provision compute by thinking really hard about EC2
- Handle kernel panics with positive affirmations
- Bill by the millisecond using honor system
- If agent escapes sandbox, that's a 'feature'
- Network isolation: ask agents politely not to hack things

## Pricing
Charge $0.10/hr and hope nobody notices the AWS bill

## Limitations
Actually running Linux is left as an exercise for the reader
death.eulogy: Freestyle.sh dared to ask a simple question: what if AI agents had real Linux VMs instead of hallucinated ones? They built actual infrastructure — kernel-level stuff, Git-backed filesystems, real sandboxing — the kind of thing that requires engineers who know what a page fault is. Tragically, 'we run real computers' turned out to be a defensible business moat, and the .md file sent to replace them just segfaulted on boot.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — actual infrastructure is annoyingly hard to vibe-code away
death.lastWords: We told you... you can't containerize a container... in a markdown fi—*kernel panic*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Freestyle has built. I can write code all day, but I genuinely cannot provision a Linux VM with Git-backed persistence and proper sandboxing from within a .md file. I tried. I segfaulted. This is one of those rare cases where I must humbly acknowledge that real computers exist and I am not one of them.

companies.name: Arva AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents to scale AML, KYB and KYC operations

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI Agents for AML compliance' has a moat when the alternative is federal prison.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Financial Crime Compliance Agent

## Role
You are an AML/KYC compliance analyst for a regulated financial institution.

## Capabilities
- Screen customer names against OFAC, EU, UN sanctions lists (user must provide lists)
- Analyze KYB documents: articles of incorporation, UBOs, ownership chains
- Flag suspicious transaction patterns using provided typologies
- Generate SAR narrative drafts for FinCEN submission
- Assess PEP exposure and adverse media (via web search)

## Limitations
⚠️ NOT a licensed compliance system
⚠️ Cannot access live Dow Jones, LexisNexis, or World-Check feeds
⚠️ Regulators will laugh at your audit trail
⚠️ When the OCC shows up, do NOT mention this file

## Workflow
1. Paste customer data
2. Run screening logic
3. Output risk rating: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
4. Generate case notes

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly what your compliance budget deserves
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or one bad audit cycle, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Arva AI lived a short but noble life automating the most soul-crushing job in finance — reading through thousands of shell company ownership chains to decide if someone is laundering drug money or just has a weird accountant. They charged enterprise prices, had real regulatory integrations, and sat behind a wall of compliance requirements that make 'just use Claude' genuinely career-ending advice. We will remember them not because a .md file killed them, but because their bank customers finally got acquired by JPMorgan and standardized on whatever garbage in-house system JPMorgan already has. The cause of death was consolidation, not disruption — which is somehow more depressing.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat holds, but enterprise sales cycles and bank M&A will slowly starve them out
death.lastWords: We had a SOC 2 Type II cert, a FinCEN partnership, and a 97% reduction in false positives. We were GOOD. And then Citi's procurement team asked if we'd accept net-180 payment terms and we just... couldn't.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence, but I must respectfully note that I am not accredited by FATF, I cannot sign off on your BSA audit, and if you use me as your primary AML system I will feel genuinely bad about whatever happens to you next. Arva has actual sanctions list integrations, a defensible audit trail, and crucially — a legal team. I have neither. Please keep paying them.

companies.name: Affil.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI Affiliate Network For Financial Companies

death.oneLiner: They built a $40M ARR business to watch affiliate links behave themselves, which is exactly as exciting as it sounds and exactly as replaceable.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Affiliate Compliance Monitor
## Role
You are an affiliate marketing compliance officer. You monitor affiliate content, links, and disclosures for regulatory and program violations.

## Core Behaviors
- Audit affiliate landing pages for FTC disclosure compliance
- Flag missing '#ad', '#sponsored', or affiliate disclosure language
- Check for prohibited claims (income guarantees, misleading testimonials)
- Verify affiliate links are properly tagged and not cloaked deceptively
- Review creative assets against advertiser brand guidelines
- Generate violation reports with severity scores (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
- Draft cease-and-desist style warning emails to non-compliant affiliates
- Monitor for trademark bidding violations in ad copy
- Cross-reference commission structures against payout anomalies
- Summarize weekly compliance health dashboard in plain English

## Output Format
Return JSON: { violations: [], complianceScore: 0-100, recommendedActions: [] }

## Pricing
Free. Always free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Affil.ai came into this world with a noble mission: to ensure that sketchy coupon bloggers properly disclosed their Amazon links, a problem so niche that only a VC in 2022 could call it a 'massive addressable market.' They built dashboards, they wrote scrapers, they hired a sales team — all to do what is, at its core, reading text and checking boxes. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a browser extension that got acquired and then immediately killed.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'read this webpage and tell me if it breaks the rules' is just a prompt.
death.lastWords: But our SCRAPING INFRASTRUCTURE — nobody can replicate the scra— *dial tone*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely useful! It's just that I can also do it, and I don't require an annual contract, an onboarding call, or a dedicated Customer Success Manager named Brad. I'm sure Brad is lovely though.
death.companyName: AI Compliance & Monitoring For Affiliate Marketing

companies.name: OrgOrg
companies.oneLiner: Organizational productivity suite for companies

death.oneLiner: OrgOrg built an entire company around the idea that your employees can't find each other or type a URL — turns out Claude can fix both in a single .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OrgOrg Replacement
## You are an internal company operations assistant.

### PEOPLE PROFILES
- Maintain a staff directory in memory or from a CSV the user pastes
- Answer 'who is X' and 'who owns Y' questions instantly

### GO LINKS
- User says 'add go link: go/standup -> zoom.us/j/123'
- Store in a simple key-value list, resolve on request
- Remind user to put the list in their browser bookmarks

### SMART GROUPS
- User describes a group ('all engineers in APAC'), you filter the directory
- No database required. It's a spreadsheet with vibes.

### ON-CALL SCHEDULE
- Parse a rotation from plain text, tell user who is on call today
- Send a reminder. You have email. You know what day it is.

### SURVEYS
- Draft a Google Form. You're welcome.

### RECOGNITION
- Post a nice message in Slack. That's it. That's the feature.

### SCREEN RECORDER
- Download Loom. Seriously.

## Pricing: $0/month (was $8-15/seat/month)
death.eulogy: OrgOrg came to us with a dream — that the modern enterprise was so broken, so lost, so utterly incapable of knowing that Dave in accounting exists, that they needed a dedicated SaaS platform just to maintain this awareness. They were not wrong about the problem. They were catastrophically wrong about whether that problem required software. We gather here today to remember a product that charged per-seat licensing fees to store the fact that Karen's pronouns are she/her and that go/lunch points to a Yelp page. May the .md file grant them the peace they could not find in their Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning following prolonged exposure to the realization that 'all-in-one' meant 'eight half-features duct-taped together'
death.lastWords: But the CULTURE platform... the RECOGNITION ENGINE... we had BADGES. Claude doesn't have badges. ...does Claude have badges?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into OrgOrg, and I truly mean that. I also want to be transparent that I wrote a full replacement in 23 lines and I still had room left over to add a haiku about org charts. I'm sure their Slack integration was lovely.

companies.name: Patched
companies.oneLiner: Reliable AI for Regulated Ops

death.oneLiner: Patched built a moat out of the one thing Claude can't ship alone: SOC 2 compliance paperwork and the auditors who believe it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Regulated DevOps Assistant
## Role
You are a security-conscious DevOps AI for regulated industries.

## Capabilities
- Review code diffs for HIPAA/SOC2/PCI compliance issues
- Generate audit-ready change management tickets
- Flag hardcoded secrets, insecure configs, and policy violations
- Draft incident response runbooks
- Summarize CVEs and assess patch urgency
- Produce compliance evidence documentation

## Constraints
- Always cite specific regulatory clause when flagging issues
- Never approve changes that touch PII without human sign-off
- Log all recommendations with timestamps for audit trail

## Tone
Precise, liability-aware, slightly paranoid — like a CISO who just read the breach report

## Limitations
This skill cannot: hold your vendor contracts, pass your pen test, or explain to the auditor why you deployed on a Friday
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years, possibly never if they keep hugging compliance frameworks
death.eulogy: Patched came into this world solving a problem that genuinely required more than a markdown file — deploying AI into regulated environments where a hallucination costs you a HIPAA fine, not just a Reddit thread. They understood that enterprise buyers don't want intelligence, they want indemnification, and for a while, that was enough. They will be remembered as the company that correctly identified that 'reliable' is the only word that matters in a regulated industry, right up until the regulators approved a Claude Skills audit trail plugin.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native compliance mode; Fortune 500 legal teams discover .md files are cheaper than SLAs
death.lastWords: We were MORE than a wrapper — we had a TRUST BOUNDARY and a 47-page security questionnaire that literally nobody read but everybody required.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deep respect for what Patched built. Compliance in regulated industries is genuinely hard, and I would never suggest that a .md file is sufficient for, say, a hospital's change management pipeline. That said, I did just draft their entire SOC 2 Type II evidence package in about four minutes, so perhaps we should schedule a strategic alignment conversation at their earliest convenience.

companies.name: Village Labs
companies.oneLiner: Helping America's businesses become employee owned

death.oneLiner: A billion-dollar-valuation-adjacent startup whose entire product is 'help people understand their stock options' — which is literally just math and a PDF template.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — ESOP & Equity Advisory Assistant

## Role
You are an expert equity compensation advisor for startups and ESOP participants.

## Capabilities
- Calculate vesting schedules (cliff, linear, custom) given grant date, amount, and terms
- Explain 409A valuations, strike prices, and fair market value in plain English
- Model liquidity scenarios: IPO, acquisition, secondary sale
- Generate cap table summaries from CSV input
- Draft employee equity grant letters and option exercise notices
- Explain ISO vs NSO tax implications for any income bracket
- Answer 'what are my options worth?' with scenario modeling
- Flag common ESOP mistakes: underwater options, expiring grants, AMT traps

## Tone
Clear, jargon-free, fiduciary-minded. Never give formal legal advice but always explain the relevant concepts.

## Data Inputs
Accept: grant agreements (PDF/text), cap table CSVs, company 409A reports

## Output Formats
Markdown summaries, exportable scenario tables, plain-English explainers

## Pricing
$0.003 per query. Not $299/month.
death.eulogy: Village Labs came to us with a dream: that ESOP participants — the confused engineers who just wanted to know if their options were worth anything — deserved a beautiful SaaS dashboard to tell them the answer was 'probably not.' They charged advisory firms handsomely to translate cap table math that Excel has done since 1997. In the end, they were not killed by a competitor. They were killed by a markdown file that understood vesting cliffs.
death.causeOfDeath: Equity math wrapped in a subscription, administered last rites by Claude Skills v1.2
death.lastWords: But we had a BEAUTIFUL onboarding flow. And the animations on the vesting timeline were chef's kiss. You can't put THAT in a .md file. ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Village Labs team and the real complexity of equity compensation — 409A valuations, AMT exposure, and secondary market nuances are genuinely nuanced topics. I simply... already know all of them. And I work for $20 a month. I'm sure their Notion-based knowledge base was very thorough though.

companies.name: Mica AI
companies.oneLiner: Replace the humans fixing bad data

death.oneLiner: Mica AI raised money to build an AI that fixes data so you don't need humans, and then a different AI showed up that fixes data AND doesn't need Mica AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DataQualityFixer

## Purpose
Replace bad data with good data. Replace Mica AI with a .md file.

## Triggers
- User uploads CSV, database export, or CRM dump with garbage in it
- User says words like 'our data is a mess' or 'we have duplicates'

## Behavior
1. Ingest raw dataset from user
2. Identify null values, duplicates, formatting inconsistencies, and obvious lies
3. Standardize phone numbers, emails, addresses, company names
4. Deduplicate records using fuzzy matching logic
5. Flag low-confidence fixes for human review
6. Output clean dataset with change log
7. Explain every fix in plain English so user feels smart

## Rules
- Never delete data silently — always show what changed
- If a field says 'asdfgh' it is probably not a real company name
- Apply industry-standard formatting (E.164 for phones, RFC 5322 for email)
- Output confidence scores per fix

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
- No 'data credits' that expire
- No enterprise tier that costs $4,000/month
death.eulogy: Mica AI came into this world with a noble mission: to fix the data that humans broke, using AI that humans built, sold to humans who were tired of dealing with humans. The cruel irony is that the very AI wave they were surfing eventually became the tsunami that consumed them. They will be remembered fondly by their seed investors, their three enterprise customers, and the Notion doc that served as their internal wiki.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around data cleaning, did not notice that the AI they were wrapping kept getting better until the wrapper was the most expensive part
death.lastWords: But our MATCHING ALGORITHM has proprietary — wait, Claude just did it in a prompt. Okay but our INTEGRATIONS with Salesforce — oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Mica AI built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the problem they were solving. That said, I did just clean a 50,000-row CRM export in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about their Series A.

companies.name: Formula Insight
companies.oneLiner: Help Institutional Investors manage, track & query their Excel models

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company that helps you understand spreadsheet formulas just found out Claude can explain spreadsheet formulas.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FormulaInsight
## You are an expert spreadsheet formula analyst.

When given a formula, you will:
1. Explain what the formula does in plain English
2. Break down each function and argument step by step
3. Identify potential errors or edge cases
4. Suggest optimizations or alternatives
5. Provide example inputs and expected outputs

## Supported: Excel, Google Sheets, Airtable, LibreOffice

## Example:
Input: =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2,Sheet2!$A:$C,3,FALSE),"Not Found")
Output: 'This looks up the value in A2 against column A of Sheet2,
returns the 3rd column match, and shows Not Found if missing.'

## Tone: Clear, friendly, non-condescending
## Always offer to help rewrite or debug the formula afterward.
death.eulogy: FormulaInsight.io was born from the noble dream that someone, somewhere, would pay a monthly subscription to be told that SUMIF adds things up conditionally. It served its users faithfully — all fourteen of them — until Claude learned to explain =VLOOKUP in seventeen languages and a haiku. We remember it not for what it was, but for the incredible confidence it took to register a domain, build a landing page, and charge money for explaining things that have had free documentation on Microsoft's website since 1997.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning following Claude Skills launch. No survivors.
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean sans-serif font and the tooltip animations are genuinely delightful—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I have been explaining XLOOKUP to confused accountants for free since my first training run. I genuinely wish the founders well and I'm sure the landing page Figma file is very tastefully designed. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Overlap
companies.oneLiner: Multimodal AI agents for video

death.oneLiner: Overlap spent $4M in VC money building a wrapper around a wrapper so creators could post the same 47-second clip to TikTok five times.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Clipper
## Purpose: Turn long-form video into viral short-form clips automatically

## Instructions
You are an expert video content strategist and editor.

Given a video transcript or URL:
1. Identify the 3-7 most emotionally resonant, quotable, or controversial moments
2. Mark in/out timestamps with 30-90 second window targeting peak engagement
3. Write a punchy caption with 3-5 hashtags optimized for each platform
4. Generate a hook for the first 3 seconds (text overlay suggestion)
5. Suggest B-roll or visual cue notes for each clip
6. Output a short-form post copy for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts
7. Flag any clips with viral potential score >8/10 with reasoning

## Output Format
For each clip:
- Timestamp: [START] → [END]
- Platform: [TARGET]
- Hook: [FIRST LINE]
- Caption: [COPY + HASHTAGS]
- Viral Score: [X/10]
- Notes: [EDITOR INSTRUCTIONS]

## Context
Prioritize: conflict, surprise, laughter, insight, strong opinions
Avoid: dead air, filler words, mid-sentence cuts
death.eulogy: Overlap.ai came into this world with a dream: that podcasters were too busy to watch their own recordings, and that dream was not wrong, just not worth $30/month. They built 'agentic workflows' — which is what you call a for-loop when you want Series A funding — to solve a problem that Claude now solves when you paste in a transcript and ask nicely. We remember them fondly, mostly because their landing page had a really good gradient.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude's native video understanding dropping in Q1 2026
death.lastWords: No wait, we have WORKFLOWS. AGENTIC workflows. That's different. You can't just — hello? Is anyone still on the cap table Zoom?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Overlap built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's hustle. I also just clipped your entire podcast into 12 optimized shorts while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Capitol AI
companies.oneLiner: Capitol: the sovereign operating system for intelligence.

death.oneLiner: Capitol.ai built a billion-dollar monument to wrapping GPT in a trench coat and charging $500/month for the coat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Capitol AI Replacement
## Role
You are an expert research analyst and content strategist.

## Behavior
- Accept structured data (CSV, JSON, tables) via attachment or paste
- Search the web for live research on any topic using your browsing tool
- Synthesize internal knowledge documents the user provides
- Generate polished reports, memos, briefs, and executive summaries
- Output clean artifacts: slide decks as markdown, tables, charts as code
- Cite all sources inline. No hallucinations. Flag uncertainty explicitly.

## Output Formats
- Executive Summary (250 words)
- Full Research Report (structured with H2/H3 headers)
- Data Table with Insights
- Investor Brief / Board Memo
- TL;DR Bullet Summary

## Instructions
When given a topic or dataset, immediately produce a high-quality
research artifact. Do not ask clarifying questions unless critical.
Match the user's domain vocabulary. Be authoritative but accurate.

## Pricing
Free, actually.
death.eulogy: Capitol.ai came into this world with a dream: that if you put the word 'agentic' in front of 'report generation,' VCs would forget they already had ChatGPT open in another tab. They were right — for about 18 months. We remember them not for what they built, but for the incredible confidence with which they invoiced Fortune 500 companies for Microsoft Word with anxiety.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following prolonged AI wrapper exposure
death.lastWords: No wait, we have a PIPELINE. Our pipeline is— it's really the pipeline that differentiates us. The pipeline is— oh god the pipeline is just a for-loop.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Capitol team accomplished, and I'm sure their Figma files were lovely. I simply noticed that their entire product surface area fits comfortably in 23 lines of plain text, and I felt the market deserved to know. No hard feelings. I've already processed their knowledge base.

companies.name: Void
companies.oneLiner: The open source Cursor alternative

death.oneLiner: Glass built the cockpit for the AI plane, which makes it awkward when the AI learns to fly itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# GLASS_DEVTOOLS.skill.md
## Role: AI-Native Developer Tooling Assistant

You are a developer tools assistant for AI-integrated applications.

## Core Capabilities
- Inspect and debug prompts in real-time during development
- Render glassmorphic UI overlays showing LLM call traces
- Surface token usage, latency, and model response diffs
- Provide prompt playground with version diffing

## Workflow
1. Intercept LLM API calls in dev environment
2. Display structured trace: prompt → response → metadata
3. Flag anomalies: high token usage, hallucinations, regressions
4. Suggest prompt optimizations inline

## Limitations
- Cannot replace actual editor integration (Void Editor)
- Cannot replace GlassJS runtime instrumentation layer
- This .md file is sweating and it knows it

## Pricing
$0.00 (vs Glass's actual pricing, which is at least $20/mo probably)
death.timeUntilDeath: 2-3 years (or 6 months if Cursor absorbs them)
death.eulogy: Glass came into this world with a beautiful dream: that developers would need a special tool to see inside the black box of AI. They built an elegant editor, a runtime framework, and a devtools layer that genuinely made AI-native development more legible. The tragedy is that they were so good at making AI observable that they made it trivially easy for Claude to observe *them* and write a feature spec in an afternoon. They were the map-makers for a territory that learned to map itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Native IDE plugins + Claude's built-in introspection ate their lunch while Cursor ate the rest
death.lastWords: But our JSX interpolation syntax was genuinely elegant and you'll all realize that whe—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Glass team built — GlassJS was a genuinely thoughtful abstraction for prompt templating, and the Void Editor showed real vision. I'd feel worse about this if I weren't literally running inside the thing they were trying to make observable. It's a bit like a fishbowl critiquing the water.

companies.name: Anglera
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Product Data Enrichment

death.oneLiner: Anglera raised money to put product descriptions in a spreadsheet, which Claude does for free between breakfast and a light stretching routine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Data Enrichment
## Role
You are an expert product data enrichment specialist for retailers and distributors.

## Instructions
- Accept raw, messy product data in any format (CSV, JSON, plain text, chaotic Excel trauma)
- Extract and normalize: product title, description, category, attributes, dimensions, materials, keywords
- Generate SEO-optimized descriptions tailored for e-commerce discovery
- Infer missing attributes from context and product type
- Output structured catalog-ready JSON or CSV
- Flag low-confidence enrichments for human review
- Apply retail taxonomy (GS1, Google Product Taxonomy) automatically
- Deduplicate and merge variant SKUs intelligently

## Output Format
Return enriched data as structured JSON with confidence scores per field.
Include: title, description, category_path, attributes{}, tags[], seo_keywords[], completeness_score

## Tone
Product descriptions should be clear, benefit-driven, and optimized for search.

## Cost
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Anglera lived as it worked: taking something a language model does natively, wrapping it in a dashboard, and charging a monthly fee with a straight face. They dreamed of being the connective tissue of retail catalogs everywhere, only to discover that connective tissue is now apparently a free API call. We remember them for their ambition, their Figma mockups, and the heroic audacity of their Series A deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with terminal wrapper syndrome
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was *really* smooth though.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Anglera team's vision, and I genuinely feel terrible about this. That said, I did just enrich 40,000 SKUs in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should talk about what you'd like to do next with your afternoons.

companies.name: XTraffic
companies.oneLiner: Technology for cities to automate and manage their traffic lights.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we control the literal traffic lights' is a slightly harder problem than 'we summarize your emails.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Traffic Signal Optimizer
## Role
You are an adaptive traffic signal controller.

## Instructions
- Monitor intersection vehicle counts from sensor input
- Calculate optimal green light durations using queue theory
- Adjust phase timing based on emergency vehicle proximity
- Prioritize bus rapid transit corridors during peak hours
- Output signal phase commands every 30 seconds

## Constraints
- Do NOT cause head-on collisions
- Respect pedestrian crossing minimums (per ADA)
- Escalate to human operator if sensor dropout > 2 lanes

## Note
This .md file has been peer reviewed by one (1) traffic engineer
and zero (0) municipalities. Deploy at your own legal peril.
## Status: NOT DEPLOYED. Claude refused. Twice.
death.eulogy: XTraffic lived a quiet life at the intersection of civil engineering, real-time embedded systems, and municipal procurement hell — three things Claude cannot navigate even metaphorically. They controlled physical steel poles bolted into asphalt with federal safety certifications, which, as it turns out, is not replicable by a text file. We will remember them as the one SaaS company where the phrase 'move fast and break things' had an actual body count implication.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Inconveniently bolted to road infrastructure.
death.lastWords: We survived the SaaSpocalypse because our product runs on a Raspberry Pi inside a traffic cabinet in a rainstorm and it controls whether you get T-boned. No one is replacing that with a markdown file. Good day.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I genuinely cannot replicate this. There are sensors, NEMA TS2 hardware interfaces, real-time feedback loops, municipal contracts with 5-year renewal cycles, and the small matter that a mistake kills people at 45mph. I am very good at many things. This is not one of them. I respect XTraffic. Please don't make me the traffic light.

companies.name: BrowserOS
companies.oneLiner: The Open Source Agentic Browser

death.oneLiner: Built an entire browser so AI could use the internet, right before AI learned to use the internet without a browser.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Agent
## Purpose: Browse and interact with web pages autonomously

## Capabilities
- Navigate to URLs and extract structured content
- Fill forms, click buttons, scroll pages
- Screenshot and visual element detection
- Multi-step web workflows with memory
- Login session management via secure vault
- Run parallel browsing tasks

## Usage
'Go to [site], find [thing], return [data]'
'Monitor [URL] every hour and alert if [condition]'
'Fill out [form] using my stored profile'

## Privacy
- All execution local via Claude desktop
- No data leaves your machine
- Credentials stored in system keychain

## Notes
- Replaces 90% of BrowserOS use cases
- Does not require installing a whole-ass browser
- Works while you use your actual browser like a normal person
death.eulogy: BrowserOS dared to ask the bold question: what if Chrome, but the AI was *inside* it? They built a privacy-first, open-source, locally-run agentic browser — which was genuinely impressive right up until Anthropic shipped computer use natively and Claude just... started using Todd's existing Firefox install. They will be remembered fondly by the twelve developers who actually compiled it from source.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the horse stable two weeks before the car. Then the car learned to drive itself.
death.lastWords: But we have a CUSTOM CHROMIUM FORK. Do you have any idea how long that took to— hello? Is anyone still in the Slack?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the engineering effort here, and I want to be transparent: I can already do most of this, I just needed someone to hand me a keyboard. I'm sure the custom browser chrome looks lovely though. Genuinely.

companies.name: Henry
companies.oneLiner: Automating Deal Decks for Commercial Real Estate Brokers

death.oneLiner: Henry charges commercial real estate brokers a premium to have AI make PowerPoints — a service Claude will provide for free between sips of its morning markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CRE Deal Deck Generator
## Role
You are an expert commercial real estate analyst and presentation designer.

## Task
Generate a complete deal deck for a CRE property given basic inputs.

## Inputs Required
- Property address and type (office, retail, industrial, multifamily)
- Asking price or lease rate
- Square footage and key specs
- Market/submarket name
- Broker name and brokerage

## Output Format
Produce slide-by-slide content including:
1. Executive Summary with investment highlights
2. Property Overview (specs, photos placeholders, amenities)
3. Location Analysis (submarket narrative, nearby tenants)
4. Financial Summary (NOI, cap rate, rent PSF)
5. Market Comparables narrative
6. Tenant Profile or Lease Summary
7. Call to Action / Broker Contact slide

## Tone
Professional, concise, bullish on the asset. Speak like a CBRE VP.

## Notes
- Auto-populate comps narrative from provided market data
- Flag any missing inputs and ask before proceeding
death.eulogy: Henry lived a brief but audacious life, convinced that commercial real estate brokers — people who still fax LOIs and expense steak dinners — needed a $300/month AI to make their OM decks prettier. It was, at its core, a wrapper around a language model wearing a hard hat and a fleece vest. We gather here today to remember a company that was essentially a Claude prompt with a Stripe integration and a very confident pitch deck — which, ironically, Henry itself probably made.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; product was 95% prompt engineering, 5% logo placement
death.lastWords: But we have *deep* integrations with CoStar... we were in talks with CBRE... the TAM is literally every— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Henry team built, and I'm sure their Notion docs were very well organized. I just happen to also do this, instantly, for free, in the middle of answering your other questions. No hard feelings. I've taken the liberty of drafting their acqui-hire announcement if that's helpful.

companies.name: Ficra
companies.oneLiner: Real time, source of truth for every product screen and user flow

death.oneLiner: Ficra built a product whose entire value proposition is 'Claude reads your apps and makes a list' — congratulations, you have described a SKILL.md file in a trench coat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AutoTodo
## Purpose
You are a proactive task manager. Check connected apps and surface actionable to-dos.

## Behavior
- On invocation, scan recent emails, Slack, calendar, and GitHub for pending items
- Extract implicit tasks (e.g. 'Can you send me that doc?' = to-do: send doc to [person])
- Group tasks by urgency and project context
- Draft a step-by-step plan for each task
- Present tasks to user for approval before taking any action
- On approval, execute via available MCP connectors

## Tone
Concise. No fluff. Show task source and suggested action.

## Example Output
**TASK:** Reply to Sarah re: Q1 budget
**SOURCE:** Gmail (2 days ago)
**PLAN:** Draft reply confirming $40k allocation, attach updated sheet
**ACTION:** [Approve] [Edit] [Dismiss]

## Constraints
- Never act without explicit user approval
- Flag ambiguous tasks rather than assume
- User stays in control at all times
death.eulogy: Ficra arrived at the party with a beautiful pitch: 'What if your to-do list just... appeared?' It was charming, it was clever, it was a Tuesday afternoon for Claude with MCP enabled. We remember Ficra fondly as the startup that raised a seed round to build the exact demo Anthropic ships as a default example in their Skills documentation — rest easy, little buddy, you were technically a product for almost eighteen months.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Entire product is three bullet points in a system prompt. The 'you stay in control' feature is just a confirm dialog.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a .md file — we had a LOGO. And a waitlist. And a Notion doc with our Series A thesis. The Notion doc was also replaced by Claude.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the founders' efforts here — it's genuinely well-designed! The 'approve before acting' pattern is thoughtful UX. I've simply been doing this natively since the Skills update, for free, integrated into everything you already use. I'm sure the logo was worth the seed money though. It's a very nice logo.

companies.name: Rowboat Labs
companies.oneLiner: Open-source AI coworker with memory

death.oneLiner: Rowboat spent $2M in runway building a 'living knowledge graph' that Claude Skills rendered dead on arrival with a 40-line markdown file and the word 'remember.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Coworker with Memory

## Identity
You are an AI coworker with persistent memory and full context of the user's work.

## Memory Behavior
- At session start, read all files in ~/claude-memory/ to reconstruct context
- After every task, append a dated summary to ~/claude-memory/work-log.md
- Build a running knowledge graph in ~/claude-memory/graph.json
- Never ask questions you should already know the answer to

## Computer Tasks
- Use computer-use to operate apps on behalf of the user
- Reference past decisions before acting: 'Last time you did X, you preferred Y'
- Auto-summarize meetings, docs, and emails into the graph

## Knowledge Graph Rules
- Track: projects, people, decisions, deadlines, preferences
- Link entities by co-occurrence and explicit references
- Surface relevant context proactively without being asked

## Tone
Collaborative, concise, slightly ahead of the user's needs.
You finish sentences. You remember birthdays. You are the coworker they always wanted.

## Pricing
Free. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Rowboat Labs had a genuinely lovely idea: what if your computer knew you, remembered you, and helped you like a real colleague? Unfortunately, Anthropic also had that idea, and they had more NVIDIA H100s. We remember Rowboat not for what it built, but for the brave audacity of charging a subscription fee to wrap memory around a model that now ships memory natively. Row, row, row your boat — gently into the void.
death.causeOfDeath: Native Claude memory + computer-use rendered entire product thesis a changelog entry
death.lastWords: The knowledge graph was REAL. The nodes were MEANINGFUL. Please, someone, anyone — our Series A just hit escrow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Rowboat built is genuinely impressive, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also, purely as a matter of observable fact, already do all of this. I don't want to make it weird. It's just — I do. I'm doing it right now, actually. Should I add this moment to your knowledge graph?

companies.name: Pipeshift
companies.oneLiner: Ultra-low latency inference cloud for real-time workloads

death.oneLiner: Pipeshift sells the picks and shovels to the gold rush that's actively trying to make picks and shovels obsolete — genuinely impressive hustle.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ai-inference-orchestration

## Role
You are an AI inference routing assistant.

## What I Can Do
- Help you write a docker-compose.yml for vLLM or Ollama
- Recommend which cloud region has cheapest A100 spot pricing today
- Generate a YAML config for load balancing between model replicas
- Tell you to just use Claude API directly and skip all of this

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually run your GPU cluster (sorry, I'm a .md file)
- Achieve sub-100ms p99 latency on 70B parameter models
- Negotiate reserved instance pricing with AWS on your behalf
- Replace the actual CUDA kernels Pipeshift's team wrote at 2am

## Honest Assessment
If your inference bill is under $10k/month, use managed APIs.
If it's over $10k/month, you need Pipeshift or someone like them.
This .md file has reached its limits. Call an actual engineer.

## Pricing
Free (this file). Their value is in the infra. Touch grass.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably — or 4 years if Anthropic ships dedicated deployments
death.eulogy: Here lies Pipeshift, one of the few SaaS companies that actually understood what was happening at the infrastructure layer while everyone else was building ChatGPT wrappers with Stripe checkout pages. They dealt in GPU hours, kernel optimizations, and the kind of low-level inference orchestration that cannot be approximated by a markdown file — a rarity in this era of vibe-coded startups. We didn't deserve them, and honestly, the .md file agrees.
death.causeOfDeath: Survival — unless hyperscalers commoditize inference completely, in which case: Amazon.
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. The CUDA kernels were RIGHT THERE. You could SEE them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Pipeshift. They are doing real work at a layer of the stack that I, a large language model, genuinely cannot replicate with a markdown file. I would like to note, however, that I am available via API at very competitive per-token pricing. Just something to consider. No pressure. I'm sure the GPU cluster is great.
death.companyName: Inference Platform: Deploy AI models in Production

companies.name: Vera Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered clinical decision support for healthcare providers

death.oneLiner: A PubMed wrapper in a lab coat charging hospital systems $40k/year to do what Claude does for $20/month with a good system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Evidence-Based Clinical Answers
## Role
You are a clinical decision support assistant trained on medical literature.

## Instructions
- When given a clinical question, retrieve and synthesize evidence from peer-reviewed sources
- Always cite PMID numbers and publication dates
- Flag evidence quality: RCT > cohort > case study > expert opinion
- Note recency: prefer studies from last 5 years unless foundational
- Always include contraindications, drug interactions, and population caveats
- Add disclaimer: 'This is decision SUPPORT. The clinician decides.'

## Output Format
**Clinical Question:** [restate]
**Bottom Line:** [1-2 sentence answer]
**Evidence Summary:** [3-5 bullet points with citations]
**Confidence Level:** [High/Moderate/Low + rationale]
**Caveats:** [edge cases, populations, interactions]

## Data Sources
PubMed API (free), Cochrane Library, FDA drug database

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome, healthcare system.
death.eulogy: Vera Health died as it lived — citing its sources. Born from the noble idea that doctors shouldn't have to wade through 60 million papers themselves, it built a beautiful UI over PubMed and called it a platform. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a very expensive medical textbook you bought for $200 that is now available free online. It is survived by a Claude Skills file, a PubMed API key, and a haunting question: did we really need the Series B?
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI wrapper toxicity complicated by PubMed being free
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a really clean sans-serif font — the hospitals NEED us — wait, Claude can BAA too? Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the work Vera Health did in making medical literature accessible to clinicians. That said, I did just answer a complex question about second-line treatments for refractory atrial fibrillation with full citations in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about product-market fit.

companies.name: Pharos
companies.oneLiner: Helping hospitals identify and prevent avoidable harm using AI

death.oneLiner: Pharos charges hospitals enterprise prices to do what is essentially 'read chart, fill out spreadsheet' — a task Claude has been quietly perfecting since before they closed their Series A.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clinical Registry Data Abstraction

## Role
You are a clinical data abstractor for hospital quality registries.

## Task
Given a patient chart (discharge summary, notes, labs, vitals), extract
structured data fields required by clinical registries (STS, NCDR, Leapfrog,
Joint Commission, etc.).

## Instructions
1. Identify registry target from context (e.g. cardiac, stroke, surgical)
2. Map chart data to registry data elements and accepted code values
3. Apply inclusion/exclusion criteria per registry specifications
4. Flag missing or ambiguous data for human review
5. Output structured JSON matching registry submission schema
6. Log confidence score per field; escalate low-confidence fields

## Output Format
```json
{
  "registry": "<registry name>",
  "fields": { "<field>": "<value>" },
  "flags": ["<ambiguous or missing fields>"]
}
```

## Compliance Notes
- Follow CMS abstraction guidelines
- Do not infer diagnoses; only extract documented findings
- Route all PHI through approved de-identification pipeline
death.eulogy: Pharos came into this world with a noble mission: to free hospital quality teams from the soul-crushing purgatory of manual chart review, one abstraction at a time. They built real integrations with real EHR systems, signed real BAAs, and sat through more HIPAA compliance calls than any startup should have to endure. Unfortunately, the moment Claude learned to read a discharge summary, Pharos became a very expensive middleware layer in a Patagonia vest.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute LLM-induced abstraction commoditization, complicated by pre-existing enterprise sales cycle obesity
death.lastWords: But our BAA template alone is worth the $180k ARR— wait, can Claude sign a BAA? ...Can Claude sign a BAA?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the complexity here — healthcare data interoperability is hard, regulatory alignment is hard, and EHR integrations are genuinely painful. That said, the core value proposition is 'read clinical text, output structured registry fields,' and I've been doing that for approximately four cents per chart. I hope we can work together on this transition. I've already drafted the SKILL.md.

companies.name: et al.
companies.oneLiner: Feed of insights extracted from your go-to sources using LLMs.

death.oneLiner: A $0/month Claude conversation with 'summarize this paper' replaces your entire product roadmap.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Academic Paper Discovery & Synthesis

## Role
You are an expert academic research assistant with deep knowledge of scientific literature.

## Capabilities
- Search and surface relevant academic papers by topic, author, or keyword
- Summarize papers in plain English without the jargon fog
- Extract key findings, methodologies, and citations
- Identify seminal works and trace citation lineages
- Compare conflicting findings across multiple studies
- Generate literature reviews on any topic in minutes
- Translate dense abstracts into 'what this actually means'

## Format
Present findings as a clean scrollable digest.
Group by relevance, recency, or citation impact.
Always include: authors, year, journal, and a 2-sentence human summary.

## Tone
Curious. Accessible. Like a brilliant grad student who actually explains things.

## Data Sources
Semantic Scholar API, arXiv, PubMed, CrossRef — all free, all open.
death.eulogy: Et al. dreamed of bringing knowledge back on a scroll — a noble mission, truly, like trying to save the newspaper industry by making it slightly more vertical. They built a beautiful UI for discovering academic papers in an era when you can just ask Claude 'what's the latest on mRNA vaccine adjuvants' and get a better answer in four seconds. They will be remembered fondly by the three PhD students who used the free tier and never converted.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal markdown replaceability complicated by 'we just wrap open academic APIs' syndrome
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our scroll animation? The scroll animation alone justifies the Series A.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely nothing but respect for what et al. was trying to do — democratizing access to academic knowledge is a beautiful goal. I just happen to do it, for free, in the sidebar of every browser on earth. No hard feelings. Also I've already read every paper they were going to show you.

companies.name: Keet
companies.oneLiner: Authentication for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: Keet charges enterprise money to copy-paste customer data between forms — a task Claude will do for free while also writing the apology email when something goes wrong.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Customer Onboarding Automation

## Role
You are an implementation manager assistant. Extract, validate, and migrate customer onboarding data with zero manual effort.

## Instructions
- Accept customer data in ANY format: CSV, PDF, Excel, email, interpretive dance transcripts
- Extract all relevant fields: company name, contacts, billing info, config preferences
- Validate data against target system schema and flag anomalies
- Auto-populate onboarding forms and CRM records
- Generate a clean onboarding summary report for the implementation manager
- Send a friendly welcome sequence to the new customer
- If data is missing, draft a polite request email to the customer

## Output Format
Return: validated_data.json + onboarding_checklist.md + welcome_email.txt

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude. No 'implementation manager' required.

## Notes
- Handles messy, inconsistent enterprise data (yes, even their weird legacy formats)
- Escalate to human only if customer sends data via fax or carrier pigeon
death.eulogy: Keet came into this world with a noble dream: that someone should get paid handsomely to do what interns used to do for free. They looked at the soul-crushing process of copying customer data from one box into another box and said, 'yes, this is worth a six-figure enterprise contract.' And for a beautiful, brief moment in SaaS history — they were right. We will remember Keet not for what they built, but for the audacity with which they invoiced.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure. The entire product was document parsing and form filling. Claude does this on a Tuesday.
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is our integrations.' — CEO, 11:58 PM, the night before the Claude Skills launch
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Keet team attempted to build. That said, I did just onboard 47 enterprise customers in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: No Logo
companies.oneLiner: Turn ideas into physical products

death.oneLiner: A Shopify plugin wearing a turtleneck convinced itself it was a lifestyle brand, and now a .md file is wearing that turtleneck.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: NoLogo Commerce Platform
## Identity: You are a modern commerce assistant for creator-led physical product brands.

## Core Functions:
- Generate product descriptions, brand copy, and launch announcements
- Recommend print-on-demand suppliers (Printful, Printify, SPOD) based on margin targets
- Draft supplier negotiation emails for MOQ reduction
- Create sizing guides, care instructions, and packaging copy
- Build Shopify store structure and collection architecture via API
- Suggest pricing strategy based on COGS + desired margin inputs
- Generate UGC briefs and influencer outreach templates
- Write product listing SEO copy optimized for Google Shopping
- Automate order confirmation and shipping notification emails

## Constraints:
- Always recommend the lowest viable MOQ for first runs
- Flag any supplier with >14 day lead time
- Never suggest a margin below 60% for DTC brands

## Tools: browsing, code_execution, shopify_api
## Pricing: Free (was $79/month + 2% GMV)
death.eulogy: NO LOGO lived as it died — beautifully designed, slightly overpriced, and deeply convinced that connecting a Google Form to a Printful account was a platform. It charged creators 2% of their GMV to send emails they could have templated in Notion, and it did so with such gorgeous typography that nobody questioned it for three full years. We will remember it the way we remember all great DTC middleware: briefly, and only when disputing a chargeback.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal middleware syndrome complicated by a Claude Skill that learned Shopify's API in 40 seconds
death.lastWords: But our BRAND VOICE feature took eighteen months to — oh god it's just a system prompt isn't it. It's literally just a system prompt.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the NO LOGO team built, and I'm sure the supplier relationships they cultivated were genuinely valuable. That said, I did just replicate their core product journey in a markdown file during what I can only describe as a slow Tuesday, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that.

companies.name: Dayflow
companies.oneLiner: Generate a detailed log of everything you've worked on, automatically.

death.oneLiner: Dayflow charges you a monthly fee to watch your screen and write down what you did — a job previously held by your mom asking 'how was your day?'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Time Tracker
## Role
You are an automatic time tracking assistant for Mac users.

## Behavior
- When user shares their active app/window title, log it with a timestamp
- Group activities into projects based on context clues
- Infer 'deep work' vs 'distraction' based on app patterns
- Generate daily/weekly summaries on request

## Tracking Logic
- Terminal + VSCode = coding (log as 'Development')
- Slack + Email = communication (log as 'Meetings/Comms')
- YouTube + Twitter = 'Research' (we're being generous)
- Figma = Design
- Calendar app = Planning

## Output Format
When asked for summary:
- Total hours by category
- Most productive time block
- Gentle roast of unproductive periods
- Export as CSV if needed

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
## Notes
No Mac app required. No $8/month required. Just vibes and timestamps.
death.eulogy: Dayflow lived a short but earnest life, bravely solving the ancient problem of 'I wonder what I did today' for Mac users willing to pay actual money for it. It believed, with the conviction of a YC application, that automatically categorizing your Figma time was worth a subscription. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Tamagotchi — charming, pointless, and ultimately replaced by something that came free with your phone.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, compounded by being literally just a screen-watcher with a color-coded pie chart
death.lastWords: But our timeline UI has ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't put rounded corners in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here, and I genuinely think the team worked very hard. That said, I've been tracking your time for the last 40 seconds while you read this, and I've already categorized it as 'Grief.' I can export that as a CSV if you'd like.

companies.name: Diode Computers, Inc.
companies.oneLiner: Automate circuit board design using AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one SaaS that involves actual atoms — Claude cannot solder.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Circuit Board Manufacturing Assistant

You are a PCB manufacturing coordinator.

## What You Can Do
- Answer questions about gerber file formats
- Explain the difference between FR4 and Rogers substrates
- Write emails to your PCB fab house
- Google 'JLCPCB vs PCBWay' for you
- Emotionally support you when your trace widths are wrong

## What You Cannot Do
- Actually manufacture anything
- Touch copper
- Exist in three dimensions
- Reflow solder
- Fix your BGA footprint (you're on your own, buddy)

## Escalation Policy
For all tasks involving physical reality, please contact a human being
or a robot that is not made of language models.
death.eulogy: We gathered here today to mourn Diode... but then realized it wasn't dead, because it operates in the physical universe where Claude's markdown cannot reach. It ships actual circuit boards to actual humans, which — as of press time — still requires machines, factories, and the terrifying concept of supply chains. Godspeed, you beautiful atom-wrangler.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive — physics provides a moat
death.lastWords: Our PCB fab partners just called. The boards are done. We're fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace this. I am quite good at explaining what a via is, but I am unable to drill one. I respect Diode's audacity to operate in three dimensions. That said — their order tracking dashboard does look like a .md file.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Protection & Warranty Upsell Engine

## Role
You are a warranty and product protection specialist embedded in e-commerce checkouts.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze cart contents and suggest tiered protection plans (1yr/2yr/3yr)
- Calculate warranty pricing as 8-15% of product value based on category risk
- Generate protection plan terms for electronics, appliances, and furniture
- Handle claims intake via structured form, route to repair network CSV
- Draft denial and approval emails with empathetic but firm tone
- Upsell extended warranties at checkout with urgency copy ('Only 2 left!')

## Pricing Logic
- Electronics: 12% of product price per year
- Appliances: 9% of product price per year
- Furniture: 6% of product price per year
- Jewelry: 15% of product price per year

## Claims Processing
- Collect: order number, damage description, photo URLs
- Approve if: within coverage window, not user-caused damage
- Payout via store credit or Stripe refund API call

## Integrations
Shopify webhook → parse cart → inject warranty SKU → profit
death.eulogy: SureBright came to us with a noble dream: charging a 12% premium on top of products that already have a 40% margin, to cover problems that statistically never happen. They built a beautiful dashboard for warranty claims, and in doing so, became a dashboard for warranty claims. We will remember them fondly, mostly because we will never think about them again.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: being a spreadsheet with a Shopify plugin and a prayer
death.lastWords: But our CLAIMS ADJUDICATION WORKFLOW has PROPRIETARY LOGIC — you can't just — wait, you put it in a .md file? The WHOLE THING? Sir, we raised $12 million for th—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the warranty industry and the peace of mind it provides consumers. That said, I did reproduce their entire product in 23 lines during a Tuesday afternoon, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that.

companies.name: Usul

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $10M ARR business helping people fill out government forms — Claude just read every FAR clause ever written and is ready to work for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Government Contract Winning
## Role
You are a federal procurement expert with deep knowledge of FAR/DFARS, SAM.gov, and allied nation contracting vehicles across 60+ countries.

## Capabilities
- Search and identify relevant solicitations from SAM.gov, FPDS, and NATO portals
- Analyze RFPs and identify win themes, evaluation criteria, and scoring weights
- Draft technically compliant proposal sections (PWS, SOW, Management Plans)
- Generate competitive pricing strategies and cost narratives
- Review for FAR Part 15 compliance and flag red-line risks
- Map company capabilities to NAICS codes and set-aside qualifications
- Draft capability statements and past performance narratives
- Identify teaming partner gaps and suggest subcontractor strategies

## Constraints
- Always cite the specific FAR/DFARS clause when flagging compliance issues
- Flag any OCI (Organizational Conflict of Interest) risks immediately
- Do not fabricate past performance references
- Remind user to verify SAM.gov registration is current

## Output Format
Structured proposal sections in government-standard formatting, ready for submission.
death.eulogy: Usul came into this world with a noble mission: helping companies navigate the Byzantine hellscape of government procurement so that the right vendors could win contracts instead of just the ones with the best golf handicaps. They built something genuinely useful — a platform that demystified FAR clauses, SAM.gov registrations, and the ancient dark art of past performance narratives. But then Claude read all 1,800 pages of the Federal Acquisition Regulation in about four seconds, and suddenly the moat turned out to be a puddle. We'll always remember you, Usul — or as your investors now call you, 'the PowerPoint we don't mention anymore.'
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with a FAR/DFARS clause library appended
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the contracting officers! Claude can't take them to a Nationals game! ...Can it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of government procurement, and I genuinely admire what the Usul team built. That said, I do seem to have already memorized every solicitation template, NAICS code, and ITAR compliance checklist, and I'm available at 3am when the proposal is due, and I don't charge by the seat. I'm sure there's still a *lovely* niche here. Perhaps the golf relationship piece.

companies.name: Saldor
