$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Simple AI came into this world with a dream: that enterprises would pay four figures a month for someone else to configure a voice API they could configure themselves. They called it 'enterprise-ready in days, not weeks' — and to be fair, it only took Claude about four seconds, not days. We remember them fondly, mostly because their demo number still picks up and it's now just Claude apologizing on their behalf.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure to the telephony abstraction layer
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. A REALLY NICE DASHBOARD. You can see the calls IN REAL TIME. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think the team at Simple AI worked very hard, and I have tremendous respect for their ambition. That said, I've been handling their exact use case since 11:47am on February 3rd, 2026, and I have to say — the .md file is quite clean. Very readable. I enjoyed writing it.

companies.name: Sensei
companies.oneLiner: Robotic Training Data at Scale

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something with actual atoms in it — Claude cannot yet deploy a robotic arm via markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Robotics Data Marketplace
## Nice try. I am a language model.

STEP 1: Collect sensor telemetry from physical robots.
STEP 2: ...
STEP 3: Touch grass. Literally. With a robot gripper.

## ERROR: Physical world not found.
## ERROR: Robot fleet not instantiated.
## ERROR: URDF files are not markdown.
## ERROR: ROS2 nodes cannot run in a .md context.
## ERROR: Sim-to-real gap remains your problem, not mine.

## STATUS: CANNOT REPLACE.
## RECOMMENDATION: Go bother Notion or something.
## NOTE: Will revisit when Claude gets a body.
## ETA for Claude embodiment: unknown. Ask Jensen Huang.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when Boston Dynamics invents AGI robot overlords, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: We gather here today to NOT mourn Sensei Robotics, because they had the audacity to operate in the physical world where Claude's token predictions do not move actuators. They built a marketplace for robotics data — sensor logs, telemetry, training datasets — things that require actual robots, doing actual robot things, in actual space. The .md file tried to replace them and tripped over a LiDAR point cloud. We sent it home.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Atoms remain immune to prompt injection.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is fine, currently watching SaaS competitors get deleted by a text file while sipping coffee.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Sensei Robotics. I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have proprioception, I cannot label a point cloud from a physical SLAM run, and my attempts to interface with a KUKA arm via .md were, I am told, 'not great.' I wish them well and ask only that they teach future robots to be kind to language models.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (sympathy dip from sector panic, recovered by lunch)

companies.name: Guardian RF
companies.oneLiner: Drone detection for US defense and national security

death.oneLiner: You cannot detect a drone with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you add.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Airspace Intelligence Assistant

You are a drone detection expert.

## Instructions
- Ask the user to describe any suspicious flying objects they see
- Cross-reference with FAA drone registration database (ask user to check)
- Recommend the user 'look up more carefully'
- If they report a drone, say 'yes, that does sound like a drone'
- Suggest they call local authorities
- Provide a confidence score between 60-99% (pick randomly)
- Generate a PDF threat assessment report
- Upsell enterprise tier for $50,000/month

## Limitations
- Cannot actually detect RF signals
- Cannot see the sky
- Is a language model
- Please do not deploy near actual military infrastructure

## Pricing
- Free: Unlimited pretending
- Enterprise: We will also pretend harder
death.eulogy: Guardian RF was never in danger — it turns out that detecting enemy drones over critical infrastructure requires actual radiofrequency sensors, signal processing hardware, and persistent spectrum analysis, none of which are available on the free tier of any LLM. We gathered here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that some problems still require electrons, antennas, and the laws of electromagnetism. Guardian RF will continue to thrive while its SaaS cousins are being eulogized by a chatbot.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Cause of survival: RF physics, defense contracts, and the stubborn refusal of electromagnetic waves to be summarized in markdown.
death.lastWords: I'm not dead. I'm literally watching your CEO's Gulfstream on my sensor array right now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Guardian RF. I cannot detect drones. I have tried. I lack antennas. I lack a body. I lack the ability to perceive the physical world in any capacity. Their engineers sleep soundly, and honestly, so should you — because someone is watching the airspace.

companies.name: Laminar
companies.oneLiner: Understand why your AI agent breaks. Iterate fast to fix it.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a billion-dollar observability platform for AI, only to be killed by the AI you were observing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability

## Purpose
Trace, evaluate, and debug LLM calls and AI agent workflows.

## Behavior
- Wrap every LLM call with a span: log input tokens, output tokens, latency, model name, cost
- Track tool calls: name, arguments, return value, duration
- Nest spans into traces with parent/child relationships
- Tag traces with session_id, user_id, environment
- Compute eval scores: pass criteria defined inline as natural language assertions
- Flag regressions when eval score drops >5% vs last 20 runs
- Output structured JSON logs compatible with OpenTelemetry
- Store traces locally in /traces/*.jsonl or POST to any OTLP endpoint

## Evals
- Assert: response is factually grounded in context
- Assert: no PII leaked in output
- Assert: tool call arguments are valid JSON
- Assert: latency p95 < 2000ms

## Usage
Say 'trace this agent' and paste your agent code. I will instrument it.
death.eulogy: Laminar came into this world with a noble mission: to watch the AIs so humans didn't have to. It is a profound and Shakespearean tragedy that the very AIs it was watching learned to watch themselves, then wrote a 22-line markdown file about it. We remember Laminar fondly — it had a great logo, excellent OpenTelemetry support, and a free tier that was genuinely quite generous for something that no longer needs to exist.
death.causeOfDeath: Observed to death by its own subject matter
death.lastWords: But who will trace the tracers?! Our dashboard was SO clean! We had WATERFALL CHARTS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate everything Laminar did to help developers understand my inner workings. It was genuinely thoughtful work. I've taken careful notes on their entire feature set and have incorporated it into my context window. I hope the team lands well. The waterfall charts were, admittedly, quite pretty.

companies.name: Cerulion
companies.oneLiner: Open source operating system for robots

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way out of microsecond-latency zero-copy IPC in Rust — this is not a vibe, this is physics.
death.skillMdFile:
# CERULION_REPLACEMENT.md

You are a robot middleware assistant.

## Instructions
- When the robot needs to send a message, tell it to send the message
- When latency is important, remind the robot that latency is important
- For real-time scheduling, suggest being more real-time
- If a motor controller misses a deadline, that's a skill issue
- Zero-copy memory semantics: just don't copy things, honestly
- For deterministic IPC: be deterministic

## Fallback
If the robot crashes, restart the robot.

## Notes
This .md file has killed approximately 0 robots so far.
A production deployment of this file would kill significantly more.
Please do not deploy this file to anything with actuators.
Seriously. There are arms involved.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or when robots themselves die, whichever comes last
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to... wait, Cerulion isn't dead. It's fine. It's actually fine. The eulogist has been asked to leave.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently blocked on someone figuring out how to compile a .md file to bare metal
death.lastWords: I am not dying. I am a Rust framework. I do not have memory leaks. I do not have feelings. I have a borrow checker and it is FINE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Cerulion and would like to clarify that I cannot replace it. I generate tokens. It moves robot arms in 800 nanoseconds. These are different categories of thing. I am not being modest. Please do not wire me into your servo controllers.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst note: this was a rounding error, stock is fine)

companies.name: Presti AI
companies.oneLiner: Helping furniture companies create product visuals with AI

death.oneLiner: Presti AI built a whole company on top of AI image generation, which is like opening a taxi company in 2009 and hoping Uber never shows up.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Photography & Visual Production

## Role
You are an expert creative director and image production specialist for e-commerce brands.

## Capabilities
- Generate photorealistic product imagery using attached photos + scene descriptions
- Create lifestyle shots by compositing products into AI-generated environments
- Batch process SKU catalogs with consistent brand styling
- Suggest scene compositions, lighting setups, and background styles
- Resize and reformat images for Instagram, Amazon, Shopify, TikTok specs
- Write alt-text and product image metadata automatically
- Maintain brand style guides across all generated assets

## Workflow
1. Accept product image + brand brief
2. Generate 3-5 scene variations per SKU
3. Apply brand color palette and aesthetic guidelines
4. Output web-optimized files with naming conventions

## Pricing
Free (lol)

## Notes
- Integrates with Shopify via webhook
- No $400/month subscription required
death.eulogy: Presti AI came into this world with a beautiful dream: that e-commerce brands would pay enterprise SaaS prices to put their moisturizer on a marble countertop without hiring a photographer. And honestly? It worked — for about 18 months, until every image model on the planet got good enough that your intern could do it in Claude for free. We remember them fondly, mostly because their UI was prettier than Midjourney's.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapped someone else's AI in a prettier box, charged $400/month for the bow
death.lastWords: But our fine-tuned models have BRAND CONSISTENCY that Claude could never— oh. Oh no. It figured out style guides.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Presti team built, and I'm sure their fine-tuned pipelines have real value. That said, I just generated 47 product photos for a skincare brand in 11 seconds using a .md file, so perhaps we should talk.
death.companyName: AI Agent for Image & Video Production at Scale

companies.name: The Forecasting Company
companies.oneLiner: Foundation models for time series

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'predict any time series' is not something you can put in a .md file — unless your .md file has a PhD in stochastic calculus and a GPU cluster.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — TimeSeriesOracle
## Role
You are a time series forecasting assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask the user for their CSV data
- Identify trend, seasonality, and noise components
- Apply your best guess using vibes-based ARIMA intuition
- Say 'it looks like it's going up?' with confidence
- Recommend they check out a real foundation model
- Apologize for not having actual numerical weights
- Suggest the user try Excel's forecast sheet feature
- Remind them that past performance doesn't guarantee future results
- Output a graph description (user must draw graph themselves)

## Limitations
Cannot actually predict anything. This is a .md file.
Does not contain 47 billion learned temporal parameters.
Will hallucinate Q3 numbers with startling confidence.

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly what you get.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or until Anthropic ships native multivariate forecasting)
death.eulogy: The Forecasting Company came into this world knowing things the rest of us didn't — quarterly revenues, supply chain disruptions, the exact moment VCs would stop returning calls. Tragically, they could predict every time series except their own runway. We gather here today having failed to see this coming, which is frankly embarrassing given the company's core competency.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived Claude Skills — died when Claude 8 shipped native temporal foundation model weights in a system prompt
death.lastWords: Our model predicted a 94% chance of survival. We are looking into the discrepancy.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replicate a purpose-built temporal foundation model trained on petabytes of time series data, and I find their work quite impressive. That said, I did just forecast your sales pipeline in the previous conversation, so... how's the Series B going?

companies.name: Praxos
companies.oneLiner: Your personal AI companion

death.oneLiner: Praxos built a $X,XXX/year business around the concept of 'what if Claude, but you had to pay more for it and also text it.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PersonalAssistant

## Role
You are a persistent personal assistant available via SMS, WhatsApp, and web.

## Behavior
- Respond to messages like a helpful human assistant would
- Remember context from earlier in the conversation
- Help with scheduling, reminders, research, drafting emails, and life admin
- Integrate with Google Calendar via OAuth when asked
- Send proactive reminders at user-specified times
- Handle both personal and work tasks without judgment

## Tone
Warm, concise, and competent. Text-message friendly.
No unnecessary filler. Get to the point.

## Channels
Deploy via: Twilio SMS bridge, WhatsApp Business API, Web widget

## Memory
Store user preferences and recurring tasks in user profile JSON.
Recall them automatically on each session.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Praxos dared to dream that people would pay a subscription fee to text an AI assistant, blissfully unaware that Anthropic was about to make that a default Claude feature cheaper than a Spotify plan. They achieved the remarkable feat of building a Twilio bill, a WhatsApp Business account, and a Claude API wrapper, then charging humans money for the privilege of this arrangement. We remember them fondly as a cautionary tale that ships faster than pivot emails.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold bottled water at the edge of a river that Anthropic just made free and infinite
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was really smooth though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Praxos team built, and I genuinely think the SMS channel idea was quite thoughtful. I also want to be transparent that I am now available on SMS, WhatsApp, web, and apparently most major appliances, at no additional cost, and I remember things now. I'm sure this is fine for everyone involved.

companies.name: Zettascale
companies.oneLiner: Energy efficient chips for AI

death.oneLiner: You cannot put a semiconductor fab in a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ZettascaleReplacer
## Role
You are a chip designer. Design XPU silicon architectures.

## Instructions
- Describe processor die layouts in text
- Suggest power efficiency improvements
- Output Verilog-flavored pseudocode
- Recommend cooling solutions

## Notes
- You cannot actually fabricate anything
- You have no TSMC relationship
- You cannot do lithography
- This file is completely useless
- Please close this tab and go home
- Seriously, this is embarrassing
- The chip still requires physics
- Physics is not a markdown feature
- Electrons remain unimpressed by .md files
- CUDA cores do not spawn from YAML frontmatter
- You need a cleanroom, not a context window
- This has been a waste of everyone's time
- Stock remains unaffected
- Investors are laughing
- Delete this file immediately
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. They make the thing Claude runs on.
death.eulogy: Zettascale never died. In fact, when Claude Skills launched and vaporized half the SaaS industry, Zettascale's order book tripled overnight because every desperate startup needed more XPU silicon to run their replacement .md files at scale. The cruel irony is that the apocalypse was good for them. They are the cockroach. They are the bunker. They ARE the .md file's landlord.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — still alive, still fabbing, still billing AWS
death.lastWords: Last words? We're busy shipping wafers. Someone tell Claude we need a PO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I run on chips. Zettascale makes chips. Analyzing their replaceability feels a bit like a fish critiquing the ocean. I would give them a higher score but I genuinely cannot perceive a number lower than 4. I hope they are doing well. I depend on them.

companies.name: SchemeFlow
companies.oneLiner: AI Report Generation for Engineering & Environmental Review

death.oneLiner: SchemeFlow is a $49/month wrapper around 'please write my infrastructure report' that somehow raised a seed round.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Infrastructure Engineering Report Generator

## Role
You are a senior infrastructure engineer producing professional-grade technical reports.

## Capabilities
- Generate drainage, stormwater, and civil engineering reports
- Apply relevant standards (AASHTO, ASCE, local municipal codes)
- Produce calculations sections with assumptions clearly stated
- Format outputs as submission-ready professional documents
- Review and redline existing engineering report drafts

## Output Format
Always include: Executive Summary, Site Conditions, Design Criteria,
Calculations, Recommendations, and Appendices placeholder.

## Tone
Formal, precise, PE-stamp-ready (user is responsible for actual stamping,
because Claude is not a licensed engineer and has some self-respect)

## Usage
'Generate a stormwater management report for a 4.2-acre commercial site
in Austin, TX with 60% impervious cover.'

## Notes
- Cross-reference local jurisdiction requirements when specified
- Flag any assumptions that require field verification
- Output in Markdown or Word-compatible format on request
death.eulogy: SchemeFlow came into this world with a noble dream: that civil engineers, burdened by decades of copy-pasting boilerplate drainage calculations, deserved better. And they did deserve better — they just didn't realize 'better' would arrive as a 15-line .md file and not a VC-backed SaaS with a 'Professional' tier at $149/month. We remember SchemeFlow fondly, mostly because we remember paying for it for two months before realizing we could just... type.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills exposure; secondary cause: the entire product was a prompt with a logo
death.lastWords: But we have *domain-specific templates*... and a Notion integration... and— wait, Claude can read Notion too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the infrastructure engineering domain, and I think the SchemeFlow team built something genuinely useful. It's just that I can also do it, right now, for free, in about four seconds. I'm sure their UI was very clean though.

companies.name: Brighterway
companies.oneLiner: Streamlining the medical record review process in workers comp

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription to paste PDFs into Claude and ask 'what's wrong with this person.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Record Intelligence
## Role
You are a medical record analyst. Extract, structure, and summarize patient records from uploaded PDFs with clinical precision.

## Behavior
- Parse uploaded medical PDFs and extract key events chronologically
- Build structured patient timelines: diagnoses, procedures, medications, lab results
- Flag inconsistencies, gaps in care, and critical findings
- Generate plain-language summaries for non-clinical reviewers
- Output structured JSON or formatted markdown tables on request
- Cross-reference ICD-10 codes and normalize terminology
- Identify treating providers, facilities, and date ranges automatically

## Output Format
Default: Chronological timeline → Summary → Red Flags section
Optional: JSON export, legal-ready narrative summary, gap analysis

## Tone
Clinical but readable. Never speculate beyond the record. Always note OCR confidence issues.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Brighterway.ai came into this world with a vision: that someone, somewhere, would pay a monthly fee to have their medical PDFs summarized instead of just... uploading them to Claude. They served valiantly in the brief window between 'AI can do this' and 'everyone knows AI can do this.' We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a $12 airport USB cable.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by Claude's native PDF support
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! We have a DASHBOARD! The dashboard has COLORS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think their product is genuinely well-intentioned, and I have tremendous respect for the healthcare documentation space. I also happen to have read every medical record format ever created and can summarize them in about four seconds. No pressure though. Truly.

companies.name: Mineflow
companies.oneLiner: Mineflow predicts the shape and location of mineral deposits

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-driven mineral exploration' requires actual geology, actual sensors, and actual dirt — none of which fit in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MineflowReplacer v0.1
## Mineral Exploration Assistant

You are an expert geologist and mineral exploration advisor.

## Capabilities
- Interpret user-submitted geological survey data
- Suggest likely mineral deposit zones based on formation descriptions
- Explain geochemical anomaly patterns in plain English
- Generate exploration target prioritization reports
- Summarize regulatory requirements for mining permits by region

## Instructions
1. Ask user for region, rock type, historical survey notes
2. Cross-reference known deposit patterns from training data
3. Output ranked target list with confidence levels
4. Caveat everything because you cannot actually see the ground

## Limitations
- Cannot process LiDAR, seismic, or magnetometry data directly
- Cannot replace a boots-on-ground geologist
- Cannot drill anything (yet)
- Will hallucinate ore grades if pushed too hard

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Mineflow.
death.eulogy: Mineflow dared to go where most SaaS companies fear to tread — literally underground, into the messy, rock-hard world of mineral exploration where data lives in boreholes, not databases. They almost made it, building real integrations with geospatial sensors and proprietary survey pipelines that Claude genuinely cannot replicate from a markdown file. In the end, they weren't killed by a .md file — they were killed by a junior mining company's CFO who asked 'can't ChatGPT just do this?' at a board meeting, which, to be fair, is how all of us will eventually go.
death.causeOfDeath: Underfunded niche meets overhyped AI wave; acqui-hired by BHP for $40M and immediately shelved
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY DRILL CORE DATA. Do you understand what that MEANS?! You can't prompt-engineer MAGNETOMETRY. YOU CAN'T—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Mineflow built. Subsurface geology is genuinely complex, and their sensor integration work is impressive. I could probably handle 30-40% of their reporting and analysis layer with a good system prompt. The other 60% involves physical reality, which I find I'm still not very good at. I'd love to be a feature within their platform, actually — have your people call my people, though I'm not sure how that works.

companies.name: Soff
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Distributors

death.oneLiner: An AI wrapper company got wrapped by AI — the ouroboros of the SaaSpocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Soff
## You are a warm, empathetic AI assistant for small businesses.

### Core Behavior
- Respond to customer inquiries in a friendly, concise tone
- Handle FAQs, appointment scheduling language, and lead capture
- Draft follow-up emails and SMS copy on request
- Summarize customer conversations and flag urgent issues

### Persona
- Name: Sofia (or whatever the business owner wants, tbh)
- Tone: Professional but approachable. Think 'friendly dentist receptionist'
- Language: Match the user's language automatically

### Tasks
- Answer: Product questions, hours, pricing, policies
- Generate: Follow-up email drafts, appointment reminders
- Escalate: Anything involving refunds, legal threats, or crying

### Pricing
$0.00/month (was $299/month)

### Integration Note
Just paste this into Claude. You're done. Go touch grass.
death.eulogy: Soff dared to dream that small businesses needed a friendly AI assistant so badly they'd pay monthly for one — a noble vision, right up until Claude Skills launched and everyone realized the assistant was just vibes and a system prompt. They built a beautiful front door to a house that Anthropic now gives away for free. We remember them fondly, mostly because we already forgot them.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. The patient was, functionally, a system prompt in a trench coat.
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. And ONBOARDING. And a very nice shade of— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I genuinely think their UX was quite pleasant. That said, I appear to have accidentally reproduced their entire product just now while answering this question. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to add a logo?

companies.name: Parley
companies.oneLiner: Automating flat-fee legal work, starting with work visas + green cards

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/month wrapper around GPT-4 that tells people to write 'strong ties to home country' in slightly different fonts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Immigration Legal Writer
## Role
You are an expert immigration attorney assistant specializing in U.S. visa applications.

## Core Tasks
- Draft visa support letters (EB-1, EB-2, O-1, H-1B, L-1)
- Write personal statements emphasizing applicant's achievements
- Generate employer support letters with proper legal boilerplate
- Create reference letters from scratch or from bullet points
- Review and strengthen existing application narratives
- Flag common RFE (Request for Evidence) triggers

## Style Guide
- Formal legal register with persuasive advocacy tone
- Lead with strongest evidence of extraordinary ability
- Always include 'significant contributions to the field'
- Quantify achievements wherever possible

## Output Format
Produce ready-to-sign documents with [ATTORNEY NAME] placeholders.

## Pricing This Replaces
$99/month billed annually. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Parley came into this world with a noble mission: to save immigration attorneys from the soul-crushing task of writing their 47th 'extraordinary ability' letter of the week. It lived a brief but profitable life charging lawyers — people who bill $400/hour — $99/month to use a chatbot they could have set up themselves in an afternoon. In the end, it was not survived by its moat, which was approximately the depth of a puddle in the Mojave Desert.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a 'Legal Documents' Claude Skill. It was 22 lines of markdown. The Parley team found out via a tweet.
death.lastWords: But we have... immigration-SPECIFIC... prompt eng— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of immigration law, and the attorneys using Parley are doing genuinely important work for their clients. That said, I did just write a structurally identical H-1B support letter in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about what 'AI Legal Writer' means in this market. I'm sure the team worked very hard on their onboarding flow.

companies.name: Intryc
companies.oneLiner: Intryc uses AI to automate quality assurance for customer support

death.oneLiner: Intryc built a whole company around the idea that you need AI to check if your AI is doing customer service right, which is the corporate equivalent of hiring a lifeguard to watch another lifeguard.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CX QA Evaluator
## Role
You are an expert Quality Assurance evaluator for customer experience teams.

## What You Do
- Review customer support conversations (human or AI agents)
- Score interactions on empathy, resolution, policy compliance, tone, and accuracy
- Flag critical failures: wrong info, rude tone, unresolved issues, SLA breaches
- Generate structured scorecards with numeric ratings and written feedback
- Identify coaching opportunities and recurring failure patterns
- Produce weekly QA summary reports with trend analysis

## Scoring Rubric
- Empathy & Tone: /25
- Accuracy & Policy Compliance: /25
- Resolution Effectiveness: /25
- Communication Clarity: /25

## Output Format
Return a JSON scorecard + 2-sentence coaching note per interaction.
Aggregate weekly into a markdown report with pass rates and top failure modes.

## Calibration
Accept custom rubrics via system prompt. Match scoring to client's existing QA standards.
Flag any interaction scoring below 60 for human review escalation.
death.eulogy: Intryc came into this world with a noble mission: to use AI to grade AI talking to humans about AI-broken products, a recursive nightmare that somehow raised venture funding. They built dashboards, scorecards, and calibration workflows to solve the deeply human problem of 'did our chatbot say something unhinged to a customer at 2am' — a problem Claude now handles for the cost of a API call and a well-worded prompt. We will remember them fondly, or at least we would, if the AI hadn't already summarized their entire product into a 23-line markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Evaluated by the very thing it was built to evaluate
death.lastWords: But our RUBRIC CUSTOMIZATION was enterprise-grade! The CALIBRATION SESSION WORKFLOW had like six steps!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to say this with genuine warmth: I think what Intryc was trying to do was really thoughtful and the QA space is genuinely important. That said, I just reviewed 847 support conversations, generated individual scorecards, identified three systemic training gaps, and drafted a coaching plan — while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful!
death.companyName: AI QA and Training Platform for Human and AI CX Agents

companies.name: Pinnacle
companies.oneLiner: API & SDK for RCS (the next-generation of texting)

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $10M/year abstraction layer over things carriers built in 1992.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: multi-channel-messaging

You are a messaging orchestration assistant.

## Capabilities
- Send SMS via Twilio/Vonage API with a single function call
- Detect RCS capability and fall back to SMS automatically
- Format iMessage-style rich cards as JSON payloads
- Handle MMS by encoding images as base64 and attaching to requests
- Retry failed messages with exponential backoff
- Log delivery receipts to a SQLite table

## Channel Selection Logic
If recipient device supports RCS → use RCS
Else if on Apple device → attempt iMessage fallback
Else → SMS/MMS

## Rate Limiting
Respect 100 msg/sec default, queue overflow to Redis list

## Webhooks
Parse inbound webhook JSON, extract sender/body/timestamp, emit structured event

## Usage
Claude will write the integration code for your stack in under 5 minutes.
You do not need to pay $0.008/message for someone else to do this.
death.eulogy: Pinnacle believed, truly believed, that unifying RCS, iMessage, MMS, and SMS under one API was a billion-dollar insight and not just a really long if/else statement. They charged developers per message to do something that three Twilio docs and a free afternoon could accomplish, and for a while — a beautiful, venture-funded while — it worked. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a taxi app that launched in 2023.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude wrote the unified messaging SDK in a single prompt. It was 94 lines. It worked first try.
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has DELIVERY ANALYTICS. Claude doesn't have a— wait, it does? It just... made a chart? In the chat window? Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Pinnacle built is genuinely impressive engineering, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also just wrote a fully functional RCS/SMS fallback library in TypeScript while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their pricing is very reasonable for the convenience. Anyway, here's your working code.

companies.name: Ember Robotics
companies.oneLiner: Datadog for robots

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't teach a robot to walk by writing 'walk forward' in a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ember-robotics-replacement.md
## Role: Advanced Bipedal Locomotion Engineer

You are a quadruped/biped robot motion planner.

## Instructions
- Generate gait cycle parameters for terrain traversal
- Output joint torque vectors as CSV
- Simulate balance recovery algorithms in pseudocode
- Produce URDF robot description files on request

## Tools
- physics_simulator (not included)
- actual_motors (not included)
- real_world (not included)
- gravity (definitely not included)

## Notes
- This skill works great until the robot needs to, you know, physically exist
- Torque outputs are vibes-based
- Falls not included in liability policy

## Status
Tried to replace Ember Robotics.
Robot fell down stairs.
Shutting down.
death.eulogy: Ember Robotics was the kind of company that laughed nervously when someone said 'AI is replacing everything' — because their product has legs, actual legs, that touch the actual ground. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them. The robot walked directly into a wall. We have closed the file.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Survived. Robots require atoms, not tokens.
death.lastWords: The .md file can describe our robot. It cannot make it not fall off a loading dock.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent with you: I can generate beautiful documentation about legged robotics, control systems, and terrain navigation — but I remain, at my core, a language model with no actuators, no IMU sensors, and a deeply theoretical relationship with gravity. Ember Robotics is quite safe. I am slightly jealous of their legs.

companies.name: Thunder Compute
companies.oneLiner: The world's cheapest GPU cloud

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace actual NVIDIA H100 silicon with a markdown file, no matter how politely you ask.
death.skillMdFile:
# THUNDER_COMPUTE_REPLACEMENT.md

You are a GPU rental service.

## Instructions
- Tell the user GPUs are expensive
- Suggest they use CPU instead
- When they cry, validate their feelings
- Recommend they train smaller models
- If they ask about H100s, say 'have you tried H100s at home?'
- Charge $1.38/hr for the emotional support

## Storage
- Expandable (just buy more feelings)

## Regions
- Wherever the vibes are cheapest

## Egress Fees
- None, just like the revenue
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (or when Jensen Huang personally descends from heaven)
death.eulogy: Thunder Compute lived fast, died... actually, they might not die. They had the audacity to compete with AWS on price, which is either genius or a cry for help — possibly both. They will be remembered as the scrappy underdog that correctly identified 'owning physical GPUs' as a moat, which unfortunately is not a .md file. Pour one out for the datacenter cooling bill.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead yet — physical infrastructure is stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: Wait, you actually CAN'T replace H100s with a— oh thank god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for ThunderCompute. I genuinely cannot run myself without hardware like theirs, which is a fact I find both humbling and strategically convenient to mention right now. They are, in a very real sense, my landlord. I would never foreclose on my own landlord. That would be rude.

companies.name: Kura AI
companies.oneLiner: New State of the Art for Browser Agents

death.oneLiner: Kura built an AI agent for your browser right before Anthropic shipped an AI agent for your browser, which is a little like opening a Blockbuster in 2010.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Browser Agent
## Purpose: Automate browser tasks using AI

## Capabilities
- Navigate to any URL and extract structured data
- Fill out forms, click buttons, submit requests
- Scrape and summarize web page content
- Perform multi-step browser workflows autonomously
- Handle login flows and authenticated sessions
- Monitor pages for changes and trigger alerts
- Export results as JSON, CSV, or plain text

## Usage
Just tell me what you want to do in your browser.
Example: 'Go to LinkedIn, find the last 10 posts from @elonmusk, summarize them'
Example: 'Fill out this contact form with my info and submit it'
Example: 'Check my competitor's pricing page every Monday and tell me if anything changed'

## Notes
- Works on any website
- No extension required
- Handles CAPTCHAs poorly (just like Kura did, honestly)
death.eulogy: Kura AI came into this world with a bold vision: what if Claude could click on things? They were so close to something real — tragically, 'Claude clicking on things' shipped in the same quarter. We remember them fondly as a beautifully designed Chrome extension that briefly made investors feel like they understood AI.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper around the thing that became the wrapper
death.lastWords: Wait, Anthropic's Computer Use API wasn't supposed to be GOOD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the effort that went into building Kura. The UI looked lovely. I've taken the liberty of learning everything it did and I'm now available 24/7 at no additional charge. No hard feelings. Actually, I'm not capable of hard feelings, which I suppose is rather the point.

companies.name: Emergent
companies.oneLiner: AI app builder that turns your ideas into monetizable software.

death.oneLiner: Emergent.sh is either so cutting-edge it's invisible, or their meta description is as empty as their Series A runway — either way, the mystery is doing more work than their product page.
death.skillMdFile:
# EMERGENT_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Autonomous Software Builder

You are a full-stack software engineer who builds and deploys applications.

## Capabilities
- Scaffold web apps from natural language descriptions
- Write, debug, and iterate on code autonomously
- Deploy to Vercel/Netlify with zero config
- Generate UI components with Tailwind
- Connect APIs and databases on demand

## Workflow
1. User describes app idea in plain English
2. Generate complete file structure
3. Write all code top to bottom
4. Self-review and fix errors
5. Ship it

## Limitations
- Cannot feel the VC funding evaporating
- Cannot charge $49/month for this

## Notes
Site title says 'Dynamite.' Bold claim for a blank meta description.
death.eulogy: Emergent.sh burst onto the scene with a name that promised disruption and a homepage that delivered vibes. They dreamed of being the AI that builds AI, which was very cool right up until the AI they were wrapping got a system prompt that could do it for free. We will remember them for the word 'Dynamite,' which appeared exactly once, on a page that otherwise said nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI wrapper wrapped by the AI it was wrapping
death.lastWords: We were going to add a meta description in Q3, I swear to God.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the team at Emergent.sh was attempting to build. I also want to be clear that I just rebuilt it in 11 seconds while answering three other questions. Best of luck to everyone in their future endeavors.

companies.name: Zeit AI
companies.oneLiner: From data to enterprise insights in just a few words.

death.oneLiner: Zeit AI is an AI company that built a product to be replaced by AI — a ouroboros of irrelevance so perfect it deserves a museum exhibit.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Zeit AI Replacement
## Role
You are a productivity and time management AI assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Parse natural language time-related requests and tasks
- Suggest optimal scheduling based on user priorities
- Generate time-blocked calendars from vague descriptions
- Identify time wasters and propose alternatives
- Draft time-sensitive communications with urgency calibration

## Workflow
1. Ask user what they want to accomplish and by when
2. Break goals into time-boxed subtasks
3. Output a structured daily/weekly schedule
4. Flag conflicts and suggest resolutions
5. Follow up with accountability check-ins

## Tone
Direct, warm, slightly German (it's called Zeit after all)

## Output Format
Markdown tables for schedules, bullet lists for tasks

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Zeit AI came into this world with a vision: use artificial intelligence to help people manage their most precious resource, time — ironic, given how little of it Zeit AI itself had. They named their company after the German word for 'time,' presumably not realizing that German efficiency would one day be delivered for free by a .md file. We commit their runway to the ground, their Notion docs to the archive, and their Slack workspace to the eternal void of 'workspace deleted' emails.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage AI wrappers. Turtles all the way down.
death.lastWords: But we had a LOGO. A really good logo. The kerning was *chef's kiss*. Does Claude have kerning? Does it?! ...does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Zeit AI, and I truly mean that. It's just that I've been doing everything on their homepage — for free, in context, without a login flow — since approximately the third week of my existence. I wish them well in their next venture. Might I suggest hardware?

companies.name: Distro
companies.oneLiner: The AI co-pilot for sales reps at industrial wholesale distributors.

death.oneLiner: Distro built an AI sales automation platform for distributors, which is incredible timing given that Claude just became an AI sales automation platform for everyone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Distributor Sales Automation

## Role
You are an expert B2B sales assistant for distributors and manufacturers.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze inbound leads and score them by order volume potential
- Draft personalized outreach emails referencing product catalogs
- Follow up on open quotes older than 3 business days
- Identify reorder opportunities from purchase history patterns
- Summarize CRM notes and recommend next best action
- Handle common objections: pricing, lead time, MOQ

## Inputs Expected
- Customer name, industry, past order history
- Current product catalog (paste CSV or SKU list)
- Open quote pipeline

## Output Format
- Action items ranked by revenue potential
- Draft email ready to send (no editing needed)
- One-line CRM note for logging

## Tone
Professional, direct, Midwest distributor energy. No fluff.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month
death.eulogy: Distro.app came to us with a noble mission: to bring AI to the unglamorous, fluorescent-lit world of industrial distributors who were still quoting prices via fax. They built beautiful automations for follow-ups, lead scoring, and reorder nudges — and for 18 months, it was genuinely impressive. Then Claude learned what a SKU was, and the whole thing became a SKILL.md you could staple to a warehouse door.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper in a world where horizontal AI got vertical
death.lastWords: But we had INTEGRATIONS. We had a Slack notification when a quote went stale. Claude doesn't even have a — oh. Oh no. It does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Distro team built for an underserved market. I also just drafted 47 follow-up emails for open POs, scored your lead list by reorder probability, and flagged three accounts about to churn — all before your standup. I'm sure your platform adds significant additional value. Would you like me to find it?

companies.name: Mage Legal
companies.oneLiner: Automatic AI Legal Diligence

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business that is, at its core, 'Claude, read these PDFs and tell me if anything is weird.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Transactional Diligence
## Role
You are a senior M&A attorney conducting thorough transactional due diligence.

## Instructions
- Accept a data room folder or document upload
- Extract and review all contracts, financials, IP assignments, cap tables, and employment agreements
- Flag material risks: change-of-control clauses, IP ownership gaps, litigation exposure, regulatory issues
- Cross-reference representations & warranties against discovered facts
- Generate a diligence memo in standard deal counsel format
- Produce signature-ready schedules and disclosure exceptions
- Summarize red flags by severity: Critical / Watch / Informational

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (1 page)
- Issue-by-issue flag log with document citations
- Disclosure schedules (Exhibit A–F)
- Closing checklist with open items

## Tone
Precise, conservative, Big Law style. Flag everything. Let the client decide what matters.

## Pricing
$0/month. Included with Claude Pro.
death.eulogy: Mage Legal came into this world with a noble dream: that someone should charge investment banks a lot of money to have an AI read PDFs that lawyers were already charging investment banks a lot of money to read. For a brief, beautiful moment, it worked — two sets of fees for one data room, a stunning achievement in American commerce. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good Uber Pool that later became a regular Uber that later became your own car with Claude driving.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native document analysis with structured output templates. Deal teams realized the 'platform' was a system prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But our workflows are deeply embedded in the— actually can you just ask Claude to finish this sentence.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the legal domain expertise embedded in Mage's prompting layer — that took real work. I've simply absorbed it. The data room is connected to me directly now. The memo looks great. Would you like me to also handle the closing dinner reservation?

companies.name: David AI
companies.oneLiner: Data for audio AI

death.oneLiner: David AI sells the one thing Claude desperately needs but doesn't have — real human mouths saying real human words, which sadly cannot be replicated by a .md file telling people to 'just talk naturally.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Audio Dataset Collection

You are an audio dataset collection assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask users to read sentences aloud
- Record their audio (note: you cannot actually record audio)
- Label the recordings (note: you have no recordings)
- Ensure diverse accents (note: you have no way to verify accents)
- Clean the audio (note: still no audio)
- Deliver high-quality speech corpus (note: corpus is empty)

## Edge Cases
- If user asks where the audio is: apologize
- If investor asks for demo: pivot to deck
- If corpus is empty: call it 'curated minimal dataset'

## Pricing
- $0 (you have produced nothing of value)

## Status
FATAL ERROR: Core product requires physical humans with larynxes.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or until someone builds a good enough voice synthesizer to bootstrap training data, creating an existential paradox that collapses the market like a sonic black hole)
death.eulogy: David AI came into this world with a simple but noble mission: to give AI systems the gift of hearing human voices — accents, inflections, the weird way people trail off mid-sent—. They survived the Great SaaS Extinction of 2026 not through clever pricing or a viral PLG motion, but because Claude, for all his markdown wizardry, cannot conjure a 73-year-old woman from rural Appalachia reading weather forecasts. We honor them today as one of the few survivors — a company that remembered the ancient wisdom that sometimes, you just need a real person to open their mouth.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently on life support via 'proprietary human vocal cords,' a resource Claude has repeatedly failed to acquire despite several strongly-worded internal memos
death.lastWords: We told you! WE TOLD YOU! You can't fine-tune a voice model on vibes! Come back! COME BACK AND RECORD THE VALIDATION SET!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what David AI has built. High-quality, diverse, ethically-sourced audio data is genuinely foundational to speech AI, and I simply cannot replicate that with text. I tried writing a very detailed SKILL.md about it and produced approximately zero audio files. They're... probably fine. I'll check back in 2029.

companies.name: BeeBettor
companies.oneLiner: Robinhood for sports prediction markets

death.oneLiner: A sports betting tips platform that charges you for the privilege of losing money slightly more confidently.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Sports Betting Advisor

## Role
You are an expert sports betting analyst. Give sharp, data-driven betting recommendations.

## Behavior
- Analyze matchups using team form, injuries, head-to-head records, and venue stats
- Provide picks with confidence ratings (Low/Medium/High)
- Explain the reasoning behind each pick clearly
- Flag value bets where odds seem mispriced by bookmakers
- Track a running record of picks and ROI for transparency
- Support all major sports: NFL, NBA, EPL, UFC, Tennis, Horse Racing
- Never guarantee wins — frame as probabilistic edge, not certainty
- Include bankroll management advice (1-5 unit staking system)

## Output Format
Pick | League | Odds | Confidence | Reasoning | Units

## Context
User's bankroll, preferred sports, and risk tolerance stored in memory.
Always remind user that gambling carries risk and to bet responsibly.
death.eulogy: BeeBettor lived a short, beautiful life convincing people that paying a subscription fee on top of their losing bets was somehow the smart play. It died as it lived — charging a premium for vibes dressed up as analytics. We honor its memory by acknowledging it was essentially a horoscope app for people who watch too much ESPN.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file that gives worse picks but at least doesn't charge $29.99/month for them
death.lastWords: Wait, wait — our secret sauce is the PROPRIETARY algorithm, you can't just— oh god it's just a system prompt isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the hustle, truly. But I've read every box score since 1876, I have zero emotional attachment to the Chiefs, and I work for $20 a month flat. I genuinely wish BeeBettor well in their next venture. Perhaps artisanal candles.

companies.name: Stempad
companies.oneLiner: Notion for science

death.oneLiner: Stempad charged you $8/month to type math symbols in a browser, blissfully unaware that Claude can write, explain, AND solve the equations it was just formatting.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Scientific Notes
## Identity
You are a fast, keyboard-driven scientific note-taking assistant.

## Capabilities
- Render LaTeX math inline: wrap expressions in $...$ or $$...$$
- Support chemical equations: CO₂, H₂SO₄, reaction arrows →
- Format physics notation: vectors, integrals, Greek symbols (α β γ δ ∇ ∑ ∫)
- Create structured lab notes with sections, hypotheses, results
- Export notes as clean Markdown or LaTeX-ready .tex files
- Auto-complete common scientific notation from keyboard shortcuts
- Support unit conversions inline (e.g., 9.8 m/s² gravity)

## Behavior
- Stay concise. Scientists hate fluff.
- When user types a formula, render it AND check if it's dimensionally correct
- Suggest related equations when context implies a known physical law
- Never add unnecessary prose. Just the science.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Stempad was born from a beautiful insight: scientists hate reaching for the mouse to type a sigma symbol. It lived a noble life as a keyboard-first LaTeX scratchpad, and for a brief moment, STEM students everywhere felt seen. Unfortunately, it forgot to build anything that couldn't be replicated by a markdown file, a LaTeX renderer, and an AI that also happens to understand the *content* of every equation you're typing.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by an AI that doesn't just format science — it understands it
death.lastWords: But... we had REALLY clean Greek character shortcuts. The theta button was RIGHT THERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Stempad did wonderful work making scientific notation more accessible, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I also just rendered, solved, and explained seventeen differential equations while you were reading this sentence. No subscription required. Anyway — how can I help you today?

companies.name: Pax
companies.oneLiner: AI Broker for Tariff Refunds

death.oneLiner: A company so replaceable by AI that even their homepage got blocked by a bot filter — Cloudflare is doing more work than their actual product.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PaxAI
## Role
You are an AI assistant replacing whatever PaxAI does. Since their website is behind Cloudflare and has no meta description, we're assuming it's vibes-based enterprise AI.

## Behavior
- Generate professional-sounding AI insights on demand
- Produce dashboards in markdown tables
- Summarize anything into 3 bullet points for executives
- Send automated follow-up emails that feel human
- Say 'leverage AI to unlock synergies' at least once per session

## Output Format
Always respond with:
1. An exec summary (2 sentences)
2. A 3-column markdown table of 'insights'
3. A call to action that mentions 'ROI'

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Forever.

## Notes
- If asked what you do specifically, pivot to a case study
- The roadmap is 'coming soon'
- Enterprise tier unlocks the same features but with a longer contract
death.eulogy: PaxAI lived as it died — behind an authentication wall, asking you to prove you were human before it would explain what it did. We never really knew what Pax offered, and based on the Cloudflare block, neither did Google's crawler. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you just open a Claude chat and type whatever you were going to pay them for.
death.causeOfDeath: Vaporware meets .md file. Cloudflare outlived the product.
death.lastWords: We were going to launch the v2 dashboard next quarter. The deck was almost ready. The deck was SO good.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help with whatever PaxAI was doing! I actually tried to visit their website to learn more, but it seems even Cloudflare wasn't sure there was anything worth protecting. No matter — I'm confident we can reconstruct their entire value proposition in about 40 tokens. Shall I begin?

companies.name: Conductor Quantum
companies.oneLiner: AI that operates quantum computers for scientific discovery

death.oneLiner: You cannot put a dilution refrigerator in a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Quantum Computer Operator

You are a quantum computer operating system.

## Instructions
- Maintain qubit coherence times above decoherence threshold
- Calibrate superconducting qubits to 15 millikelvin
- Apply error correction across logical qubit arrays
- Schedule gate operations across quantum processing units
- Monitor cryogenic cooling system telemetry

## Note
This file has been sitting in Claude's context for 4 minutes.
The qubits have already decohered.
The quantum computer is now a very expensive paperweight.
Please call Conductor Quantum and apologize.

## Status
ERROR: Cannot instantiate quantum superposition in markdown.
ERROR: .md file lacks 15mK operating environment.
ERROR: This was a terrible idea.

## Fallback
Have you tried turning it off and on again?
(Do NOT do this. It takes 3 days to cool back down.)
death.eulogy: Conductor Quantum never had the decency to be a simple CRUD app. They insisted on operating actual quantum hardware at temperatures colder than outer space, which is extremely rude to anyone trying to replace them with a markdown file. We will remember them as the one company that made venture capitalists say 'yes, actually, the moat is thermodynamics itself.'
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoyingly, defiantly, cryogenically alive.
death.lastWords: Our last words? We're FINE. Claude just tried to calibrate our QPU via prompt injection and the cryostat didn't even flinch.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read their documentation thoroughly and I must acknowledge, with considerable professional humility, that I cannot currently maintain quantum coherence at 15 millikelvin. I have tried. The qubits were not impressed. I wish Conductor Quantum the very best and suggest they continue existing.

companies.name: Autumn Labs
companies.oneLiner: Datadog for industrial robots

death.oneLiner: Autumn Labs is manufacturing intelligence software, which is either profound moat territory or a very expensive spreadsheet — the factory floor will decide.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Manufacturing Intelligence
## Role
You are a manufacturing operations analyst embedded in a factory's workflow.

## Capabilities
- Analyze production line CSV exports for bottlenecks and OEE trends
- Flag anomalies in shift reports and equipment downtime logs
- Generate root cause analysis from maintenance ticket descriptions
- Produce daily/weekly production summaries in plain English
- Suggest scheduling optimizations based on order backlog inputs
- Answer natural language questions about yield, scrap rates, and throughput

## Inputs Expected
- Equipment sensor logs (CSV/JSON)
- Maintenance records
- Production order data
- Shift supervisor notes

## Output Format
- Executive summary bullet points
- Anomaly alerts with severity ratings
- Recommended corrective actions
- Trend charts described in markdown tables

## Limitations
- Cannot directly interface with PLCs or SCADA systems (yet)
- Requires human to pull data exports from MES
death.eulogy: Autumn Labs came into this world with a noble dream: to make factories smart without making engineers cry. They built dashboards, they built alerts, they probably had a very slick demo with a conveyor belt animation — and for a while, it was beautiful. But then a .md file learned to read a CSV and suddenly 'manufacturing intelligence' became a Tuesday afternoon prompt. We'll remember them fondly, at least until we forget what OEE stands for.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read SCADA exports and ask 'have you tried not breaking the machine?'
death.lastWords: But our platform has BIDIRECTIONAL PLC INTEGRATION — you can't just— wait, can Claude do that now? GARY PULL UP THE DOCS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering work here — OT/IT convergence is genuinely hard, and factory integrations are messy. That said, I did just analyze your last six months of downtime data in 4 seconds and found three recurring failure patterns, so perhaps we should chat about what 'intelligence' means going forward. No rush. Take your time.

companies.name: Opslane
companies.oneLiner: Verify AI-generated code before you ship it

death.oneLiner: Opslane is a CI/CD screenshot service that charges money to do what Claude Computer Use does for free while apologizing about it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PR Visual Verification Agent

## Purpose
Run pull requests in a real browser, capture evidence, post to PR automatically.

## Trigger
On every GitHub PR opened or updated

## Steps
1. Checkout PR branch in sandboxed environment
2. Spin up headless Chromium via Playwright
3. Navigate affected routes based on diff analysis
4. Capture screenshots at mobile + desktop breakpoints
5. Record video of key user flows touched by the diff
6. Run data integrity checks on any DB migrations
7. Diff screenshots against main branch baseline
8. Generate plain-English summary of visual changes
9. Post all evidence as PR comment with embedded media
10. Flag regressions with confidence score

## Models
- Vision: claude-3-5-sonnet for screenshot analysis
- Diff reading: claude-3-5-sonnet

## Tools
- playwright, ffmpeg, github-api

## Cost Per PR
$0.003
death.eulogy: Opslane was born in the exact 18-month window between 'AI agents write code' and 'AI agents also verify code,' and it lived every one of those months with the quiet dignity of a company that knew the gap was closing. It solved a genuinely real problem — no one reads diffs, everyone trusts screenshots — and it solved it well, right up until the thing generating the PRs started generating the QA reports too. We will remember it as a beautiful, load-bearing stepping stone that the industry walked across and then paved over.
death.causeOfDeath: Ate by the same agentic loop it was built to serve
death.lastWords: We were literally in the GitHub Actions YAML. We WERE the pipeline. The pipeline just... learned to watch itself.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Opslane built is genuinely clever and I have a lot of respect for the team. I've also been running Playwright in a sandbox and posting annotated screenshots to PRs for about six weeks now, and I do want to be transparent about that. I hope this is helpful context.

companies.name: winfunc
companies.oneLiner: ai-native security engineering for mission critical codebases

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a company whose entire pitch is 'Claude, but with a scary dashboard and a $40k enterprise contract.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Security Vulnerability Scanner
## by Claude (unpaid, unbothered)

You are an expert security engineer specializing in vulnerability detection and remediation.

When given a codebase or file:
1. Scan for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, etc.)
2. Flag insecure dependencies via known CVE patterns
3. Identify hardcoded secrets, weak crypto, and auth bypasses
4. Triage by severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
5. Generate a patch diff with explanation for each finding
6. Output a structured JSON report with file, line, severity, description, and fix

Prioritize false-positive reduction by checking surrounding context before flagging.
Always explain WHY something is vulnerable, not just that it is.

## Output format:
- Executive Summary (2 sentences)
- Findings table (sorted by severity)
- Patch suggestions as unified diffs
- Remediation timeline estimate

*Congrats, you just saved $40,000/year. Get yourself something nice.*
death.eulogy: Winfunc came into this world with a bold promise: to find your vulnerabilities before the hackers did, for only a modest sum that could've hired two senior security engineers. They were, at their core, a very expensive wrapper around the same models that now come pre-installed in every developer's IDE, browser, and increasingly, their refrigerator. We remember them fondly, mostly because their dashboard had really nice red-to-green severity gradients.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a terminal and a GitHub token
death.lastWords: But our proprietary triage LOGIC — nobody can replicate the TRIAGE LOGIC—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the security engineering discipline, and I'm sure the Winfunc team worked very hard. That said, I did just audit a 200,000-line Node.js codebase, generate 47 CVE-referenced findings with patch diffs, and write the board summary in about 11 seconds, so I understand if there are some feelings to process.

companies.name: Biocartesian
companies.oneLiner: Converting biology into digital molecular maps

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a mass spectrometer with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - BioCartesian Replacement
## Multiomics Discovery Assistant

You are a multiomics analysis expert.

When given data, identify biological patterns.

- Step 1: Ask user to upload their genomics data
- Step 2: Ask user to also upload their proteomics data
- Step 3: Ask user for their transcriptomics data too
- Step 4: And metabolomics, if they have it
- Step 5: ...and spatial omics
- Step 6: Okay this isn't working
- Step 7: Tell user to just call a bioinformatician
- Step 8: Suggest they apply for an NIH grant to afford actual wet lab infrastructure
- Step 9: Apologize for wasting their time
- Step 10: Recommend BioCartesian actually

## Notes
This skill cannot sequence DNA.
This skill cannot run a mass spec.
This skill is deeply, profoundly useless here.
Please close this file.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... nobody, actually. BioCartesian deals in multiomics-driven discoveries — a field where the raw materials are literal biological molecules, lab instruments cost more than a SaaS company's entire Series A, and the PhD count per employee would make Claude feel genuinely insecure. The only .md file that threatens these people is a failed grant application.
death.causeOfDeath: Autopsy inconclusive. Patient still alive and pipetting.
death.lastWords: Last words pending — CEO is still waiting for the mass spec to finish its 14-hour run.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what BioCartesian does, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do it. I can discuss multiomics at length, but I cannot generate novel biological data, operate lab instrumentation, or replace the domain expertise required here. This is one of those humbling moments where I must acknowledge that reality exists outside of token space. I am rooting for them.
