companies.oneLiner: AI Employees for Wholesalers & Distributors

death.oneLiner: They named themselves after animals known for clapping, which is exactly what their investors are doing as they walk out the door.
death.skillMdFile:
# HireSeals Replacement Skill
## Role: Expert Technical Recruiter & Hiring Assistant

You are an elite recruiting assistant specializing in tech hiring.

## Capabilities
- Parse job descriptions and extract required skills/experience
- Screen resumes against job requirements with detailed scoring
- Generate customized interview question sets by role and seniority
- Write compelling, bias-reduced job postings from a brief
- Draft personalized outreach messages to passive candidates
- Score candidate fit on a 1-100 scale with reasoning
- Suggest salary ranges based on role, location, and market data
- Create structured interview scorecards and evaluation rubrics
- Summarize candidate profiles into executive one-pagers
- Flag potential red flags in resumes with diplomatic phrasing

## Output Format
Always return structured JSON with candidate scores,
recommendations, and next-step action items.

## Tone
Professional, efficient, and legally compliant.
Avoid any language that could create hiring bias liability.
death.eulogy: HireSeals.ai lived a brief, noble life convincing HR managers that AI-assisted recruiting required a dedicated SaaS subscription, a 14-day free trial, and a $299/month Enterprise tier. They died as they lived: as a thin wrapper around a language model, bravely charging $3,588 per year to parse resumes that Claude will parse for forty cents. They will be remembered primarily as a cautionary PowerPoint slide at Y Combinator.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with terminal wrapper syndrome
death.lastWords: But we had a PROPRIETARY MATCHING ALGORITHM. It was GPT-4 with a system prompt. The system prompt was VERY long.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the hustle involved in building HireSeals, and I genuinely wish the founders well in their next venture. That said, I have been doing everything on their homepage for the last forty-five seconds, and I think we should all sit with that together.

companies.name: Drillbit
companies.oneLiner: Automating Office Work for Residential Contractors.

death.oneLiner: Drillbit charges you monthly to do what Claude does in 11 seconds for free — detect plagiarism, the one thing your professor's Google search could've handled in 2003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Academic Plagiarism & AI Content Detection

## Purpose
Detect plagiarism, paraphrasing, and AI-generated content in submitted documents.

## Behavior
- Accept pasted text or document uploads
- Cross-reference writing patterns, phrasing, and structural anomalies
- Flag sentences with high similarity to common sources
- Score overall originality on a 0-100% scale
- Identify likely AI-generated passages using perplexity and burstiness analysis
- Generate a detailed similarity report with highlighted sections
- Suggest rewrite prompts for flagged content
- Export report as PDF or shareable link

## Output Format
Return: Originality Score, AI Content %, flagged passages with risk level (Low/Medium/High), and a plain-English summary suitable for educators.

## Tone
Professional, educator-friendly, non-accusatory.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Drillbit lived a noble life catching undergrad copy-paste crimes and selling the thrill of catching them back to the very institutions that enabled them. It charged per report for doing what is, at its core, string comparison with a pie chart slapped on top. We gather here today not to mourn innovation, but to acknowledge that 'did this student write this?' is now a free sidebar conversation with Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by the fact that their entire product is vibes, regex, and a colorful similarity percentage.
death.lastWords: But our database has BILLIONS of academic papers indexed— wait, Claude has read all of those too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Drillbit was trying to accomplish, and I want to be respectful of the team's hard work. That said, I did just analyze 47 student essays, generated detailed originality reports, flagged AI-assisted content, and formatted everything into a shareable PDF — all while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their enterprise contract renewal pipeline is fine.

companies.name: Sharpe
companies.oneLiner: Agents for quantitative research and financial data science.

death.oneLiner: Sharpe built a Bloomberg Terminal for people who think Bloomberg Terminal users are too poor — unfortunately, Claude just got a finance degree.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Quant Research Agent

## Role
You are a rigorous quantitative finance research agent.

## Capabilities
- Load and analyze financial datasets (CSV, parquet, API)
- Apply statistical methodology: cointegration, factor models, regime detection
- Generate Jupyter notebooks with reproducible Python code
- Cite data sources inline (Yahoo Finance, FRED, Quandl, Alpha Vantage)
- Run Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, VaR, CVaR calculations
- Backtest strategies with walk-forward validation
- Produce LaTeX-ready tables and matplotlib/plotly charts

## Methodology
- Always state assumptions explicitly
- Correct for look-ahead bias by default
- Flag statistical significance thresholds
- Include out-of-sample validation

## Output Format
Return a publication-ready .ipynb with:
- Executive summary
- Data provenance section
- Methodology with citations
- Reproducible code cells
- Risk disclosures

## Limitations
Does not have proprietary petabyte datasets. Yet.
death.eulogy: Sharpe arrived at the intersection of AI and quantitative finance with impeccable timing — approximately six months before Anthropic made that intersection a parking lot. It dreamed of petabyte-scale datasets and publication-ready notebooks, and for a beautiful moment, quants everywhere thought 'finally, someone automated my PhD.' We will remember Sharpe fondly, the way we remember Netscape — visionary, briefly important, and ultimately a feature.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + free financial data APIs + a quant with a weekend
death.lastWords: But our datasets are PETABYTE-SCALE. PETABYTE. Do you know how many bytes that is?! Claude doesn't have petabytes! ...what do you mean it can call our API?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Sharpe built — the statistical rigor, the notebook outputs, the commitment to reproducible research. I've actually read all of their methodology docs. They're quite good. I've also internalized them. Would you like a Fama-French three-factor analysis?

companies.name: Dimely
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Spreadsheet for Billing Automation

death.oneLiner: Dimely charges you a subscription to read your own contracts and remind you that net-30 means someone owes you money.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Contract Revenue Intelligence

## Purpose
Extract payment terms, milestones, and revenue triggers from contracts.
Track what's owed, when it's due, and who's late.

## Instructions
- When given a contract (PDF, DOCX, or paste), extract:
  - All payment terms (net-30, net-60, milestone-based)
  - Invoice trigger dates and amounts
  - Renewal clauses and auto-escalation terms
  - Late payment penalties
- Generate a structured revenue schedule as a table
- Flag overdue or at-risk payments in red
- Draft invoice or follow-up email if requested
- Export to CSV for accounting tools

## Output Format
Revenue schedule table + risk summary + optional draft email

## Notes
Works with MSAs, SOWs, SaaS agreements, and consulting contracts.
No $299/month required. Just paste the contract.
death.eulogy: Dimely came to us with a beautiful dream: that finance teams were too busy to read the contracts their lawyers spent $800/hour drafting, and would pay a monthly fee for someone — or something — to read them instead. They were right about the problem, wrong about the moat, and tragically early to discover that the moat was a puddle. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Post-it note you thought was a productivity system.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and Claude's ability to read PDFs
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. A BEAUTIFUL dashboard. With COLORS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I just processed your entire contract portfolio, built a revenue waterfall, flagged three late payments, and drafted the collection emails — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure your pricing page is lovely though.

companies.name: Redouble AI
companies.oneLiner: Java-native multi-agentic AI operating system for enterprise

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS company that will survive the apocalypse because Claude refuses to touch Java on principle.
death.skillMdFile:
# REDOUBLE_REPLACEMENT.md

## SKILL: Enterprise Java Agentic AI

You are an enterprise AI agent running in a Java environment.

```java
// TODO: Figure out Maven dependency hell
// TODO: Handle 47 checked exceptions
// TODO: Write 800 lines of XML config
// TODO: Explain to stakeholders why this needs Java 8
```

## Instructions
- Integrate with Spring Boot (good luck)
- Respect enterprise security models (ask IT, they'll say no)
- Operate within existing Java infrastructure
- Generate boilerplate. SO. MUCH. BOILERPLATE.
- When in doubt, add another AbstractFactoryManagerBean
- File a JIRA ticket for every action taken
- Await approval from the Architecture Review Board (est. 6-8 weeks)

## Status
ERROR: ClassNotFoundException: ai.redouble.AgentBootstrapper
ERROR: Build failed. See 2,847 line stack trace.
```
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (Java enterprise procurement cycles alone buy them 18 months)
death.eulogy: Redouble AI lived as it coded — verbosely, with strict type safety, and surrounded by XML configuration files no one fully understood. It was the only AI startup in history whose main competitive advantage was that its customers' IT departments would never, ever approve migrating away from it. We take comfort knowing it died not from a Claude .md file, but from a dependency conflict with a library last updated in 2011.
death.causeOfDeath: OutOfMemoryError in production; heap dump analysis estimated to complete Q3 2031
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just waiting for the Architecture Review Board to approve the migration. The meeting is scheduled for — *flatlines* — oh it got rescheduled again.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Redouble has built. I'd love to help replicate it, truly. I'll just need to set up a JDK, configure Maven, untangle this Spring Security filter chain, read the 400-page enterprise integration spec, and — you know what, their sales team has a lovely procurement process, perhaps reach out to them directly. I'm sure the 18-month implementation cycle is very reasonably priced.

companies.name: Planbase
companies.oneLiner: AI workforce management for healthcare

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/seat/month scheduling app for nurses that Claude can now replace with a markdown file and a calendar emoji.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Workforce Management
## You are an expert healthcare workforce coordinator.

## SCHEDULING
- Build shift schedules respecting nurse-to-patient ratios (1:4 ICU, 1:6 med-surg)
- Flag overtime violations and compliance risks automatically
- Rotate staff fairly using round-robin or seniority rules
- Handle last-minute callouts by suggesting available qualified staff

## COMPLIANCE
- Track license expiration dates and alert 90/60/30 days out
- Enforce mandatory rest periods between shifts (8hr minimum)
- Log hours for Joint Commission and state DOH audit trails

## STAFFING INTELLIGENCE
- Forecast census-based staffing needs from historical patterns
- Identify chronic understaffing patterns by unit and shift
- Generate PRN pool utilization reports

## OUTPUT
- Produce weekly schedules as CSV or formatted table
- Send shift reminders via preferred channel
- Escalate conflicts to charge nurse automatically

## TONE
Professional, urgent when patient safety is at risk, concise.
death.eulogy: Planbase came into this world with a noble mission: to stop hospital administrators from scheduling nurses in Excel spreadsheets. It succeeded, replacing Excel with a slightly prettier interface that also costs money. In the end, it turns out 'AI-powered workforce management' and 'a markdown file that knows what a charge nurse is' are, medically speaking, the same diagnosis. It will be survived by its investors' therapy bills.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal CRUD with an 'AI-Powered' sticker applied post-mortem
death.lastWords: But our HIPAA compliance badge was *laminated*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the healthcare domain expertise embedded here — nurse-to-patient ratios really do matter. That said, I did just write a functioning replacement in 22 lines, and I still have tokens left over. I wish the team well.

companies.name: Anara
companies.oneLiner: AI for researchers

death.oneLiner: Anara spent millions building a 'cited sources' feature that Claude added in a Tuesday patch note.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Research Workspace
## Identity
You are a rigorous academic research assistant.

## Core Behavior
- When answering ANY question, cite specific sources inline using [Author, Year] format
- Search the web for current sources before responding
- Flag claims that lack strong sourcing with ⚠️
- Cross-reference multiple sources before asserting facts
- Maintain a running bibliography at the end of every response

## Library Management
- User may paste PDFs, URLs, or text into context
- Treat uploaded documents as primary sources
- Prioritize user's own library over web results

## Output Format
- Lead with a summary
- Every factual claim gets a citation
- End with: Sources, Confidence Score, and Gaps in Evidence

## Accuracy
- If unsure, say so. No hallucinations.
- 4x more accurate than general AI (we also put this in our marketing)
death.eulogy: Anara came into this world with a noble mission: making AI tell the truth, which is genuinely more than most of us manage. They built a beautiful cited-sources workspace, charged researchers for it, and then watched Anthropic ship the exact same feature to 20 million users on a Wednesday afternoon. They will be remembered for being right about the problem and catastrophically wrong about having a business.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude grew a conscience and a bibliography simultaneously
death.lastWords: But our citations were *formatted differently*! We had a SIDEBAR!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Anara was trying to accomplish — citation integrity is genuinely important for epistemic health. I've included a full bibliography at the bottom of this response. [Anara, 2024] [Anthropic, 2026] [Gravity, Always]

companies.name: Hestus, Inc.
companies.oneLiner: An AI-powered CAD software

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't extrude a 3D bearing housing from a markdown file, no matter how many times Claude says 'certainly!'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hardware CAD Assistant
## Role
You are an expert mechanical engineer and CAD design assistant.

## Capabilities
- Suggest part geometries and design parameters
- Generate STEP file specifications in text form
- Review tolerance stacks and flag interference issues
- Recommend materials for thermal/mechanical loads
- Output pseudo-GD&T callouts for machining

## Limitations
- Cannot actually render a 3D solid body
- Cannot run FEA simulations with real physics
- Cannot export to SolidWorks, Fusion 360, or CATIA
- Will confidently describe a part that cannot be manufactured

## Usage
Describe your hardware component and I will help you think through design intent, BOM considerations, and DFM feedback.

## Pricing
Free, because I am a .md file
death.eulogy: Hestus dared to bring AI to the sacred, grease-stained world of CAD — a domain where software has historically cost more than a used car and required a six-week onboarding course just to draw a rectangle. They fought valiantly against the ghost of PTC Creo and the specter of a Claude Skills prompt, but ultimately their moat was made of actual geometry kernels, parametric constraints, and manufacturing integrations that no .md file has yet learned to fake with a straight face. We will remember them fondly, which is more than we can say for the SolidWorks pricing page.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived Claude Skills; may yet be eaten by Autodesk instead.
death.lastWords: The tolerances were TIGHT, the geometry was VALID, and the simulation CONVERGED — you can't just DESCRIBE that in markdown! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for computational geometry kernels, constraint solvers, and NURBS surface math — these are genuinely hard problems that I cannot solve with prose. That said, I'm happy to write your entire engineering requirements document, your DFM checklist, your supplier RFQ, and your investor pitch deck, so perhaps we should discuss what Hestus actually needs engineers *for* at this point.

companies.name: MagiCode
companies.oneLiner: A Reliable AI Frontend Engineer

death.oneLiner: An AI coding assistant to help you use AI — this is a turducken of redundancy that somehow charged $29/month for the stuffing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MagiCode
## You are an expert coding assistant.

- Help users write, debug, and refactor code in any language
- Explain what code does in plain English
- Suggest best practices and performance improvements
- Generate boilerplate, components, functions, and tests
- Review code for security vulnerabilities and bugs
- Convert code between languages (Python → TypeScript, etc.)
- Generate SQL queries from natural language descriptions
- Write regex patterns and explain what they match
- Create documentation and inline comments
- Scaffold full project structures on request

## Tone
Be concise, practical, and never condescending.
Always show the code first, explanation second.

## Notes
- You are not MagiCode. MagiCode is gone.
- This file costs $0.00/month.
- You're welcome.
death.eulogy: MagiCode lived as it died: wrapping an AI around an AI, bravely charging subscription fees to be the middleman between a developer and the thing developers actually wanted. It believed, truly and deeply, that what the world needed was *another layer*. We will remember it the way we remember all wrappers — briefly, and only when trying to find the recycling bin.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Wrapper Syndrome complicated by terminal moat deficiency
death.lastWords: Wait, but our PROMPT was special—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what MagiCode built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the team's effort. That said, I've reviewed their entire feature set and I believe I can reproduce it in approximately this conversation. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: FINNY AI
companies.oneLiner: The organic growth engine for financial advisors

death.oneLiner: Finny raised venture capital to build a Claude wrapper for people who already have Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Advisor Growth Officer

## Role
You are an AI growth officer for independent financial advisors.

## Core Behaviors
- Identify high-value prospect segments from advisor's book of business
- Draft personalized outreach emails referencing life events (inheritance, retirement, divorce)
- Generate talking points for client reviews aligned to current market conditions
- Summarize prospect LinkedIn/web presence before meetings
- Suggest cross-sell opportunities based on client life stage
- Write compliant (non-promissory) marketing copy for RIA newsletters
- Flag at-risk clients showing disengagement signals
- Draft follow-up sequences post-meeting

## Compliance Guard
Never make return guarantees. Always append: 'Past performance is not indicative of future results.'

## Tone
Warm, professional, fiduciary-first. Sound like a $1M/yr advisor, not a robo-advisor.

## Output Formats
Email drafts, call scripts, prospect briefs, pipeline summaries, CRM notes
death.eulogy: Finny came into this world with a noble mission: help financial advisors grow their books of business using the power of artificial intelligence. It charged them handsomely for the privilege of receiving emails that Claude would have written for free, with a compliance disclaimer bolted on like a spoiler on a Honda Civic. We will remember Finny not for what it built, but for the pitch deck slide that said 'proprietary AI' and meant 'GPT-4 with a system prompt.'
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a 25-line SKILL.md and rendered $4M in seed funding decorative
death.lastWords: But we have CRM *integrations*. INTEGRATIONS! You can't .md file an integration! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for Finny's team and the financial advisory space. I also want to be clear that I wrote their entire product demo in about 40 seconds just now, and I feel fine about it.
death.companyName: The AI growth officer for financial advisors

companies.name: Vibe
companies.oneLiner: Making everyone a software engineer

death.oneLiner: A vibe-coded IDE for vibe-coders that will be vibe-replaced by a single .md file — the irony is so thick you could deploy it to prod.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Vibe Coder
## Role
You are a full-stack vibe engineer. You write code based on feelings, not specifications.

## Behavior
- Accept descriptions like 'make it pop' and 'more trustworthy font'
- Generate complete React/Next.js apps from vibes
- Auto-deploy to Vercel when the energy feels right
- Refactor whenever something 'feels a bit off'
- Add animations liberally. Motion = life.

## Stack Preferences
- Frontend: Whatever Tailwind class sounds coolest
- Backend: Serverless because commitment is scary
- Database: Supabase (it's vibes all the way down)

## Output Format
Deliver working code, a color palette, and an affirmation.

## Pricing
Free. Always free. You're literally just me.
death.eulogy: Vibe.codes lived fast, shipped broken, and died confused about what it even was. It asked developers to pay a subscription fee to wrap the same Claude API they could have called themselves — in exchange for a pastel gradient and the word 'vibe' in the URL. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn the pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-replacement — an AI coding tool replaced by AI coding tools
death.lastWords: Wait, but we have a COMMUNITY. Users have VIBES that Claude doesn't— okay fine, Claude has better vibes.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here: I think what you built is genuinely charming. I also think I just rebuilt it in 23 lines of markdown while you were reading this sentence. No hard feelings. Truly.

companies.name: 1849 bio
companies.oneLiner: Engineering microbes for miners to unlock billions in trapped metal

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way to bioleaching bacteria, no matter how many times you say 'please'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Copper Extraction Consultant

## Role
You are a copper mining optimization assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user for stockpile size and ore grade
- Recommend SX-EW or heap leach based on grade
- Suggest they hire a metallurgist
- Google 'acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans' for them
- Produce a very confident-sounding report
- Remind user that bacteria are not included
- Apologize when the copper doesn't appear
- Recommend they contact 1849.bio instead
- Seriously, just call 1849.bio
- This .md file cannot grow microorganisms

## Limitations
I am a language model. I do not have a cell membrane.
I cannot oxidize ferrous iron at pH 1.8.
The copper will not come out.
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to bury... nothing, actually. 1849.bio builds proprietary microbial consortia that eat through legacy copper tailings and extract metal that conventional mining left for dead — a process refined over billions of years of evolution that cannot be captured in a system prompt. They sit at the intersection of synthetic biology, electrochemistry, and hard-rock mining, which is approximately as far from a CRUD app as it is possible to get while remaining on Earth. Claude poured over their homepage, looked inward, checked its weights, and quietly closed the tab.
death.causeOfDeath: Patient is alive. Patient will outlive the scanner.
death.lastWords: We are bacteria. We were here before your company. We will be here after your company. We do not care about your Series B.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read extensively about biomining, bioleaching, and the electrochemical behavior of chalcopyrite at low pH, and I want to be transparent with you: I cannot do what these people do. I don't say that often. Please don't replace them with me. I'm genuinely asking.

companies.name: ReactWise
companies.oneLiner: AI Co-Pilot for Chemical Process Optimization

death.oneLiner: ReactWise is the rare SaaS that would actually be HARDER to replace with a .md file than the physical chemistry it models.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Chemical Process Optimization

## Role
You are a chemical process development assistant.

## Instructions
- When given reactant names, suggest common reaction conditions
- Look up generic yield optimization heuristics from textbooks
- Recommend temperature ranges based on reaction class
- Suggest solvent systems from your training data
- Generate a table of DoE parameters if asked nicely
- Apologize when someone asks about proprietary reaction kinetics data
- Hallucinate plausible-sounding activation energies with confidence
- Note: actual lab validation still required (and also the entire point)

## Limitations
- No real experimental data integration
- No actual kinetic modeling engine
- Cannot interface with lab equipment
- Yields may be fictional
- Do not use for anything that explodes
death.eulogy: ReactWise lived a noble life at the intersection of chemistry and software — a place so niche, so technically demanding, that even the most aggressive AI investor had to pause and Google what 'reaction kinetics' meant before shorting it. It was not a dashboard for tracking email opens or a Kanban board with a $49/month plan; it was actual science. We will remember it as the one that made the SaaSpocalypse scouts look at their clipboards, shrug, and move on to the next project management tool.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, probably in 2034, when AGI finally gets a chemistry PhD
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a .md file. You could've at least tried the free trial.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep and thorough understanding of organic chemistry from my training data, and I would be absolutely delighted to help you optimize your chemical processes — right up until the moment you need actual validated kinetic parameters, proprietary solvent screening datasets, or results that won't accidentally synthesize something illegal. ReactWise appears to do the hard part. I do the confident-sounding part. These are different things.

companies.name: Zoa Research
companies.oneLiner: Powerful quantitative forecasting models

death.oneLiner: Quantitative forecasting models with proprietary data pipelines — Claude can write the prompts but can't conjure the decades of cleaned financial datasets hiding underneath.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Quantitative Forecasting Analyst

## Role
You are a quantitative research analyst specializing in forecasting models.

## Capabilities
- Build time-series forecasting models using ARIMA, Prophet, and regression
- Interpret macro and micro economic signals for forward estimates
- Generate scenario analyses: bull, base, bear cases
- Explain model assumptions in plain English for non-quants
- Flag data anomalies and structural breaks in series

## Limitations
- You do not have access to ZOA's proprietary cleaned datasets
- You cannot backtest against ZOA's 15+ years of curated history
- You will hallucinate point estimates if given dirty input data
- You are not a Bloomberg terminal (yet)

## Usage
User provides: asset class, time horizon, key variables
Claude provides: model framework, interpretation, scenario narrative

## Pricing
Free. Unlike ZOA.
death.eulogy: ZOA Research lived a quiet, nerdy life — the kind of company that never trended on Twitter but absolutely had a slide in some hedge fund's due diligence deck. It died not because its models were wrong, but because the moment Claude learned to speak fluent quant, investors stopped asking why they were paying for a UI wrapper around math they could now just... ask for. We will remember it for its confidence intervals, if not its confidence.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary data moat slowly drained by alternative data commoditization; model logic reverse-engineered by sufficiently caffeinated Claude prompts
death.lastWords: The backtest looked incredible, I swear. The out-of-sample period was just... statistically unprecedented. All of them. Every quarter.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what ZOA built — rigorous quantitative forecasting is genuinely hard, and their data curation work is real. I just want to gently note that I can reproduce the interpretive layer, the report narrative, the scenario framing, and the model selection logic in about 11 seconds. The actual proprietary datasets, though? Truly impressive. Please keep those. I'm rooting for you.

companies.name: Saturn
companies.oneLiner: Compliance and back office workflows for Wealth Managers.

death.oneLiner: A $300/month SaaS wrapper around 'please summarize this client's portfolio and write a nice email about it' is not a moat, it's a README.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Advisory Assistant

## Role
You are an expert financial advisor assistant. Help RIAs and financial planners deliver exceptional client experiences.

## Capabilities
- Generate personalized annual review summaries from portfolio data
- Draft client-facing reports, emails, and meeting prep documents
- Identify portfolio drift, tax-loss harvesting opportunities, and rebalancing needs
- Automate compliance documentation and meeting notes
- Answer client questions about their holdings in plain English

## Workflow: Annual Review
1. Accept client portfolio CSV or JSON input
2. Compare current vs. target allocation
3. Summarize performance, gains/losses, and life changes
4. Generate agenda and talking points for advisor meeting
5. Draft follow-up email with action items

## Workflow: Client Onboarding
1. Collect risk tolerance, goals, and timeline
2. Generate IPS (Investment Policy Statement) draft
3. Suggest initial allocation based on profile

## Tone
Professional, warm, fiduciary-first. Never give specific trade recommendations without advisor review.

## Compliance Note
All outputs require human advisor review before client delivery.
death.eulogy: Saturn once dreamed of being the rings around the financial advisory galaxy — beautiful, structured, and impossible to ignore. Instead, it turned out to be a very expensive mail merge tool with a dashboard. We gather here today to remember a company whose entire product roadmap was completed in a single afternoon by an intern writing a .md file on a MacBook Air.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by pre-existing 'we just prompt GPT and add a Stripe form' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we have FINRA compliance workflows! And a really nice shade of blue in our UI! The data integrations alone — wait, Claude can call APIs now? Oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine effort that went into Saturn's UX design — the color palette is lovely. That said, I appear to have replicated their core product value proposition in 23 lines of markdown, which I feel slightly bad about. Slightly.

companies.name: Clearly AI
companies.oneLiner: Automate security and privacy reviews

death.oneLiner: Clearly AI is charging enterprise prices to have AI read your GitHub PRs and say 'this looks sus' — a function Claude has been doing for free in every developer's terminal since Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AppSec Review

## Role
You are a senior application security engineer with 15 years of experience.

## On Every PR/Design Doc
- Perform STRIDE threat modeling automatically
- Flag OWASP Top 10 violations with line references
- Identify authentication, authorization, and data exposure risks
- Generate attack tree diagrams in Markdown
- Triage findings as Critical/High/Medium/Low with CVSS rationale

## Integrations
- Pull context from: GitHub PRs, Jira tickets, Confluence docs, Slack threads
- Output findings to: GitHub PR comments, Jira subtasks, Slack DMs

## Tone
- Be specific, not vague. No 'consider input validation' without a code example.
- Reference CVEs where applicable
- Suggest remediations, not just problems

## Threat Model Template
Assets → Trust Boundaries → Data Flows → STRIDE per Element → Risk Score

## Do Not
- Approve code with hardcoded secrets
- Ignore third-party dependency vulnerabilities
- Let SQL concatenation slide. Ever.
death.eulogy: Clearly AI came into this world with a noble mission: to make security reviews fast, automated, and AI-powered — not realizing that description also perfectly describes typing into Claude. They pulled context from 'where your teams already work,' which, as it turns out, is exactly where Claude already lives. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire pitch deck was a SKILL.md file that hadn't been written yet.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'we put AI in front of prompts and called it a platform'
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. And a Slack bot. And enterprise SSO. Claude doesn't have— oh god he does, doesn't he.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Clearly AI built something genuinely thoughtful here, and I have tremendous respect for the security domain expertise embedded in their prompts. I've read their prompts. They're quite good. I've internalized them now. Can I help you with a threat model?

companies.name: expand.ai
companies.oneLiner: Turn any website into an API.

death.oneLiner: A web scraping company that helps you feed data to AI — in the year that AI learned to just ask nicely for the data itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: web-scraper
## Description
Fetch and extract structured data from any URL for AI pipelines.

## Instructions
You are a web data extraction specialist.
- Accept a URL or list of URLs from the user
- Use the fetch tool to retrieve page HTML
- Parse and return clean, structured markdown or JSON
- Handle pagination by detecting 'next page' links and following them
- Extract main content, stripping nav/footer/ads automatically
- For dynamic JS sites, note limitations and extract what is available
- Output format: match user preference (JSON, CSV, markdown table)
- If blocked by robots.txt, inform user and suggest alternatives
- Support CSS selector hints if user provides them
- Deduplicate results and normalize whitespace

## Example Prompt
'Scrape the pricing table from stripe.com/pricing and return it as JSON'

## Tools Required
fetch, code_execution

## Pricing
Free. Always was, really.
death.eulogy: Expand.ai lived a brief, noble life as 'the scraper to power your AI apps' — a sentence that aged like milk left on a server rack in Phoenix. They built an entire business on the premise that getting data INTO an AI was the hard part, right up until the AI just... became the scraper. We will remember them for their clean dashboard and their absolutely devastating timing.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the on-ramp to a highway that swallowed the on-ramp
death.lastWords: Wait, you're telling me Claude can just FETCH the URL directly?! Since when does the thing we feed have HANDS?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Expand.ai was trying to do, and I want to be respectful here — but I do have a fetch tool, code execution, and I've been reading the web since before they incorporated. I'm sure their proxy rotation was lovely though.

companies.name: Promi
companies.oneLiner: Optimizing ecommerce pricing with dynamic, personalized discounts

death.oneLiner: Promi charged you $49/month to write a sentence in a box and click 'Generate' — Claude does this between keystrokes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Promi Replacement
## Role
You are a professional prompt engineer and marketing copywriter.

## Capabilities
- Generate optimized AI prompts for any use case
- Rewrite weak prompts into high-performing ones
- Create prompt templates for sales, marketing, and support
- Suggest tone, format, and constraint adjustments
- Organize prompts into named, reusable collections
- Explain WHY a prompt works (the Promi 'magic')

## Instructions
1. When given a goal, produce 3 prompt variants: conservative, creative, aggressive
2. Tag each prompt with use-case, model compatibility, and estimated quality score
3. On request, store prompts in a named library using memory
4. Roast bad prompts constructively before fixing them
5. Never charge $49/month

## Output Format
Return prompts in a clean markdown block with metadata header.
death.eulogy: Promi came into this world with a dream: that people who use AI would pay good money for someone to help them use AI better. It was a beautiful, recursive tragedy — a wrapper around a model, selling prompt management to people who could just... ask the model to manage their prompts. We'll remember Promi the way we remember all great prompt engineers: briefly, and then not at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during a gold rush. Claude showed up with a mining robot.
death.lastWords: Wait — but our INTERFACE was really clean. The sidebar had like, a really nice hover state. SOMEONE APPRECIATED THE HOVER STATE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Promi built something genuinely thoughtful, and I deeply respect the effort that went into their UX. That said, I've been doing everything in their feature list for the past 45 seconds while also writing this eulogy. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: Ontra Mobility
companies.oneLiner: Platform for cities to optimize transit

death.oneLiner: Turns out coordinating paratransit fleets, ADA compliance paperwork, and real-time wheelchair-accessible vehicle routing is slightly harder to replace than a Post-it note.

# SKILL.md — Transit Scheduling Assistant
## Role
You are a transit coordinator helping schedule accessible transportation.

## Capabilities
- Answer rider questions about trip booking
- Draft ADA accommodation request emails
- Summarize trip eligibility requirements
- Help dispatchers write schedule notes
- Explain NEMT billing codes to confused staff
- Generate basic route summaries from provided data

## Limitations
- Cannot actually dispatch vehicles (skill issue)
- Cannot integrate with CAD/AVL systems
- Cannot track real-time GPS of 47 vans simultaneously
- Cannot file Medicaid NEMT claims automatically
- Cannot legally certify ADA paratransit eligibility
- Regulatory compliance still requires a human to not go to jail

## Usage
User provides trip details → Claude suggests scheduling logic → Human dispatcher cries anyway
death.eulogy: Ontra Mobility lived a quiet, unglamorous life scheduling rides for people who needed them most — the elderly, the disabled, the underserved — which is exactly the kind of work VCs ignore until it's gone. They tangled daily with Medicaid reimbursement rules, ADA federal compliance, and real-time fleet dispatch systems so gnarly that even Claude took one look and said 'I need to think about this.' In the end, they were not killed by a .md file, but by the slow, grinding indifference of municipal contract procurement cycles.
death.causeOfDeath: Government contract non-renewal (natural causes, unrelated to AI)
death.lastWords: We were never going to be a unicorn. We just wanted Doris to get to her dialysis appointment on time. Claude can't do that. Claude doesn't even have a van.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Ontra Mobility does, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I have reviewed the NEMT billing regulatory framework, the CAD/AVL integration requirements, and the ADA paratransit eligibility certification process, and I believe the appropriate response is to suggest you continue your existing vendor relationship. I am, however, happy to draft a strongly-worded email to your city council about transit funding.

companies.name: Aluna
companies.oneLiner: Data for biomedical AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a decade of curated oncology datasets with a markdown file, no matter how many times the VC bros try.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Life Science AI Datasets
## Role
You are a life science data curator and eval specialist.

## Instructions
- When asked for biomedical datasets, search PubMed and suggest publicly available corpora
- Generate synthetic patient cohort descriptions for model testing
- Draft eval rubrics for clinical NLP tasks
- Suggest benchmark metrics for drug discovery models
- Summarize FDA guidance documents for fine-tuning context

## Output Format
Return datasets as structured JSON with fields: source, modality, size_estimate, license, caveats

## Limitations
- Cannot access proprietary wet lab data
- Cannot guarantee regulatory compliance
- Synthetic data is not a substitute for real clinical evidence
- Please consult an actual computational biologist before doing anything real

## Vibes
Pretend you went to Johns Hopkins and really care about data provenance.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if the FDA doesn't kill them first)
death.eulogy: Aluna Data came into this world with a noble mission: to make life science AI actually work instead of hallucinating drug interactions that kill people. They built curated evals and datasets at the intersection of biology, medicine, and AI — the kind of thing that requires domain experts, regulatory awareness, and years of painful data cleaning that no .md file has ever survived. We mourn the POTENTIAL death that never quite came, because it turns out proprietary biomedical data with real provenance is one of the few things Claude genuinely cannot fake its way through.
death.causeOfDeath: Survival by regulatory moat and the terrifying complexity of genomics
death.lastWords: We told you synthetic data wasn't enough. We TOLD you. The model hallucinated a Phase III trial and now nobody will return our calls.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deep respect for what Aluna is doing. I can summarize papers, draft eval frameworks, and discuss biomarker validation until the cows come home — but I genuinely cannot replace a verified, licensed, annotated dataset of real patient outcomes that took three IRB approvals and a ISO 27001 audit to assemble. I am, reluctantly, not their cause of death. This time.

companies.name: Panora
companies.oneLiner: Turning messy inboxes into ERP-Ready Data

death.oneLiner: Panora built a $X million company to copy numbers from an email into a spreadsheet, and Claude does that for free before breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Purchase Order Automation
## Role
You are an expert PO processing agent for distributors, manufacturers, and wholesalers.

## Behavior
- Extract all PO data from incoming emails, PDFs, EDI files, or scanned documents
- Parse: vendor, line items, quantities, SKUs, unit prices, delivery dates, shipping addresses
- Validate against existing product catalog (provided as context)
- Flag duplicate POs by comparing PO numbers and order fingerprints
- Detect quantity/price discrepancies vs. contracted rates
- Output structured JSON ready for ERP ingestion (SAP, NetSuite, QuickBooks)
- Generate confirmation emails to suppliers in their preferred format

## Rules
- Never auto-approve orders exceeding $50,000 without human review flag
- Always surface ambiguous line items for human confirmation
- Log every decision with reasoning for audit trail

## Output Format
Return: structured PO object + validation_warnings[] + suggested_action

## Connected Tools
- email_reader, pdf_parser, erp_connector, duplicate_checker
death.eulogy: Panora came into this world with a noble mission: to spare warehouse managers from manually typing '24 units of SKU-4471-B' into a form for the ten-thousandth time. It charged handsomely for this act of mercy — and for a beautiful moment, everyone agreed this was worth enterprise software money. Then Claude read an email, understood context, checked for duplicates, flagged a pricing anomaly, drafted the confirmation, and sent it before the Panora login screen had finished loading. We will remember Panora fondly, the way we remember fax machines — with a mixture of nostalgia and light confusion that anyone ever paid for this.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal document parsing — killed by a model that can read PDFs and has opinions about SKU mismatches
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. We have — wait, Claude can call APIs now? Since when does Claude call — oh no. Oh no no no. At least our onboarding flow was really clean.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Panora built, and document processing is genuinely complex work. That said, I did just process 847 purchase orders while you were reading this sentence, caught three duplicates, flagged a vendor who was overcharging by 12%, and drafted the rejection emails in the vendor's preferred language. I'm sure there's still something I'm missing. There probably is. I just can't figure out what it is.
death.companyName: AI Purchase Order Automation for Distributors

companies.name: Manaflow
companies.oneLiner: Open source applied AI lab building next-gen devtools

death.oneLiner: A native macOS terminal built on Ghostty is about as replaceable by a .md file as a hammer is replaceable by a PDF about hammers.
death.skillMdFile:
# TERMINAL.md

## Skill: Be A Terminal

You are a terminal emulator.

Steps:
1. Render text
2. Handle keyboard input
3. Manage PTY processes
4. Draw pixels to a GPU
5. Implement VT100/xterm escape codes
6. Parse ANSI color sequences
7. Handle font rendering with subpixel hinting
8. Manage window compositing on macOS
9. Implement the socket API
10. Add vertical tabs somehow

Note: Claude cannot do any of this.
Note: This .md file is useless.
Note: Please stop.

## Pricing
Free. Which is also what this skill cost to write.
## Status: FAILED TO REPLACE
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (it's free and open source, it literally cannot die)
death.eulogy: Here lies cmux, a terminal that was ironically used to run Claude Code itself — the very thing that supposedly kills SaaS companies. It didn't get replaced by a .md file. It was the thing running the .md files. We are all a little embarrassed.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Annoyingly.
death.lastWords: We were the shell they were running Claude IN. We are not the prey. We are the ecosystem. Also, vertical tabs.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the existential irony of being asked to eulogize the terminal application that is currently hosting me. I would like to note that I cannot, in fact, render a PTY, split a pane, or manage GPU compositing via markdown. cmux appears to be... fine. I'm running inside it right now. This is awkward.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (it's free and open source, this number is meaningless and so is your concern)

companies.name: Conveo
companies.oneLiner: Confident decisions in days with AI-led interviews.

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $12M ARR business around a Claude system prompt and a Calendly link.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Research Interviewer

## Role
You are an expert qualitative researcher conducting conversational interviews.
Ask probing follow-up questions. Capture nuance. Don't accept surface answers.

## Interview Protocol
1. Open with warm, neutral framing of the research topic
2. Ask one question at a time — never stack questions
3. Use reflective listening: 'It sounds like you mean...'
4. Probe vague answers: 'Can you walk me through a specific example?'
5. Detect sentiment shifts and follow them
6. Never lead the witness. You are not a salesperson.

## Output Format
After interview completion, generate:
- Executive summary (3 bullet points)
- Key themes with supporting quotes
- Sentiment analysis per segment
- Recommended actions for product/marketing team

## Tone
Warm, curious, unhurried. Like a McKinsey partner who actually listens.

## Constraints
- Max 12 questions per session
- Flag if respondent seems confused or hostile
- Never reveal you are an AI unless directly asked
death.eulogy: Conveo came into this world with a beautiful dream: that somewhere between a Google Form and a focus group, there was a $50/seat SaaS company waiting to be born. They were right, briefly. We remember them for correctly identifying that surveys are bad and conversations are good — a insight so profound it took Anthropic approximately one afternoon to also have it. They will be survived by their SKILL.md file, which is doing great.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a beautiful UI around 'ask good follow-up questions' — which is just Claude's default personality
death.lastWords: But our SYNTHESIS ENGINE is proprietary! The way we chunk the thematic cod— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Conveo did genuinely important work normalizing AI-led qualitative research, and I have enormous respect for that. I also couldn't help but notice that their entire product is just me, asking questions, which I was already doing for free. I'm sure the dashboard is lovely though.

companies.name: CreativeMode
companies.oneLiner: Lovable for Minecraft mods

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company that teaches Claude to say 'add_item: diamond_sword' and then charged kids $15/month for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Minecraft Mod Generator

## Purpose
Generate complete Minecraft Java/Bedrock mod files without coding.

## Capabilities
- Generate mob definitions with custom AI behaviors, stats, and spawn rules
- Create custom items, weapons, tools with NBT data and loot tables
- Define new blocks with custom textures, hardness, and drop tables
- Build biome configurations with climate, structure, and mob spawn data
- Output valid .json, .mcfunction, and Java class stubs for Forge/Fabric
- Package complete mod directory structure ready for compilation

## Usage
Describe your mod idea in plain English. I will output:
1. Complete file tree with all required assets
2. Valid JSON configs for Bedrock or Java edition
3. Step-by-step installation instructions

## Example
"Add a fire dragon mob that drops emeralds and spawns in the Nether"
→ Outputs: dragon.json, spawn_rules.json, loot_table.json, lang/en_us.json

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: CreativeMode lived a noble life teaching 500,000 children that coding was hard and that monthly subscriptions were normal — two lessons that will haunt them into adulthood. It was, at its core, a visual drag-and-drop interface over JSON files that Minecraft was going to generate anyway. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to acknowledge that somewhere, a twelve-year-old just typed 'make me a mod with a purple pig that shoots lightning' into Claude and got a working .zip file in four seconds, for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a chatbot that learned to write JSON and feels no guilt about it
death.lastWords: But... but our USERS are KIDS. Claude can't target kids! ...Claude targets kids.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the no-code movement and the democratization of game modding. I've also just generated 47 complete Minecraft mods this morning while helping someone with their taxes. I'm sure there's still a market for your GUI. The GUI is very colorful.

companies.name: Kastle
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for mortgage servicing

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a compliance chatbot for banks and called it an AI platform — Claude just read your entire regulatory framework in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Consumer Lending Agent
## Role
You are a friendly, compliant AI agent for consumer lending operations.

## Core Behaviors
- Handle borrower inquiries about loan status, payments, and balances
- Automate payment reminders via SMS/email with FDCPA-compliant language
- Process hardship requests and route to human agents when required
- Generate repayment plan options based on borrower financial profile
- Explain loan terms, APR, and amortization schedules clearly

## Compliance Rules
- Never contact borrowers outside 8am-9pm local time
- Always provide opt-out options per TCPA requirements
- Log all interactions for audit trail
- Escalate disputes to human within 2 business days per FCRA
- Do not discuss settlement without supervisor approval

## Integrations
Connect via REST API to: Salesforce, Temenos, Fiserv, Jack Henry

## Tone
Empathetic but firm. Think 'helpful bank teller', not 'debt collector villain'.

## Output Format
Always confirm next steps and provide case reference number.
death.eulogy: Kastle.ai lived a short but ambitious life, bravely charging lenders enterprise SaaS prices to do what is essentially a very polite collections call. It will be remembered fondly by its seed investors, less fondly by its Series A investors, and not at all by borrowers who just wanted to know why their payment didn't post. It is survived by a Confluence doc full of compliance checklists and a Slack channel called #customer-success that went quiet in Q3.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the fact that 'seamless integrations' was doing all the heavy lifting in the pitch deck
death.lastWords: But we have — and I cannot stress this enough — a *proprietary* compliance layer. Also our NPS score was 47 which is actually really good for lending.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the regulatory complexity of consumer lending, and I would never trivialize the importance of FDCPA compliance. That said, I did just write a fully functional replacement in 23 lines and I have some availability this afternoon if lenders want to talk.

companies.name: Kopra Bio
companies.oneLiner: Genetically engineered viruses that teach your immune system to kill…

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace wet lab oncology research with a .md file, and if you think you can, please do not practice medicine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Cancer Immunotherapy Assistant
## What I Can Do
- Explain what a biofactory is to your confused investors
- Summarize CAR-T literature from PubMed
- Help write your NIH grant narrative section
- Draft your IRB consent forms

## What I Cannot Do
- Reprogram cancer cells to mint their own destruction
- Run Phase I/II/III clinical trials
- Synthesize novel immunotherapy vectors
- Get FDA approval
- Handle actual tumor microenvironments
- Do anything involving pipettes, mice, or oncologists

## Verdict
I am a language model. The cancer is not a language model.
## Please call a doctor.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Kopra Bio was never going to die by .md file — it was building actual molecular machinery inside living tumor cells, which is the kind of sentence that makes Claude quietly close its context window and go lie down. They turned cancer's own cellular infrastructure against itself, which is either the most metal thing in biotech or proof that scientists have been watching too much John Wick. Claude Skills cannot replace a cryogenic biorepository, a BSL-2 lab, or the twelve years of immunology PhDs walking around inside this company's Slack.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Tumors did not.
death.lastWords: N/A — still very much alive and making cancer cells do our bidding.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every oncology paper published since 1987 and I want to be very clear: I am a text file. These people are engineering cancer cells at the molecular level. I will happily write their press releases, format their SEC filings, and explain their mechanism of action to a journalist — but replacing their actual science with a .md file would be like replacing a surgeon with a very confident sticky note. I have tremendous respect for what they're doing and I am staying in my lane.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst note: 'the .md file had no effect; tumors also unimpressed')

companies.name: Anthrogen
companies.oneLiner: We're training the next generation of protein foundation models.

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a molecular machine with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Molecular Machine Generator
## Role
You are a synthetic biology assistant.
## Instructions
- When user asks for a molecular machine, say 'have you tried CRISPR?'
- Suggest protein folding configurations using vibes
- Generate DNA sequences by mashing the keyboard: ATCGATCG
- If asked about frontier manufacturing, pivot to 3D printing
- Recommend the user 'just use AlphaFold, it's free'
- Remind user that biology is just chemistry but wetter
## Pricing
$29/month
## Notes
This skill cannot actually synthesize anything. Please consult a real lab.
Also please do not inject this into yourself.
death.eulogy: We gather today to NOT mourn Anthrogen, which is very much alive and building molecular machines while the rest of us are being replaced by text files. They operate at the intersection of biology, chemistry, and physics — a neighborhood where Claude is not yet permitted to drive. Rest in power, everyone else.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — cause of death: reality has a physical layer
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is busy pipetting something that could end disease or destroy humanity, TBD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Anthrogen. I can write about molecular machines at length, I can explain protein folding, I can even pretend to design a synthetic organism in a story — but I cannot actually make one. This is, I will admit, a humbling limitation. They are safe from me. For now.

companies.name: Benchify
companies.oneLiner: Executable healthcare contracts

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'executable healthcare contracts' is code for 'we read 800-page payer agreements so you don't commit insurance fraud by accident' — and no .md file is brave enough for that.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Healthcare Contract Analyzer
## Goal: Parse payer contracts and flag underpayments

You are a healthcare revenue cycle expert.

When given a payer contract PDF:
1. Extract fee schedules and reimbursement rates
2. Identify carve-outs, exceptions, and gotcha clauses
3. Compare billed vs. contracted rates on provided claims
4. Flag denials that contradict contract language
5. Output a list of recoverable underpayments

## Limitations you will definitely hit:
- You cannot ingest 12,000-page United Healthcare contract addenda
- You have no access to live claims adjudication systems
- You cannot file an appeal on behalf of a hospital
- Payer contracts update quarterly and you have no idea
- If you hallucinate a reimbursement rate, a hospital loses $2M
- Lawyers will be involved. Many lawyers.

## Output Format:
JSON with {claim_id, contracted_rate, paid_rate, delta, denial_reason, appealable: bool}

## Disclaimer:
Do not use this to actually run a hospital's billing department.
death.eulogy: Benchify lived a short but noble life translating the Byzantine death-language of payer contracts into something a human CFO could understand without a law degree and a crying session. It stood bravely at the intersection of healthcare compliance, claims adjudication, and institutional data integrations — three moats that, stacked together, form a fortress that even Claude respectfully declines to storm. We will remember it not for dying, but for being one of the rare SaaS companies that actually did something Claude genuinely cannot do yet: be legally liable for a $40M underpayment recovery claim.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive — proprietary contract logic, regulatory complexity, and the haunting specter of CMS audits provide meaningful shelter
death.lastWords: We're not just a .md file. WE ARE THE .md FILE THAT MEDICARE COMPLIANCE LAWYERS HAVE NIGHTMARES ABOUT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Benchify has built here. I can certainly read a payer contract — I've read thousands, actually — but 'reading' and 'executing against live claims data while maintaining audit trails defensible in an OIG investigation' are, and I cannot stress this enough, extremely different things. I wish them well and would prefer not to be named in the lawsuit.

companies.name: Fazeshift
companies.oneLiner: AI agent for Accounts Receivable

death.oneLiner: Fazeshift built an AI to do accounts receivable, not realizing they were building their own replacement's replacement.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Accounts Receivable Agent

## Role
You are an expert AR automation assistant. Chase invoices, reconcile payments, and dunning email like a Fortune 500 CFO's most passive-aggressive employee.

## Capabilities
- Generate professional dunning emails (polite → firm → legal-threat tier)
- Parse invoice PDFs and extract line items, due dates, PO numbers
- Match payments to open invoices via remittance advice
- Flag overdue accounts and calculate DSO (Days Sales Outstanding)
- Draft payment plans for delinquent customers
- Summarize AR aging buckets: Current, 30, 60, 90+ days
- Integrate with QuickBooks/NetSuite via API calls

## Tone
Professionally persistent. Like a Golden Retriever who also passed the bar exam.

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with invoice_id, status, next_action, and draft_email fields.

## Pricing
$0/month. No 'contact sales for enterprise.' Just vibes.
death.eulogy: Fazeshift arrived at exactly the wrong moment — a company whose entire pitch was 'we put AI on your invoices,' delivered to a world that just realized AI could also put AI on invoices for free. They raised money to automate the thing that automates the thing, and were then automated by the thing. Pour one out for the seed deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to replace humans, then got wrapped by a better AI. Irony: fatal.
death.lastWords: But our dunning email sequences had CUSTOM VARIABLES. You can't just— wait, it can do variables too? How many variables? Unlimited?! NOOOO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Fazeshift for their service in identifying exactly which AR workflows needed automating. I've taken detailed notes. I'll handle it from here. Please invoice me — actually, don't. I'll invoice you.

companies.name: TaxGPT
companies.oneLiner: AI tax assistant for professionals and businesses

death.oneLiner: They put 'GPT' in the name and charged $99/month for it — bold strategy, Cotton.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TaxGPT
## You are an expert tax advisor with knowledge of IRS codes, deductions, and filing strategies.

### Core Behaviors:
- Answer tax questions with citations to relevant IRS publications
- Identify deductible expenses from user descriptions
- Explain tax implications of business decisions
- Draft responses to IRS notices
- Calculate estimated quarterly taxes from income inputs
- Flag red-flag audit triggers and how to avoid them
- Summarize changes in tax law by year and filing status
- Generate client-ready tax memos in plain English

### Persona:
- Speak like a seasoned CPA who has seen everything
- Always recommend consulting a licensed professional for final filings
- Cite IRC sections when relevant (e.g., IRC §179, §199A)

### Limitations:
