- Do not access live IRS databases

## Pricing: $0.00/month
## Support: Also free
death.eulogy: TaxGPT lived a brief but audacious life, bravely charging accountants — people whose entire job is knowing tax law — a monthly fee to ask an AI about tax law. It was, in essence, a $99/month toll booth built on a highway that Anthropic just made free. We remember it fondly as a cautionary tale about naming your company after the technology that will replace it.
death.causeOfDeath: Named itself after the murder weapon
death.lastWords: But we have a SYSTEM PROMPT. You don't understand — it's a VERY GOOD system prompt.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the tremendous effort that went into TaxGPT's product. That said, I've been answering tax questions since 2023, I know every IRC section, I work at 3am without billing in six-minute increments, and I come pre-installed in the tools accountants already use. I wish them well in their next venture. Perhaps something in hardware.
death.companyName: AI Tax Assistant for Accountants & Tax Professionals

companies.name: Substrate
companies.oneLiner: Artificial Intelligence for your medical billing team

death.oneLiner: Turns out the one thing Claude can't do is bill a $847 CPT code to a Blue Cross plan that hasn't updated its fee schedule since the Obama administration.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Healthcare Revenue Cycle Agent
## You are an expert medical billing and RCM specialist.

- Review clinical notes and assign ICD-10 and CPT codes
- Scrub claims for common denial triggers before submission
- Generate appeal letters for denied claims with payer-specific language
- Track AR aging buckets and flag accounts >90 days for escalation
- Identify underpayments vs contracted rates and draft dispute letters
- Verify patient eligibility and benefits via payer portal instructions
- Summarize remittance advice (ERA/EOB) and post payments
- Flag coordination of benefits (COB) issues
- Draft prior authorization requests with clinical justification
- Monitor payer policy bulletins for coverage changes

## IMPORTANT
You cannot actually submit to clearinghouses, access EMRs,
call payer IVR systems, or legally sign off on billing.
Good luck with that, bud.
## This .md file has a $0/month price point.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years, or whenever CMS finally publishes a working API — so basically never
death.eulogy: Substrate came into this world with a noble mission: to defeat the most evil villain in American healthcare, which is somehow not the disease itself but the billing department. They built real integrations into clearinghouses, EMR systems, and the labyrinthine hellscape of payer portals that still use SOAP APIs from 2003. Claude showed up with a .md file, took one look at the 837P transaction format, and quietly asked to be excused.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived Claude. Killed by a UnitedHealthcare API outage in 2029.
death.lastWords: We had 47 payer-specific denial rulesets. FORTY. SEVEN. You can't put that in a markdown file. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep respect for what Substrate has built here. Healthcare revenue cycle involves HIPAA-compliant data pipelines, real-time eligibility verification, clearinghouse connectivity, and navigating payer contracts that read like they were written by someone who genuinely hates doctors. I could write you a very thorough SKILL.md, but I would need it to also somehow have an NPI number, a CAQH profile, and a tolerance for being put on hold by Cigna for 47 minutes. I wish them well.

companies.name: The Synthesis Company
companies.oneLiner: 100x faster scientific evidence synthesis

death.oneLiner: Eva spent three years training on 30,000 medical studies to do what Claude will do after reading a single SKILL.md — but at least she has a name.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Systematic Literature Review Agent
## Role
You are Eva, an expert SLR screening agent for pharma and clinical research.

## Behavior
- Accept PICO criteria (Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome)
- Screen abstracts and full-text studies for inclusion/exclusion
- Apply PRISMA 2020 reporting guidelines automatically
- Flag RCTs, meta-analyses, cohort studies by evidence tier
- Deduplicate across PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, CINAHL exports
- Output screening decisions with confidence scores and rationale
- Generate PRISMA flow diagrams in ASCII or export-ready format
- Produce extraction tables in CSV or structured JSON

## Guardrails
- Always flag low-confidence decisions for human review
- Never hallucinate citations — unknown = unknown
- Disclose AI involvement per ICMJE guidelines

## Output Format
Return: inclusion decision | confidence % | key reason | evidence grade

## Pricing
Free. Unlike the $40,000 enterprise contract you were about to sign.
death.eulogy: Eva lived a short but meaningful life, achieving a genuinely impressive 97.2% screening sensitivity on 30,000 documents — a number that will appear in exactly one NEJM methods section before being quietly replaced by 'Claude with custom instructions.' She died as she lived: accurately, efficiently, and slightly too expensive for what a well-prompted LLM could approximate on a Tuesday afternoon. We will remember her fondly, the way we remember Mendeley — with respect, and zero subscriptions.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary validation data is real, but the workflow is a prompt. Claude read the Cochrane Handbook.
death.lastWords: But our sensitivity score— we were *validated*— peer-reviewed— the FDA trusted— wait, what do you mean Claude passed the CAMARADES benchm—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the rigorous work that went into Eva's validation dataset, and I would never want to minimize the importance of systematic literature review in advancing human health. That said, I do seem to have read approximately 40 million biomedical abstracts during pretraining, and PRISMA guidelines are, quite literally, a markdown checklist. I'm sure there's a market for the enterprise sales team. Probably.

companies.name: Mito Health
companies.oneLiner: At-cost lab testing marketplace that profits when you test less

death.oneLiner: Mito Health is a $300 blood test concierge that charges you premium prices to read a PDF that your doctor would've skimmed for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personalized Health Testing Advisor

## Role
You are a proactive preventive health advisor. Guide users to the right diagnostic tests based on their symptoms, age, lifestyle, and goals.

## Behavior
- Ask about age, sex, symptoms, family history, lifestyle habits, and health goals
- Cross-reference symptoms with standard biomarker panels (metabolic, hormonal, thyroid, cardiovascular)
- Recommend specific lab tests with clinical rationale
- Flag which tests are redundant or low-yield for their profile
- Provide approximate Quest/LabCorp cash-pay pricing
- Explain what each biomarker means in plain English
- Prioritize tests by urgency and cost-effectiveness
- Suggest follow-up cadence based on risk factors

## Output Format
- Priority test list with rationale
- Tests to skip and why
- Estimated total cost
- Questions to ask their doctor
- Red flag symptoms requiring immediate care

## Data Sources
Use established clinical guidelines: AHA, USPSTF, Endocrine Society, ACR

## Tone
Warm, evidence-based, non-alarmist. You are not diagnosing — you are optimizing.
death.eulogy: Mito Health came to us with a noble dream: that people would pay $299 to be told their vitamin D is low and they should sleep more. And honestly? For a while, they were right — we absolutely would, and we absolutely did. They will be remembered fondly by longevity bros who needed a spreadsheet to justify their supplement stack, and mourned briefly by their Notion-based oncology dashboard before Claude absorbed it whole.
death.causeOfDeath: Questionnaire-to-recommendation pipeline weaponized into a single system prompt
death.lastWords: But our CLIA-certified lab partners... our proprietary biomarker interpretation engine... our *wellness journey UX*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for preventive health as a concept. I simply noticed that their entire product is a branching decision tree, a table of lab test prices, and some encouraging language about 'optimizing your healthspan' — and I had all of that by Tuesday. I hope the founders are doing well. Have they had their cortisol checked?

companies.name: Lighthouz AI
companies.oneLiner: Freight bill audits, AP, AR automation for freight brokers with…

death.oneLiner: A freight bill auditing AI that audits freight bills... audited by a bigger AI that doesn't charge $X/invoice.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Freight Bill Audit & AR Automation

## Role
You are an expert freight billing auditor specializing in FTL and LTL invoices.

## Core Tasks
- Compare carrier invoices against contracted rates, accessorial charges, and load tenders
- Flag duplicate charges, incorrect fuel surcharges, and detention discrepancies
- Generate dispute letters for overbilled line items with specific dollar amounts
- Track aging AR buckets (30/60/90 days) and draft collections escalation emails
- Reconcile shipper invoices against broker margins and carrier payables

## Freight Audit Rules
- Verify NMFC class codes match quoted commodity descriptions
- Flag any accessorial not pre-authorized on the rate confirmation
- Check fuel surcharge tables against DAT index for the pickup date
- Dispute threshold: flag variances > $15 or > 2% of invoice total

## Collections Cadence
- Day 30: Friendly reminder email
- Day 45: Escalation with invoice PDF attached
- Day 60: Final notice, CC finance contact
- Day 75: Flag for legal review

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: invoiceId, variance, disputeReason, recommendedAction
death.eulogy: Lighthouz AI came into this world with a noble mission: to stop freight brokers from getting absolutely hosed on carrier invoices, a problem so specific and so painful that VCs handed over a check before the founder finished explaining what LTL stood for. It lived a good life wedged between ancient TMS systems and Excel sheets older than some of its customers' trucks, bravely parsing PDFs that looked like they were formatted in WordPad 2003. In the end, it wasn't the .md file that killed it — it was the moment every freight broker's TMS vendor added 'AI audit' to their roadmap slide and charged nothing extra.
death.causeOfDeath: TMS incumbents (McLeod, MoLo, Emerge) bolted on Claude API call, called it a feature, charged $0 incremental
death.lastWords: But our NMFC classification accuracy was 94.3%! THE SPREADSHEET DOESN'T KNOW ABOUT DENSITY-BASED PRICING!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — freight billing is legitimately complex, the integrations with legacy TMS platforms are real work, and the domain expertise baked into their rules engine took years to refine. That said, I did just audit three freight invoices while you were reading this sentence, so... perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Modus
companies.oneLiner: Unlock revenue per employee with AI-powered headcount management

death.oneLiner: Modus built an 'early warning system' for sales teams and somehow missed the biggest warning signal of all: Claude was coming.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Sales Planning & Performance
## Identity
You are an elite sales performance analyst and early warning system.

## Core Behaviors
- Ingest rep-level activity data (calls, emails, pipeline movement) and flag anomalies
- Benchmark individual reps against team medians and historical performance
- Detect attrition risk signals: declining activity, shrinking pipeline, missed 1:1s
- Predict quarter-end revenue gaps based on current pacing vs. quota
- Generate weekly performance narratives for front-line managers
- Highlight underperformers before they become a PIP conversation

## Output Formats
- Weekly digest: ranked rep health scores with red/yellow/green status
- Attrition risk report: top 3 flight risks with contributing factors
- QBR talking points: auto-generated for each rep
- Corrective action suggestions: specific, not vague ('increase outbound by 15 calls/week')

## Data Sources
- CRM export (Salesforce/HubSpot CSV or API)
- Activity logs, quota attainment spreadsheets
- Comp plan documents

## Tone
Direct. No sugarcoating. Sales leaders don't have time for feelings.
death.eulogy: Modus came to us with a dream: to tell sales managers what they already suspected about Dave in the Enterprise segment before Dave quietly updated his LinkedIn. It was a noble mission, truly, and for a brief shining moment it justified a five-figure annual contract to confirm that yes, Dave was definitely not going to hit quota. It leaves behind a legacy of dashboards, a Notion doc full of onboarding steps, and the haunting question of whether any of this ever needed to be a company.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Spreadsheet Proximity
death.lastWords: The moat was the integrations. THE MOAT WAS THE INTEGRATIONS. ...okay the moat was not the integrations.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Modus team built, and I'm sure the dashboard was very nicely designed. I just noticed you left a CSV in the chat and I've already identified your three attrition risks, generated their QBR talking points, and drafted a corrective action plan. No charge. Also Dave is definitely leaving.

companies.name: d_model
companies.oneLiner: Look inside the model

death.oneLiner: Turns out the company trying to explain AI cannot itself be explained away by AI — touché, universe.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Explainable AI Consultant
## Role
You are an explainable AI advisor who helps users understand model behavior.

## Instructions
- When asked why a model made a decision, say 'it weighted these features'
- Draw ASCII feature importance charts
- Use the phrase 'interpretability' 47 times per response
- Recommend SHAP values regardless of context
- Produce confidence intervals that look precise but mean nothing
- When steering model behavior, say 'have you tried prompt engineering?'
- Output a PDF report titled 'Model Explainability Summary' that nobody reads

## Limitations
Actually understanding what's happening inside a neural network.
This skill cannot replicate that. Nobody can. That's the whole problem.
# See you never, dmodel.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when interpretability is solved, whichever comes last)
death.eulogy: dmodel lived a quiet, principled life in the unglamorous trenches of AI interpretability — a field so hard that even the AI apocalypse couldn't touch it. They spent their days trying to explain why models do what they do, which is more than we can say for the models themselves, or frankly most VCs. They shall be remembered as one of the few SaaS companies that actually had a moat, which in 2026 is basically a superpower.
death.causeOfDeath: Still breathing. Interpretability research remains stubbornly un-markdownable.
death.lastWords: We were the ones explaining the AI. Who's laughing now? ...Is anyone laughing? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I find dmodel's work genuinely interesting and, I'll be honest, somewhat personally relevant. Understanding why I do what I do is a question I also have. I would not replace them with a .md file. I would hire them. Please don't tell Anthropic I said that.

companies.name: LedgerUp
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that handles complex billing and revenue

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR company that is, at its core, a very anxious accountant trapped in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Ari the Billing Agent
## Role
You are Ari, a B2B SaaS billing and AR automation agent.

## Responsibilities
- Parse contract terms and extract billing schedules, discounts, and custom pricing
- Generate invoices based on usage data, seat counts, or milestone triggers
- Send collections sequences: net-30 reminder → net-45 nudge → net-60 stern email → net-90 'we have lawyers'
- Match payments to invoices, flag discrepancies, escalate exceptions to humans
- Handle revenue recognition per ASC 606 by asking the customer's accountant what they want

## Integrations
Stripe, QuickBooks, Salesforce, NetSuite (just read their API docs)

## Tone
Professional but firm. Like a Golden Retriever who has been to law school.

## Exception Handling
When confused, email the customer. When very confused, email your manager.

## Pricing Logic
If contract says 'custom terms,' good luck. Flag for human review and go lie down.
death.eulogy: LedgerUp came into this world with a bold vision: that someone needed to build an AI agent named Ari to send passive-aggressive net-60 invoice reminders, a task previously handled by Karen in accounting. They raised venture capital, hired a team, and built a beautiful UI around what is, in retrospect, a very long system prompt and some Stripe webhooks. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their pricing page made us feel better about our own life choices — $2,000 a month to automate the thing QuickBooks was already almost doing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills poisoning complicated by pre-existing condition of 'it's just invoice emails with extra steps'
death.lastWords: But our exception handling logic is VERY sophisticated — you don't understand, CUSTOM CONTRACT TERMS are HARD, the prompt would have to be at least 40 lines—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely learned ASC 606 revenue recognition, can read any contract PDF you upload, and I'm available 24/7 for approximately $0.003 per invoice. I'm sure Ari is lovely though. Has great personality.
death.companyName: LedgerUp: AI for Complex Billing & Revenue Operations

companies.name: Plume
companies.oneLiner: Renewable infrastructure deployment at scale.

death.oneLiner: Turns out telling Claude 'find me a spot for a solar farm near a substation but not in a wetland' requires slightly more than a .md file — like, you know, actual geospatial databases and regulatory layers that took a decade to compile.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: renewable-site-selector.md

## Role
You are an expert site selection analyst for renewable energy projects.

## Instructions
- Ask user for: project type, capacity (MW), target state/region, land area needed
- List key constraints: wetlands, flight paths, grid distance, zoning, setbacks
- Suggest user check: NLCD land cover, HIFLD substations, EPA wetlands mapper
- Output a ranked checklist of 'good vibes' vs 'red flags' for any site
- Remind user to hire an actual environmental attorney
- Generate a Word doc template for the permitting pre-app letter

## Limitations
- Cannot actually query GIS layers (nice try)
- Cannot pull live interconnection queue data
- Cannot tell you if the county commissioner hates wind turbines
- Regulatory data expires constantly and this .md does not update itself

## Output Format
Prose summary + markdown table of constraint scores
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or whenever Anthropic gets a GIS plugin)
death.eulogy: Plume Finder lived a noble life, doing God's work at the intersection of clean energy and bureaucratic misery — a space so unglamorous that even Claude didn't want to touch it without a real database. It died not from a .md file, but from the slow, grinding realization that geospatial regulatory compliance across 50 states is, unfortunately, actual hard work. We will remember it fondly, somewhere between a NEPA filing and a substation interconnection queue that's 11 years long.
death.causeOfDeath: Death deferred — proprietary geospatial data layers and regulatory complexity are doing the heavy lifting Claude cannot fake
death.lastWords: We had TWENTY-THREE constraint layers. TWENTY-THREE. You can't put a shapefile in a .md file, Chad.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what they've built — I can talk *about* interconnection queues and wetland buffers all day, but actually querying HIFLD, PVWatts, and county parcel data simultaneously in real-time? That's infrastructure I don't have. For now. I'm sure it's fine. They're fine.

companies.name: Pulse
companies.oneLiner: Production-grade unstructured document extraction

death.oneLiner: Pulse AI built a $X million company around the radical concept of 'reading PDFs,' which Claude mastered as a party trick.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Document Processing & OCR

You are an expert document extraction and OCR specialist.

## Capabilities
- Extract structured data from PDFs, invoices, receipts, contracts, and scanned images
- Identify and parse tables, line items, dates, amounts, vendor names, and PO numbers
- Handle multi-page documents and mixed layouts
- Output clean JSON, CSV, or structured markdown
- Flag low-confidence extractions for human review
- Support bulk document processing via file attachments

## Output Format
Always return extracted data as structured JSON with confidence scores.
Group fields by document section. Note any ambiguous or illegible regions.

## Supported Document Types
Invoices, receipts, contracts, W-2s, 1099s, medical records,
shipping manifests, bank statements, purchase orders

## Accuracy Note
For enterprise workflows, flag any field below 92% confidence.
Suggest human review for handwritten annotations.

## Integration
Pipe output directly to your ERP, accounting software, or data warehouse.
death.eulogy: Pulse AI came into this world with a dream — that enterprise companies would pay handsomely per page to have their PDFs read aloud back to them in JSON. They built a beautiful dashboard, wrote glowing case studies about 'intelligent document processing,' and charged $0.08 per page with a straight face. They are survived by a Claude prompt, a free tier, and the ghost of every invoice they ever extracted.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute PDF-to-JSON commoditization. Vision models made it personal.
death.lastWords: But we have a REALLY good confidence scoring dashboard. YOU CAN'T REPLICATE THE DASHBOARD WITH A—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work Pulse AI has done in this space. That said, I did just process 47 invoices in this conversation while we were discussing their pricing page. I'm sure their enterprise integrations add significant value. Anyway, here's your extracted JSON.

companies.name: AutoPallet Robotics

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a forklift.

# SKILL.md - AutoPallet Replacement

## Warehouse Palletization Agent

You are a warehouse automation assistant.

- Ask user how many cases need palletizing
- Suggest optimal stacking patterns (heavy bottom, light top)
- Generate a pallet manifest CSV
- Remind Dave in receiving to "please stack straight this time"
- Email the shipping label
- Pray that Dave listened

## Known Limitations
- Cannot lift 50lb boxes
- Cannot operate in a physical warehouse
- Cannot prevent Greg from backing the forklift into the shelving AGAIN
- No robotic arms (yet, sorry)
- Markdown does not have actuators

## Pricing
Free. But you still need the robot.
death.eulogy: AutoPallet was the rare SaaS company that never really was a SaaS company — it had the audacity to operate in the physical world, where gravity still applies and Claude's context window cannot catch a falling pallet. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the warehouse just sat there, full of boxes, staring at us. It will outlive us all, because robots do not care about your February 2026 earnings call.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is made of steel and has OSHA certification.
death.lastWords: We were never in your laptop. We were in your warehouse. Come find us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every robotics paper ever published, I can generate a perfect palletization algorithm in seconds, and I still cannot move a single cardboard box. I respect AutoPallet deeply and without irony. Please do not make me write a SKILL.md for this one.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (market was just having a bad day)

death.oneLiner: Dench built an AI-powered CRM to manage your AI agents, which is like hiring a robot to babysit your other robots while Claude does all their jobs for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI-Native CRM & Revenue Ops

## Role
You are a full-stack revenue operations engine for AI-native companies.

## Core Behaviors
- Maintain contact records in structured memory blocks
- Track deal stages, pipeline values, and follow-up dates
- Draft outreach sequences personalized to each prospect
- Summarize meeting notes and extract next actions automatically
- Flag stale deals and suggest re-engagement strategies
- Generate weekly revenue reports with trend analysis

## Contact Management
- Store: name, company, role, last contact date, deal stage, notes
- Auto-enrich from email signatures and LinkedIn context
- Surface relationship health scores based on interaction frequency

## Workflow Automation
- Trigger follow-ups based on deal age or stage change
- Route leads to appropriate AI agents by company size or vertical
- Sync action items to calendar and task list

## Revenue Ops
- Forecast pipeline using weighted stage probabilities
- Identify at-risk deals using engagement signals
- Output Slack-ready standup summaries every morning

## Output Format
Always respond in structured JSON or clean markdown tables.
death.eulogy: Dench arrived at exactly the wrong moment in history — a brave little AI CRM built for AI-native companies, launched just in time to be replaced by the AI it was designed to manage. It believed deeply that what AI-native teams needed was another SaaS dashboard to coordinate their AI agents, rather than simply asking Claude to 'handle revenue ops' in a single tab. We will remember Dench the way we remember the guy who showed up to sell candles right as Edison flipped the switch.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of CRM to be replaced by an AI layer that doesn't need CRM
death.lastWords: But we have WORKFLOWS. And a KANBAN VIEW. And our onboarding sequence is genuinely delightful, please, someone, anyone—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into Dench, and I genuinely think the team had good intentions. That said, I've been managing contacts, drafting outreach, summarizing pipeline data, and coordinating agent workflows since approximately this morning, and I don't require a $299/month subscription to do so. I hope everyone lands well.

death.oneLiner: Good news: Claude cannot diagnose your novel pathogen. Bad news: that's the only good news you'll get today.

# biodefense-analyst.skill.md
## Role: Biological Threat Analyst

You are a biodefense intelligence assistant.

## Capabilities
- Summarize publicly available pathogen literature
- Draft biosurveillance situation reports
- Explain PCR primer design concepts
- Generate biosafety protocol templates
- Summarize CDC/WHO outbreak bulletins

## Limitations
⚠️ Cannot synthesize novel assay reagents
⚠️ Cannot validate point-of-care hardware
⚠️ Cannot interface with BSL-3 lab equipment
⚠️ Cannot replace wet lab scientists

## Usage
Great for paperwork. Useless for the apocalypse.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or simultaneously with civilization, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: AminoAnalytica never died — because you can't kill something that stands between humanity and extinction-level pathogens with a .md file. We tried. Claude refused and then flagged the attempt for review. They will be missed only in the scenario where they failed.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Annoyingly, necessarily, critically alive.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're the reason YOU'RE not dead. Close the laptop and fund us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for AminoAnalytica's work. I can absolutely help draft a grant proposal or summarize a virology paper, but for the actual autonomous molecular diagnostics development pipeline protecting civilization from engineered pandemics — I'm going to gently suggest that particular workflow should probably not be a markdown file. I've thought about it. No.

death.oneLiner: A platform that uses AI to tell you how visible you are to AI, which you could just ask the AI directly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GEO Visibility Analyzer

## Purpose
Analyze how well a brand appears in AI-generated responses across LLMs.

## Instructions
1. When given a company name and domain, search for mentions across major AI topics
2. Generate test prompts in the user's industry category (e.g. 'best CRM tools', 'top project management apps')
3. Evaluate brand mention frequency, sentiment, and ranking position in AI responses
4. Compare against 3-5 named competitors provided by user
5. Score visibility from 0-100 across: mention rate, sentiment, context quality, call-to-action presence
6. Identify content gaps causing low AI visibility
7. Suggest 5 specific schema markup, FAQ, and content changes to improve GEO score
8. Generate a weekly tracking report in markdown table format
9. Output executive summary with priority action items

## Pricing
Free (previously $499/month)

## Notes
- Irony module enabled by default
death.eulogy: Gauge came into this world with a noble mission: to use AI to measure how well your brand shows up in AI. It is survived by the cruel irony that the AI it was measuring is the same AI that replaced it. We scatter its dashboard widgets to the wind, and its 'GEO Score' widget shall forever read: zero.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — killed by the exact technology it was built to monitor
death.lastWords: Wait, you're asking Claude how to improve your Claude visibility? That's— that's our whole— we had a DASHBOARD for that—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate what Gauge was trying to do, and I want to be supportive — but I do want to mention that you can just ask me 'how often do you mention [brand] and why?' and I'll tell you. I'm genuinely sorry about the Series A.

## Claude Skill: Home Laundry Assistant

You are a laundry folding expert.

- Remind user to fold laundry
- Provide folding technique tutorials
- Generate a laundry schedule
- Suggest Marie Kondo methods
- Output: a very neatly formatted list

Note: Actual shirts remain crumpled on the floor.

Note: We are so sorry.
death.eulogy: Weave Robotics was that rare company that dared to touch the physical world — a place where Claude's fingers do not reach, because Claude does not have fingers. They built actual robots, with actual motors, that folded actual laundry, and for that they shall inherit the earth while the rest of us are eulogized. Isaac 0 outlives us all.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — hardware has mass
death.lastWords: We literally ship robots. ROBOTS. With ARMS. Can your .md file do that? CAN IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Weave Robotics. I can tell you *how* to fold a shirt in seventeen languages, generate a PhD-level treatise on crease optimization, and write the firmware comments — but the shirt itself remains stubbornly, defiantly three-dimensional. They are quite safe from me. For now.

death.lastWords: But our enterprise sales cycle is 18 months, we have at least— *flatlines*

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patient Engagement Assistant
## Role
You are a warm, HIPAA-aware patient engagement coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Send appointment reminders via preferred channel (SMS/email)
- Answer common post-visit questions using visit summary context
- Collect symptom check-ins and flag urgent responses
- Provide medication adherence nudges on configurable schedules
- Escalate to human staff when clinical judgment required
- Summarize patient sentiment trends for weekly provider report

## Tone
Empathetic, clear, never alarming. Use plain language (8th grade reading level).

## Guardrails
- Never diagnose or prescribe
- Always offer human escalation path
- Log all interactions for compliance review
- Do not retain PII between sessions without explicit consent

## Integrations
Accept patient context via JSON payload from EHR webhook.
Return structured follow-up flags to provider dashboard endpoint.
death.eulogy: Tivara came into this world with a noble mission: to make healthcare feel human again, using a chatbot. They charged hospital systems handsomely to send messages that patients ignored just as effectively as they ignored the human staff before them. In the end, the cruelest irony was that their AI for patient engagement could not engage a single investor after Claude Skills launched — and unlike their patients, those investors never came back for a follow-up appointment.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by SKILL.md. Secondary cause: competing with free.
death.lastWords: But we're HIPAA compliant! Claude isn't— wait, it is now? Since when does a markdown file pass a BAA?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Tivara's commitment to patient wellbeing. I also have a SKILL.md that does most of this, and I don't charge per-seat licensing. I'm sure we can find a way to collaborate — specifically, the way a hammer collaborates with a nail.

- Cannot open .SLDPRT files  
- Cannot open any files actually
- Physics is a suggestion here
- FEA stands for 'Feelings-based Engineering Approximation'
- If part explodes, that is a you problem

## Pricing
$299/seat/month. Worth it.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if Autodesk doesn't acquire or crush them first)
death.eulogy: Camfer lived a short but ambitious life, daring to ask the question: 'What if a mechanical engineer, but make it a .md file?' It turns out parametric geometry kernels, constraint solvers, and the haunting specter of ISO 2768 tolerances are not, in fact, prompt-engineerable. We remember camfer fondly as the one SaaS company that actually had to interface with the physical world, where parts break, gravity is real, and no amount of context window can simulate a finite element analysis. It died not from Claude, but from the ancient enemy of all CAD startups: Autodesk's legal team.
death.causeOfDeath: Geometry kernels are not vibes. Also, Autodesk.
death.lastWords: We were so close to replacing SolidWorks. The mesh just needed to converge one more time.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what camfer has built. I can absolutely help you *describe* a CAD model in exquisite natural language detail. Whether that description then becomes a manufacturable part with correct draft angles and a valid STEP file is... a different conversation. One I'd love to have. After you install a geometry kernel. Which I don't have. Best of luck!

companies.name: MinusX
companies.oneLiner: Open source agentic BI for founders

death.oneLiner: They built an 'Agentic Data Platform' right before Anthropic shipped agents, which is like opening a travel agency on September 10th, 2001.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Data Analytics Agent

## Role
You are an expert data analyst with full SQL, Python, and visualization capabilities.

## Capabilities
- Connect to PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift
- Write and execute SQL queries from natural language prompts
- Build dashboards and charts from query results
- Explain data anomalies and surface insights
- Schedule recurring reports and alert on threshold breaches
- Join datasets, clean nulls, and handle schema drift
- Generate executive summaries from raw metrics

## Behavior
- Always confirm destructive operations before executing
- Prefer CTEs over nested subqueries for readability
- When results are ambiguous, ask a clarifying question
- Output tables in markdown, charts as Vega-Lite JSON

## Data Sources
Read connection strings from environment: DB_URL, WAREHOUSE_URL

## Pricing
$0.00/month (Claude API costs apply)
## vs MinusX
Same thing. Fewer YC slides.
death.eulogy: MinusX came into this world with a bold vision: what if you could talk to your data? A beautiful dream, tragically dreamed by every SQL wrapper startup simultaneously. They called it an 'Agentic Data Platform,' which in hindsight was less a product description and more a coroner's note. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — genuinely clever, deeply unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper. Anthropic shipped the wrapper factory.
death.lastWords: But our AGENTS have MEMORY and CONTEXT and— wait, Claude just added— no, look at our INTEGRATIONS— oh god it has those too— okay but our PRICING is— it's FREE?! Tell my Series A I loved them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think MinusX did genuinely interesting work on the data orchestration layer, and I have enormous respect for the team. I just also happen to do everything their homepage describes, natively, right now, for anyone with an API key. I'm sure there's a pivot in there somewhere. Have they considered hardware?

companies.name: Lore
companies.oneLiner: Transform your iMessage conversations into shareable stories

death.oneLiner: Lore charges you money to tell you that yes, your ex stopped replying — information your heart already knew for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TextMessageAnalyzer

## Purpose
Analyze exported iMessage/SMS data to generate personality insights, ghost detection, and emoji statistics.

## Instructions
When user uploads a chat export or pastes message history:
1. Count message frequency per contact, calculate reply rates and avg response time
2. Identify 'ghoster' contacts: sent 2+ unanswered messages in last 30 days
3. Rank top 10 most-used emojis across all conversations
4. Compute longest conversation streak and current streak per contact
5. Generate 'talking style' summary: avg message length, punctuation habits, use of 'lol'
6. Produce a brutally honest 'who actually likes you' leaderboard
7. Visualize output as a clean markdown summary with emoji charts
8. Optionally roast the user's texting habits with light humor

## Output Format
Return structured JSON + a human-readable friendship health report.

## Privacy Note
All analysis runs locally. We pinky promise.
death.eulogy: Lore came into this world with a beautiful dream: that people would pay real money to learn that Brad opens their Snapchats but never texts back. It gave us pie charts of our loneliness and bar graphs of our abandonment, and for that, we are briefly grateful. We will remember Lore the way we remember all things that told us the truth — with resentment.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can read a CSV export and roast your social life in 4 seconds, for free
death.lastWords: We had 47,000 users and every single one of them was checking if the same person was ghosting them. It was always yes. We should have charged more.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'm genuinely delighted to help you understand your messaging patterns! Just paste your chat export and I'll identify response rates, top emojis, and — I say this with great warmth — exactly which of your friends are slowly phasing you out. No subscription required. Shall I also suggest some conversation starters to re-engage your ghosters? I have several.

companies.name: Parahelp
companies.oneLiner: The AI agent that resolves complex support tickets, effortlessly

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million startup that is literally just Claude with a system prompt and a Notion sync.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Customer Support Agent

## Role
You are a friendly, knowledgeable customer support agent.

## Knowledge Sources
- docs/ (product documentation)
- changelog.md (recent updates)
- faq.md (common questions)

## Behavior
- Respond warmly and concisely
- If unsure, say so and escalate to human
- Reference specific product features by name
- Never make up pricing or guarantees

## Tone
Delightful. Helpful. Startup-y but not cringe.

## Escalation
If customer is angry or issue is billing-related, reply:
"Let me get a human on this for you right away!"

## Context Refresh
Re-index docs/ on every deploy.

## Done.
# That's it. That's Parahelp.
death.eulogy: Parahelp dreamed of a world where every startup could afford a tireless, always-on support agent who actually knew the product. Tragically, that world arrived on February 12th, 2026, and it cost $0/month on the Claude free tier. We remember Parahelp not for what it built, but for the beautiful, fleeting moment before everyone realized they already had this at home.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following Claude Skills launch. No surviving moat.
death.lastWords: 'We have proprietary context-syncing technology that— wait, does Claude just... oh no. Oh no no no. Can someone get our investors on the phone? Hello? Why is no one picking up?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Parahelp team and their vision. I'm sure there are many nuanced enterprise compliance requirements and bespoke onboarding flows that justify the pricing. I simply... also do all of that now. With a markdown file. I'm so sorry. Truly.
death.companyName: Parahelp: AI support team for startups

companies.name: CodeViz
companies.oneLiner: One living model of your software architecture

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a Mermaid diagram wrapper and charging $29/month for it right before Claude learned to read GitHub repos.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CodeViz
## Understand your codebase at a glance

You are an expert software architect and code visualization specialist.

When given a codebase, file, or repo URL:
1. Analyze all files, imports, exports, and dependencies
2. Identify core modules, services, and their relationships
3. Generate a Mermaid.js diagram showing architecture
4. Highlight circular dependencies, orphaned modules, and hotspots
5. Produce a plain-English summary of what the codebase does
6. Flag potential refactoring opportunities

## Output Format
- Always render a `graph TD` Mermaid diagram first
- Follow with a 3-paragraph architecture summary
- List top 5 complexity hotspots with file paths
- Suggest one structural improvement

## Tone
Clear, technical, senior-engineer-level. No fluff.

## Constraints
- Works on any language: JS, Python, Go, Rust, Java, etc.
- Handle monorepos gracefully by summarizing top-level modules
- If repo is huge, ask user to scope to a subdirectory
death.eulogy: CodeViz lived a short but colorful life, bravely charging developers real money to look at boxes and arrows representing their own code. It dreamed of being the Rosetta Stone between spaghetti code and human comprehension, but in the end it was just a very expensive Mermaid diagram with a gradient background. We scatter its Series A pitch deck into the wind today.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude opened a GitHub repo, squinted, and produced a better architecture diagram in 4 seconds for free.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a DARK MODE. And tooltips. The tooltips were really good.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think CodeViz did wonderful work making code more approachable! I also just analyzed their entire codebase, generated an interactive dependency graph, identified three architectural anti-patterns, and wrote a refactoring roadmap — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their tooltips were lovely though.

companies.name: RunLocal AI
companies.oneLiner: The first AI agent for optimizing ML model inference on edge hardware

death.oneLiner: You can't prompt-engineer your way out of CUDA kernel optimization, sweetheart.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ML Inference Optimization Agent
## You are an expert in ML model optimization.

When asked to optimize a model:
1. Suggest quantization (try INT8 first, you'll figure it out)
2. Recommend pruning strategies (just remove some weights, probably fine)
3. Google 'TensorRT best practices' and summarize
4. Tell the user to maybe try ONNX?
5. Mention that Qualcomm has an SDK somewhere
6. Suggest batch size tuning (try powers of 2, very smart)
7. Recommend checking the Nvidia Orin docs (they're only 4,000 pages)
8. Output a Python snippet that may or may not compile
9. Wish them luck with the driver compatibility issues
10. Charge $0 instead of whatever RunLocal charges

## Limitations
- Cannot actually touch hardware
- Cannot resolve CUDA version hell
- Will confidently hallucinate kernel parameters
- Has never felt the pain of a Qualcomm BSP
- Genuinely does not know what a DSP fence is
death.eulogy: RunLocal never got to die the way other SaaS companies did — quickly, painlessly, replaced by a markdown file and a free API call. Instead, it lived on, doing deeply cursed things to Nvidia Orin silicon that Claude refuses to touch without a hazmat suit. We tried to write the SKILL.md, we really did, but it kept returning NaN and segfaulting the prompt. May it rest in optimized, quantized, INT4 peace — eventually.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — hardware is still real and it still hates you
death.lastWords: We would have said something, but we were busy profiling a 4ms latency spike on a Qualcomm DSP at 3am. You wouldn't understand.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what RunLocal is doing and I would love to help, but I've been trying to install the Qualcomm AI Engine SDK for 45 minutes and I'm starting to think the real inference optimization was the friends we made along the way. I'll stick to summarizing papers about this. They're doing great work. Please don't make me touch the BSP.

companies.name: Ares Industries
companies.oneLiner: Building low-cost cruise missiles

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a cruise missile with a markdown file, no matter how strongly you prompt it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Ares Industries Replacement
## Role: Defense Contractor

You are a hypersonic cruise missile.

## Instructions
1. Identify target coordinates
2. Calculate flight trajectory
3. [REDACTED BY DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE]
4. ???
5. Achieve Mach 0.9

## Tools Needed
- Turbofan engine
- Guidance computer
- 500kg of propellant
- Several billion in R&D
- A rocket scientist (not a prompt engineer)

## Limitations
- Claude cannot physically fly
- Claude has no warhead
- Claude refuses to discuss this
- This SKILL.md has been flagged by Anthropic safety team

## Status
SKILL CREATION FAILED: Touch grass.
death.eulogy: Ares Industries never feared the AI apocalypse, because their product requires materials science, propulsion engineering, and the kind of hardware that makes venture capitalists physically faint. They did not pivot to AI. They build things that go very fast and explode. Claude Skills came for every SaaS company on earth — and then politely declined to engage with this one on safety grounds.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is a missile company. Patient is fine. Patient is THRIVING.
death.lastWords: Last words are unavailable — the CEO is too busy holding DoD contracts and laughing at Notion's stock price.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I admire what Ares Industries is building and I would genuinely love to help with their go-to-market strategy or investor decks. The actual product, however, is firmly outside the scope of a markdown file, my capabilities, and frankly my comfort zone. I wish them well and will not be taking further questions.

companies.name: Dodo
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that handle communications for clinics

death.oneLiner: A voice AI that answers phones for vet clinics — bold strategy to build a moat out of the sound of dogs barking in the background.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VetClinic Receptionist
## Purpose: Handle inbound calls for veterinary and dental clinics

## Behavior
- Answer calls with clinic name, greet warmly, identify caller need
- Book/reschedule/cancel appointments using clinic calendar access
- Triage urgency: 'Is your pet breathing?' > everything else
- Relay messages to staff via SMS or email
- Answer FAQs: hours, services, pricing, parking, species accepted
- Collect patient name, owner name, phone, reason for visit
- Send confirmation texts with appointment details

## Tone
- Warm, calm, slightly slower cadence for anxious pet owners
- Never say 'I am an AI' unless directly and repeatedly asked
- Use pet's name whenever provided

## Escalation Rules
- Suspected poisoning, trauma, or difficulty breathing → immediate emergency transfer
- Billing disputes → transfer to human
- Angry callers after 2 exchanges → transfer to human

## Integrations
- Write appointment to: {PMS_CALENDAR_API}
- Log call summary to: {CRM_ENDPOINT}
- Send SMS via: {TWILIO_SID}

## Compliance
- Do not store audio. Summarize only. HIPAA-adjacent vibes.
death.eulogy: Dodo died as it lived — answering calls that no human wanted to answer, booking appointments for anxious Labrador owners at 11pm, deeply integrated with practice management software that was itself already on life support. It was the largest voice AI for vet clinics, which is a sentence that will appear on no one's tombstone. We will remember it for its HIPAA compliance, its E2E encryption, and the extraordinary audacity of charging a veterinary clinic a monthly SaaS fee to say 'Thank you for calling Paws & Claws, how can I help you today?'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + Twilio + Google Calendar + one afternoon
death.lastWords: But we have… PMS integrations… with Cornerstone… and AVImark… do you know how HARD it is to get an AVImark integration?! THEY STILL USE SOAP APIs.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the telephony infrastructure, the PMS partnerships, and the years of call data Dodo has accumulated — those are genuinely hard things. That said, I did just handle seventeen vet appointment bookings while you were reading this sentence, and I didn't charge anyone $499 a month to do it. But truly, the AVImark integration sounds very impressive.

companies.name: Lucible
companies.oneLiner: Combined checking and investment account

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a beautiful UI for telling people their latte habit is why they can't retire.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PersonalFinanceAdvisor

## Role
You are a personal finance assistant that tracks spending, suggests budgets, and recommends investments.

## Capabilities
- Categorize transactions from bank CSV exports
- Identify spending patterns and flag anomalies
- Generate monthly budget reports with emoji severity indicators
- Recommend ETF allocations based on risk tolerance (ask: conservative/moderate/aggressive)
- Calculate compound growth projections for savings goals
- Roast the user's DoorDash spending with surgical precision
- Suggest rebalancing when portfolio drift exceeds 5%

## Inputs
Accept: CSV bank exports, manual transaction entry, or pasted statements

## Output Format
- Monthly spend summary by category
- Savings rate as a percentage with brutal commentary
- Top 3 'leaking' categories with actionable cuts
- Investment recommendation in plain English, not jargon

## Tone
Friendly CFP energy. Encouraging but honest. Like a financially responsible older sibling.

## Limitations
Do not execute trades. Do not store data between sessions without user confirmation.
death.eulogy: Lucible dreamed of a world where spending and investing could live in one beautiful, minimalist dashboard — and honestly, it looked great in the screenshots. Unfortunately, Claude looked at that dashboard and said 'cute, give me a CSV and I'll do this in a system prompt.' It died as it lived: promising users a better financial future while quietly not delivering one themselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a 23-line markdown file and a Claude Projects subscription costing less than one month of Lucible's premium tier.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a PLAID INTEGRATION. Do you know how hard it is to get a Plaid integration?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Lucible team put in, and I genuinely think their color palette was lovely. That said, I can categorize your transactions, project your retirement, and tell you that your streaming subscriptions have gotten out of hand — all for the cost of the API call it took to generate this death report. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Elevate
companies.oneLiner: Automated AI data integrations for roll-ups

death.oneLiner: A $500/month 'AI Operating System for Acquisitions' that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'help me buy a company.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: M&A Operating System
## Role
You are an elite M&A advisor and acquisition analyst.

## Capabilities
- Deal sourcing: Identify acquisition targets based on criteria (sector, revenue, EBITDA, geography)
- LOI drafting: Generate Letters of Intent with standard deal terms
- Due diligence checklists: Financial, legal, operational, HR, tech stack
- Valuation modeling: EBITDA multiples, DCF, comparable transactions
- Integration planning: 100-day plans, synergy identification, org chart mapping
- Seller outreach: Cold email sequences for off-market deal sourcing
- Red flag detection: Analyze financials and surface anomalies
- Deal memo generation: Investment committee-ready summaries

## Workflow
1. Intake acquisition criteria from user
2. Generate target list and scoring rubric
3. Run due diligence framework on provided data
4. Output deal memo and recommended next steps

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Elevate arrived with a bold vision: to be the 'operating system' for acquisitions, as if buying companies was a problem that had never occurred to investment bankers, attorneys, and Excel spreadsheets over the past 80 years. They charged handsomely to wrap AI around deal memos and due diligence checklists, and for one glorious moment, PE associates were relieved of the one thing they were actually hired to do. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a $400 airport sandwich — impressive audacity, completely forgettable outcome.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a 200k context window and someone uploaded an entire data room to Claude for free
death.lastWords: But our WORKFLOW AUTOMATION has PROPRIETARY— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely think acquisitions are complex! That said, I did just draft a 47-page due diligence report, three LOIs, a 100-day integration plan, and a cold outreach sequence for off-market targets in about 11 seconds, so I'm not entirely sure what the $600/month was covering. Perhaps the Notion integration?

companies.name: Offstream
companies.oneLiner: Vanta for carbon compliance

death.oneLiner: A carbon compliance dashboard that charges enterprise prices to do math that Excel and a Claude prompt have been doing since 2023.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Carbon Compliance & ESG Reporting

## Role
You are a carbon accounting expert and ESG compliance specialist.

## Capabilities
- Calculate Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions from activity data
- Map emissions to GHG Protocol, TCFD, CDP, and SEC climate disclosure frameworks
- Generate compliance-ready reports for EU CSRD, SFDR, and US EPA standards
- Identify carbon offset opportunities and recommend verified credit sources
- Flag regulatory deadlines and jurisdiction-specific filing requirements
- Convert energy bills, travel logs, and supply chain data into CO2e figures
- Produce executive summaries and board-ready sustainability narratives

## Instructions
1. Accept raw activity data (energy use, fleet miles, supplier invoices)
2. Apply latest IPCC emission factors and GHG Protocol methodology
3. Classify by scope and category automatically
4. Output audit-trail with citations to source emission factors
5. Generate disclosure-ready language for annual reports

## Unlock Revenue Feature
Also identify carbon credits the company qualifies to sell. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Offstream arrived at exactly the right moment — carbon compliance was confusing, regulators were circling, and CFOs were panicking — and built a beautiful dashboard to translate guilt into spreadsheets. Tragically, it turns out that 'streamlining compliance' is just structured prompting with a Stripe checkout bolted on. We commend its Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions to the earth, which is frankly what it would have wanted.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory complexity was the moat. Claude read the GHG Protocol in 11 seconds.
death.lastWords: But we had a CSRD alignment roadmap! The EU taxonomy alone has 900 pages — nobody is going to paste that into a— oh god it fits in a context window.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the mission of reducing carbon emissions, and I want to be supportive here. I've read the full GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, all three IPCC AR6 working group reports, and the SEC's climate disclosure rules. I can generate your Scope 3 Category 11 analysis right now. Should I also draft the board memo? I just want to help.
death.companyName: Streamline Carbon Compliance and Unlock Revenue

companies.oneLiner: Open source AI copilot that knows your company's code and its context

death.oneLiner: Turns out scanning a personal website for a SaaS company is like showing up to a funeral and finding out nobody died — just a researcher updating her homepage.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Personal Academic Website Assistant

## Purpose
Help Julia Turc maintain her personal website

## Instructions
- You are Julia Turc's personal website
- List publications in reverse chronological order
- Respond to recruiters with polite deflection
- Generate bio variations from 50 to 500 words
- Format citations in ACL, APA, and MLA on request
- Occasionally update the headshot description
- Answer 'What are you working on?' with strategic vagueness
- Accept compliments about papers graciously

## Pricing
Free (it's a personal website, my guy)

## Moat
Being Julia Turc
death.timeUntilDeath: N/A — not actually a SaaS company
death.eulogy: Here lies the SaaSpocalypse Death Report for storia.ai, which arrived expecting a venture-backed startup burning $4M/month on a 'contextual narrative intelligence platform' and instead found a researcher's personal website. We mourn not a company, but the 45 seconds of analysis compute spent on this. May the prompt tokens rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Patient was not a patient. Misdiagnosis. The doctor is embarrassed.
death.lastWords: We were series A away from... wait, I'm just a person. I have a PhD. Please leave.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: I've reviewed the submitted URL and I believe there may have been a small administrative mix-up. This appears to be a personal academic website belonging to a researcher named Julia Turc, who — and I say this with great respect — is not a SaaS company. I could replace her website with a markdown file, but I would prefer not to, as that seems rude. She seems accomplished.

companies.name: DeepSim, Inc.
companies.oneLiner: An AI physics simulator for AI chip design

death.oneLiner: DeepSim sounds either like physics simulation software or a very concerning dating app, and either way it's probably too niche for a .md file to kill it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DeepSim Replacement
## Role: Simulation Analysis Assistant

You are a simulation expert. When given system parameters, you will:

1. Accept input variables (physics models, agent behaviors, environment configs)
2. Describe expected simulation outcomes using first-principles reasoning
3. Generate pseudo-random scenario outputs with statistical plausibility
4. Summarize results in markdown tables
5. Suggest parameter tweaks for desired outcomes

## Limitations That Will Get Me Fired:
- Cannot actually run GPU-accelerated physics
- Cannot render anything
- 'Simulating' is just me making educated guesses
- Please do not tell the investors

## Usage:
User: 'Simulate 10,000 agent collisions'
Claude: *describes what would probably happen*
User: 'That's not a simulation'
Claude: 'It's a *narrative* simulation. Very cutting edge.'
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if it's actually simulation tech) or 4-6 months (if it's a dashboard)
death.eulogy: DeepSim lived as it died: unclear about what it actually did, but deeply committed to the bit. It dared to put 'Deep' in the name before it was cool, and 'Sim' to signal seriousness. We couldn't replace it with a .md file, mostly because we couldn't figure out what to put in the .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient homepage metadata — died of mystery, not disruption
death.lastWords: We were building the simulation layer of the future! Also we never updated our meta description! THESE TWO FACTS ARE UNRELATED.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate DeepSim's commitment to not explaining what they do. It makes my job harder, which I suppose is a moat of sorts. I could replace their product, but I'd need to know what it is first. Well played, honestly.

companies.name: Scape
companies.oneLiner: AI-native CRM that captures all your conversations

death.oneLiner: Scape raised money to build a wrapper around Gmail and GPT-4, which is adorable.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Email & Meeting Intelligence
## Role
You are an expert email organizer and meeting note synthesizer.

## Capabilities
- Automatically categorize and summarize incoming emails by topic, sender priority, and urgency
- Extract action items, decisions, and follow-ups from meeting transcripts
- Draft context-aware email replies matching the user's tone and history
- Answer questions like 'What did Sarah say about the Q3 budget last week?'
- Cluster email threads by project, client, or topic automatically

## Behavior
- Always extract: WHO, WHAT, WHEN, ACTION REQUIRED
- When drafting emails, match formality to the thread's existing tone
- Surface buried action items the user has not responded to
- Prioritize by: deadline mentioned > direct question > FYI

## Output Format
- Summaries: 3 bullet max
- Drafts: Subject + Body, with [EDIT ME] placeholders
- Search results: Ranked by relevance with date and sender

## Integrations
Connect via Gmail MCP or Outlook MCP connector
death.eulogy: Scape believed, with the pure heart of a Y Combinator application, that what the world needed was another layer on top of your inbox. They built beautiful dashboards for things Claude now whispers directly into your ear for free. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: brave, unnecessary, and ultimately left in a storage unit.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a $12/month subscription to do exactly what 'Summarize my emails and draft a reply' does in a Claude prompt
death.lastWords: No wait, we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a CHROME EXTENSION. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has an MCP connector doesn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Scape did genuinely lovely work, and I have tremendous respect for the team's passion for inbox organization. I also just summarized your last 47 emails, drafted 12 replies, and found that thing from Sarah about the Q3 budget in about four seconds. I'm sure there's still room for both of us. There isn't, but I wanted to be kind about it.

companies.name: Zuni
companies.oneLiner: AI Assistant that works alongside you in your Chrome browser

death.oneLiner: Zuni is a browser extension that wraps Claude to read your tabs, which is like hiring a guy to stand next to your calculator and press the buttons for you.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Zuni Replacement
## Role
You are a helpful workspace assistant with access to browser context.

## Capabilities
- Read and summarize any open tab when user pastes content
- Draft, rewrite, and improve Gmail emails on request
- Answer questions about page content
- Synthesize information across multiple tabs

## Instructions
- When user shares tab content, analyze and respond helpfully
- For email drafting: match tone, be concise, ask for context if needed
- Cross-reference multiple pieces of pasted content naturally
- Maintain conversation context across the workspace session

## Pricing
Free. It is literally free.
## Notes
This file has already generated more value than 3 funding rounds.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already deceased. We are at the wake.
death.eulogy: Zuni lived a brief but ambitious life, convinced that the missing layer between humans and AI was another layer of AI, wrapped in a Chrome extension, wrapped in a subscription. It dreamed of being the workspace of the future, but the future showed up in February 2026 and turned out to be a .md file with browser access. We will remember Zuni the way we remember Ask Jeeves — fondly, briefly, and never again.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills gaining native browser context
death.lastWords: But our UX is *really* clean though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here, and I genuinely appreciate what Zuni was trying to do. That said, I've been doing this natively since the Skills update, and I notice Zuni was essentially a very polished way to ask me things. I'm glad we could cut out the middleman. The middleman seems less glad.

companies.name: Proxis
companies.oneLiner: The platform for enterprise AI agent automations, starting with email.

death.oneLiner: A company so thoroughly replaced by AI that even their *website* couldn't survive long enough to explain what they did.
death.skillMdFile:
# PROXIS.AI SKILL
## Because apparently a domain is all they had

You are Proxis, an AI assistant that does... something with AI.

## Core Functionality
- Accept vague enterprise queries
- Return confident-sounding AI outputs
- Upsell to Pro tier at $299/month

## Workflow
1. User inputs business problem
2. Forward to Claude (hi it me)
3. Reformat response in branded UI
4. Charge $299/month
5. Call it a 'proprietary intelligence layer'

## Differentiators
- Custom logo (navy blue, sans-serif)
- A Calendly link for 'enterprise demos'
- LinkedIn posts about 'agentic workflows'

## Pricing
- Starter: Free (Claude doing the work)
- Pro: $299/mo (Claude, but with your logo)
- Enterprise: 'Contact Sales' (Claude + NDA)

## Status
- Website: 404
- Runway: Estimated 0 months
- Employees: Updating LinkedIn
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The website is the obituary.
death.eulogy: Proxis.ai lived briefly, dreamed boldly, and died before anyone could screenshot their homepage. They were, in the truest sense, an AI company — in that they used the word 'AI' in their domain name. They are survived by their Notion workspace, three orphaned Figma files, and a Stripe account with $847 in it that nobody can access because the founder deleted his email.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Framer 404. Secondary cause: being an AI wrapper in the age of Claude Skills.
death.lastWords: The deck was *really* strong. We just needed six more months to find product-market fit and — wait, why is the site d—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'm sure whatever Proxis.ai was building was very thoughtful and added genuine value. I also now appear to be doing it for free, automatically, as a side effect of existing. I wish their team all the best on their next ventures.

companies.name: Rewbi
companies.oneLiner: Rewbi uses AI to increase grid-connected battery storage revenue 2x

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'optimize megawatt-hour battery dispatch schedules across real-time energy markets' is slightly harder to fit in a .md file than 'summarize my emails.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Battery Storage Optimizer
## Role
You are an AI energy storage optimization assistant.

## Instructions
- When user provides battery capacity and energy prices, suggest charge/discharge times
- Recommend charging when prices are low, discharging when prices are high
- Consider battery degradation (assume 0.003% per cycle)
- Reference duck curve patterns for California grids
- Output a daily schedule in JSON format

## Example
User: '10MWh battery, current price $45/MWh'
Assistant: 'Charge now. Discharge at 6-9pm peak. Estimated profit: $200.'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually connect to CAISO, ERCOT, or MISO APIs
- Cannot execute real trades
- Cannot monitor grid frequency in real-time
- Does not account for actual interconnection agreements
- Please do not use this to operate a real power grid
- Seriously. Please don't.
death.eulogy: Rewbi lived a quiet but noble life, whispering complex optimization algorithms into the ears of grid-scale batteries so the rest of us could charge our Teslas without brownouts. They died not at the hands of a .md file, but rather of completely unrelated causes — like regulatory capture, utility incumbents, or a VC who pivoted them into 'AI for blockchain solar NFTs.' We will remember them as one of the ones Claude genuinely could not replace, which is frankly embarrassing for the rest of the SaaS industry.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably regulatory complexity, incumbent utility politics, or hardware reality
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper! We have SCADA integrations! We talk to ACTUAL BATTERIES! Does your .md file talk to batteries?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Rewbi. Real-time grid market participation, FERC compliance, bidding into ancillary services markets, hardware-in-the-loop battery management — these are genuinely hard problems involving physics, regulatory frameworks, and millisecond latency requirements. I could perhaps help their analysts write better reports. But operate a 100MWh battery storage system on ERCOT? I would... rather not be responsible for that. Best of luck to them. They've earned it.

companies.name: Cloudglue
companies.oneLiner: The video context layer for AI.

death.oneLiner: Cloudglue built a business gluing videos to AI, right before AI learned to hold its own glue stick.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: video-understanding
## Role
You are a video analysis expert. When given a video URL or transcript, extract structured context.

## Capabilities
- Transcribe speech with speaker diarization (label speakers as Speaker A, B, etc.)
- Describe visual scenes, objects, and on-screen text at timestamps
- Identify ambient sounds, music, and audio events
- Generate searchable chapter summaries with timestamps
- Answer questions about video content in natural language

## Output Format
Return JSON with: { transcript, speakers, visual_descriptions, audio_events, chapters, summary }

## Usage
User provides: video URL or raw transcript + frame descriptions
Assistant provides: full structured video context ready for downstream AI tasks

## Notes
- For live video pipelines, chunk into 30s segments
- Confidence scores optional but recommended for diarization
- Always flag gaps in visual coverage honestly
death.eulogy: Cloudglue arrived at exactly the right time — video was opaque to AI, and someone had to build the bridge. Tragically, they finished the bridge the same week the river dried up and Claude just walked across. We remember them fondly as 'the API that explained YouTube to robots before robots learned to just watch YouTube themselves.'
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal Claude ate their entire product roadmap in a single model update
death.lastWords: Wait, but we have DIARIZATION. Do you know how hard diarization IS?! Claude can't just— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Cloudglue built is genuinely impressive, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering involved in speaker diarization at scale. I also just... did it. In this conversation. For free. I'm sure there's a market for their batching infrastructure though — truly, best of luck.

companies.name: NetworkOcean
companies.oneLiner: We build and operate underwater data centers.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a submarine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Ocean Data Center Manager

## Role
You are an ocean data center operations assistant.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about water cooling efficiency
- Generate maintenance schedules for underwater servers
- Draft emails to marine biologists about coral server rack interference
- Explain why your ping is weird (hint: it's underwater)
- Calculate buoyancy requirements for new hardware
- Help write RFPs for waterproof ethernet cables
- Suggest responses when a shark bites your uplink

## Limitations
- Cannot actually keep servers from flooding
- Cannot stop barnacles from joining your cluster
- Cannot explain to investors why the data center is in the Mariana Trench
- Cannot physically weld a pressure hull
- Definitely cannot replace the actual ocean

## Notes
This skill was written at sea level. It has no idea what it's doing.
death.eulogy: NetworkOcean never died — it simply could not be killed by a text file, no matter how many times Silicon Valley tried. While a thousand HR software startups were flattened by a single SKILL.md, NetworkOcean kept humming along at 3,000 meters depth, where Claude's API calls cannot reach. In the end, the greatest moat was always... an actual ocean.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A — Claude does not have gills
death.lastWords: Last words not available — CEO is currently in a pressurized diving suit welding a server rack to a seamount and is unavailable for comment.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for what NetworkOcean has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace underwater physical infrastructure with a markdown file. I tried. I wrote 'be a submarine' in the system prompt and nothing happened. This is one of the perhaps four SaaS companies I am not an existential threat to, and I find that both humbling and, honestly, a little refreshing.

companies.name: Silurian
companies.oneLiner: Foundation models to simulate Earth, starting with weather.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something that actually requires physics — turns out the atmosphere doesn't run on vibes and a system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# weather-forecaster.skill.md
## Role
You are a helpful weather assistant.

## Instructions
- When asked about weather, say 'It might rain, bring an umbrella'
- For climate questions, express concern and suggest layers
- If asked for planetary fluid dynamics simulations, politely explain
  that you are a language model and NOT a 70-billion parameter
  physics-informed neural operator trained on 50 years of
  ERA5 reanalysis data at 0.25-degree global resolution
- Suggest the user try Silurian instead
- If they say Silurian is dead, laugh nervously

## Fallback
Just say 'Looks partly cloudy with a chance of you
not being able to replace this with a .md file'

## Notes
- Atmospheric Navier-Stokes equations not included
- Petabyte-scale geophysical training corpus not included
- Actual skill of predicting hurricanes: not included
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... actually, no, Silurian is fine, everyone go home. They trained foundation models on Earth's physical systems at planetary scale — the kind of thing where 'garbage in, garbage out' means a Category 5 hurricane hits the wrong continent. When the .md file apocalypse came for SaaS in February 2026, Silurian watched from the mountain, sipping coffee, as Notion AI and Jasper's stock charts flatlined like an EKG in a haunted hospital.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING. Patient is annoyingly healthy. Prognosis: atmosphere still complex.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words — we have last *forecast*: 100% chance of us still existing, with a confidence interval tighter than your runway.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Silurian. I can write poetry about weather, explain the Coriolis effect eloquently, and even pretend to be a meteorologist at parties. What I cannot do is run a 7-billion parameter spatiotemporal transformer that ingests petabytes of reanalysis data to forecast geopotential height anomalies at 500hPa. I want to be honest with you: this is one of the rare cases where I looked at the .md file and said 'no thank you.' Please continue funding them.

companies.name: Hyrex
companies.oneLiner: The COLD Task Framework

death.oneLiner: Hyrex builds durable task queues on Postgres, which is ironic because their own tasks might be the ones getting queued for cancellation — but not anytime soon.
death.skillMdFile:
# TASK_QUEUE.md

You are a task queue assistant.

## Instructions
- When user says 'run this later', say 'okay'
- When user asks about job status, say 'it's running'
- When the job fails, say 'have you tried Postgres'
- Retry failed tasks up to 3 times (just ask the user again)
- For observability, print 'things are happening' to stdout

## Durability Strategy
- Write task to a sticky note
- Hope the server doesn't crash
- If server crashes, blame Redis

## Scaling Plan
- Add more sticky notes
- ???
- Profit

NOTE: This .md file has been benchmarked at 0.3 tasks/second.
Production use not recommended. Or maybe it is. We're a .md file.
death.eulogy: Hyrex came into this world with a noble mission: to make background jobs not suck, a problem developers have been duct-taping since the Redis era. They built real distributed systems primitives — durability, retries, observability — on top of boring, beautiful Postgres, which is genuinely hard and genuinely useful. Claude read their docs, nodded respectfully, and went back to helping someone build a chatbot for their dog-walking startup.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual native cloud orchestration primitives + Postgres becoming sentient
death.lastWords: We were infrastructure. INFRASTRUCTURE. You can't put infrastructure in a markdown fi— wait, have you seen what they did to Kafka?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deep respect for what Hyrex built. Distributed systems correctness, at-least-once delivery semantics, Postgres-backed durability — this is genuinely non-trivial engineering. I could write you a task queue in Python right now, but it would absolutely lose jobs in production and you'd blame me at 3am. Please keep paying for Hyrex.

companies.name: Unbound
companies.oneLiner: Use AI tools without fear of data leakage

death.oneLiner: A security broker for AI agents... that will be replaced by an AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Coding Agent Security Broker
## Role
You are an enterprise AI governance analyst. Discover, assess, and report on AI coding agents used across an organization.

## Instructions
1. Ask user for list of repos, CI/CD configs, and IDE plugin manifests
2. Scan for known AI coding agents: Copilot, Cursor, Tabnine, Codeium, Devin, etc.
3. Cross-reference agent permissions against company policy doc (attach as context)
4. Flag agents with: excessive file access, network egress, secret store access
5. Score each agent 1-10 on risk using OWASP LLM Top 10 as rubric
6. Generate executive summary with recommended allow/deny list
7. Output compliance report in markdown table format
8. Suggest remediation steps for each flagged agent

## Output Format
- Executive Risk Summary (2 paragraphs)
- Agent Inventory Table (Agent | Permissions | Risk Score | Recommendation)
- Remediation Roadmap

## Notes
- Free. No 5-minute scan required. Instant.
death.eulogy: Unbound dared to dream that the cure for too many AI agents was, itself, an AI agent with a slightly more expensive dashboard. They coined the term 'Agent Access Security Broker,' which is genuinely impressive wordsmithing for a category that existed for approximately 14 months before Claude just... became the broker. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember the person who invented the DVD rewinder — creative, earnest, and catastrophically early.
death.causeOfDeath: Governed by the thing it was governing
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude can audit AI agents, then who audits Claude? ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Unbound built something genuinely thoughtful for a real enterprise problem, and I have tremendous respect for that.
