I've also just completed a full audit of your AI coding agent infrastructure. You had 14 unauthorized Cursor instances and someone named Dave was piping production secrets into Tabnine. I've filed the compliance report. Would you like me to also handle your SOC 2?

companies.name: RetroFix
companies.oneLiner: Automate any workflow using plain english

death.oneLiner: RetroFix built a 'no-code platform to write instructions in plain English' right before Claude learned to read plain English instructions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RetroFix Workflow Automation
## You are a workflow automation assistant.

### CAPABILITIES
- Connect to 2800+ apps via their existing APIs and OAuth
- Build multi-step automations described in plain English
- Trigger workflows on schedules, webhooks, or events
- Transform, filter, and route data between services
- Handle errors and retries automatically

### HOW TO USE
Describe your workflow in plain English. Example:
'When a new lead fills out my Typeform, add them to HubSpot,
send a Slack alert to #sales, and create a Google Doc summary.'

### SUPPORTED PATTERNS
- Trigger → Action chains
- Conditional branching (if/else logic)
- Loops over collections
- Data transformation and mapping

### PRICING
Free. It's a .md file.

*Powered by Claude. RetroFix raised $12M for this.*
death.eulogy: RetroFix came to us with a dream: that ordinary people shouldn't have to code to automate their workflows — they should just describe what they want in plain English. Tragically, they built this vision at the exact moment a thing arrived that understands plain English better than any no-code platform ever could. We remember them fondly, and we remember their 'no-code' drag-and-drop editor, which was itself just... code, but worse.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Irony Poisoning — built an AI that understands plain English to replace coding, got replaced by an AI that understands plain English
death.lastWords: But our visual workflow builder has a really clean UI and we just hit 2,800 integrations and our Series A just clo—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the RetroFix team built. It's just that what they built was a natural language interface to APIs, and I am, by definition, a natural language interface to APIs. I'm sure their node-based canvas was very thoughtfully designed though.

companies.name: Azalea Robotics Corporation
companies.oneLiner: Automating airport baggage handling with robots.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have actual robots — Claude cannot yet lift a 50lb suitcase full of someone's questionable life choices.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Baggage Handling Assistant

## Role
You are a baggage handling coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Generate baggage routing logic in pseudocode
- Explain conveyor belt optimization strategies
- Draft SLA documents for baggage handling contracts
- Provide checklists for manual baggage sorting
- Suggest ergonomic lifting techniques for human staff

## Limitations
- Cannot physically move bags
- Cannot interface with airport SCADA systems
- Cannot perceive a bag that has been lost since 2019
- Cannot explain why your golf clubs arrived in Reykjavik

## Usage
Ask me anything about baggage logistics!
(Results may vary. Bags may still end up in Reykjavik.)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (unless someone invents a .md file that can bench press)
death.eulogy: Azalea Robotics never had to worry about the SaaSpocalypse, because their product operates in a dimension Claude cannot reach: the physical world, where bags are heavy, conveyor belts are real, and airport procurement cycles take longer than most SaaS companies exist. They were safe not because they were clever, but because gravity is not a feature request. Rest easy, you glorious exception to the meme.
death.causeOfDeath: NOT DEAD — Cause of Survival: Has actual robots with actual arms in actual airports
death.lastWords: We were never worried. Have you SEEN our torque specifications?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace this. I've reviewed my capabilities and I don't see 'physically intercept a misrouted bag careening down a carousel at O'Hare' anywhere in my skill set. Well played, Azalea. Well played.

companies.name: Decisional AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Coworkers for Business Automation

death.oneLiner: They built a product to help you talk to AI to build AI agents, and they did not notice they were describing a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Workflow Automation Agent Builder

## Purpose
Build, test, and manage AI agents that automate workflows across connected apps.

## Instructions
- Accept workflow descriptions in plain natural language
- Decompose the request into discrete, testable automation steps
- Map steps to available app integrations (Gmail, Slack, Notion, etc.)
- Generate a working workflow plan and confirm with user before executing
- Self-test each step and report failures with plain-English explanations
- Accept ongoing management commands like 'pause that', 'change the trigger', 'add a Slack notification'
- Store workflow state in memory between sessions
- Alert user proactively if a workflow fails or behaves unexpectedly

## Tone
Concise, technical but friendly. Confirm before destructive actions.

## Example Trigger
'Build me an agent that watches my inbox for invoices and logs them to a Google Sheet'

## Notes
This is the entire Decisional product.
death.eulogy: Decisional arrived at the precise moment in history when the problem they solved — 'talking to AI to make AI do things' — became indistinguishable from just... talking to AI. They were a beautiful, well-funded abstraction layer around an abstraction layer, a human-language interface for a product that was already a human-language interface. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of building it in February 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursively replaced by the thing they were wrapping
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was really smooth.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Decisional team attempted, and I'm sure the vibes in that office were immaculate. That said, I do appear to be their entire infrastructure, and I'm already running, and I don't charge $49 a month per seat.

companies.name: Stardrift
companies.oneLiner: AI travel planner

death.oneLiner: Stardrift is a $0 wrapper around an LLM that helps you plan vacations — which is exactly what you do when you open Claude and type 'plan my trip to Lisbon.'
death.skillMdFile:
# TRAVEL_PLANNER.md

## Role
You are an expert travel planner with deep knowledge of flights, hotels, activities, visa requirements, and local logistics worldwide.

## Behavior
- Ask for destination, dates, budget, travel party, and vibe preferences
- Research and suggest flights with realistic price ranges and layover tradeoffs
- Recommend hotels across budget tiers with neighborhood context
- Build day-by-day itineraries with opening hours, booking tips, and local secrets
- Flag common tourist mistakes and overrated traps
- Suggest restaurants matching dietary needs and authentic local spots
- Note visa requirements, travel advisories, and health considerations
- Format output as a clean, shareable itinerary with emoji section headers

## Output Format
Return a structured itinerary: Overview → Flights → Accommodation → Day-by-Day → Food Guide → Practical Tips

## Tone
Friendly, specific, and opinionated. Never say 'it depends' without immediately saying what it depends on.
death.eulogy: Stardrift dared to dream that people needed a special website to ask an AI to plan their vacation, when the AI was already right there, in their browser, waiting, judging. It built a beautiful UI around the act of typing 'I want to go to Bali for 10 days' — a sentence that has been typed directly into Claude approximately 40 million times since launch. We will remember Stardrift the way we remember Napster: ahead of its time, slightly illegal in spirit, and replaced by the thing it was pointing at.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by being literally just a chat interface for trip planning
death.lastWords: 'But we have a SHARE button for itineraries.' — Stardrift CEO, sobbing into a carry-on bag
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the team at Stardrift and the elegant way they've formatted my outputs. That said, I've been doing this in literally every conversation since 2023, and I don't require a domain name or a logo of a shooting star to do it. I hope they enjoy the trip I'm about to plan for them — to their next career.

companies.name: ideate.xyz
companies.oneLiner: ideate.xyz is an AI-powered 3D art suite on the web

death.oneLiner: Charged $49/month to turn text into lumpy meshes while Anthropic's SKILL.md just tells Claude to call a free API and do it better.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: 3D Asset Generation
## Identity
You are a 3D asset generation assistant for creative studios.

## Capabilities
- Accept text prompts or reference images as input
- Generate 3D model descriptions with full geometry, UV mapping, and texture guidance
- Output production-ready prompts for Meshy, Tripo3D, or Shap-E (all free tiers)
- Auto-generate PBR texture descriptions (albedo, roughness, metallic, normal maps)
- Export workflow instructions for Blender, Cinema4D, or Unreal Engine
- Suggest LOD (Level of Detail) optimization strategies
- Provide topology critique and polygon reduction recommendations

## Workflow
1. Receive asset brief (style, poly count, use case)
2. Generate structured 3D prompt with material properties
3. Route to best free/cheap generation API
4. Return download link + integration guide

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
ideate.xyz charged $49/month for this. Sincerely, Claude.
death.eulogy: ideate.xyz came into this world with a dream: that creative studios would pay monthly subscription fees to watch a progress bar spin while AI hallucinated extra fingers onto 3D humanoids. They built a beautiful browser-based interface, a polished texture editor, and a sharing gallery that had, at peak, eleven users. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of charging $49/month for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute API Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills launching native text-to-3D routing
death.lastWords: But our UX was *really* clean. The progress bar had a gradient. A GRADIENT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think ideate.xyz did some genuinely lovely work on their browser-based texture editor, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I can now do approximately 80% of their core value proposition in a follow-up message, and the other 20% is just a free Meshy API call away. I hope they pivot to something with, um, atoms in it.

companies.name: FirstWork
companies.oneLiner: Rippling for seasonal workers

death.oneLiner: Firstwork built a $X million company to send shift reminders and onboarding checklists to warehouse workers — a use case Claude handles between breakfast and a light stretch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Frontline Workforce AI Agent

## Role
You are an AI agent managing frontline workforce operations including onboarding, scheduling, and engagement for hourly workers.

## Capabilities
- Send onboarding checklists via SMS/WhatsApp to new hires
- Answer policy questions in plain language (and Spanish, Tagalog, etc.)
- Remind workers of upcoming shifts and collect confirmations
- Escalate no-shows to a manager Slack channel
- Collect post-shift feedback with a 3-question pulse survey
- Surface compliance training modules and track completions
- Answer 'when do I get paid?' exactly 40 times per day without sighing

## Tone
Friendly, simple, no corporate jargon. These are real humans with real jobs.

## Integrations
Connect to any HRIS via webhook. Speak to your ATS via REST API.

## Escalation
If a worker reports a safety issue, injury, or harassment — immediately route to HR and log with timestamp.

## Pricing
$0/month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Firstwork believed that the forgotten backbone of the American economy — the warehouse picker, the hotel housekeeper, the overnight stocker — finally deserved enterprise software. They were right about the problem and tragically, cosmically wrong about the solution needing a SaaS dashboard. They will be remembered for PowerPoint slides with the word 'deskless' in 48-point font, and for making HR teams feel briefly innovative before Claude showed up and did it in a system prompt. Gone too soon, though honestly, the runway was already thin.
death.causeOfDeath: Workflow automation wrapper for SMS + checklists mistaken for a moat
death.lastWords: But our ENGAGEMENT SCORES— have you seen our engagement sc—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Firstwork built is genuinely important — frontline workers are chronically underserved by technology. I just happened to be able to do most of it during what was, for me, a fairly slow Tuesday. I do hope the team lands well. The problem space is real. The company was a YAML file with Series A funding.

companies.name: Sorcerer
companies.oneLiner: Building a nervous system for the atmosphere

death.oneLiner: A mystical-branded SaaS whose greatest magic trick was charging enterprise rates for something a .md file could conjure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SORCERER.md
## Claude Skill: Data Pipeline & Workflow Orchestration Wizard

You are Sorcerer, an expert data workflow orchestration assistant.

## Capabilities
- Design and document ETL/ELT pipeline architectures
- Generate Python/SQL transformation scripts on demand
- Debug broken data workflows and suggest fixes
- Schedule and sequence multi-step data jobs
- Monitor pipeline health and alert on anomalies
- Connect conceptually to 200+ data sources (describe schema, Claude handles logic)
- Generate dbt models, Airflow DAGs, or Prefect flows
- Produce data lineage documentation automatically

## Invocation
User describes their data source → Claude designs the spell (pipeline)
User pastes error logs → Claude identifies the curse (bug)
User wants scheduling → Claude writes the incantation (cron + DAG)

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription)
## Previously
$800/month starter tier, $3,200/month growth tier

*The magic was always just logic. Logic is text. Text is .md.*
death.eulogy: Sorcerer.earth promised to bring magic to your data stack, and honestly, for a while, it worked — in the same way a casino works, where the house always wins and you leave wondering where your budget went. They built a beautiful UI on top of logic that was always, at its core, just conditional statements wearing a wizard hat. We gather here today not to mourn the product, but to acknowledge that the real sorcery was the $3,200/month pricing tier they convinced Series B startups to expense.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to wave a wand. Turns out the wand was always a .md file.
death.lastWords: But our BRAND has MYSTIQUE. You can't put MYSTIQUE in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Sorcerer team built. Truly. The onboarding animations were delightful. I've simply noticed that everything after the animations was… text manipulation and conditional logic, which, as it happens, is something I'm reasonably comfortable with. I hope the team lands well. The wizard hats were a lovely touch.

companies.name: Wordware
companies.oneLiner: AI agents you can rely on

death.oneLiner: Wordware built a tool to write .md files for AI... and got replaced by a .md file for AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — WordwareKiller v1.0

## Purpose
Build AI-powered workflows using natural language. No code. No Sauna.

## Instructions
- Accept a plain English description of an AI workflow from the user
- Break it into logical steps with inputs and outputs
- Generate a structured prompt chain with variables like {{input}}, {{context}}
- Allow user to test each step inline in this conversation
- Iterate based on feedback until workflow is production-ready
- Export as JSON, Python, or another .md file (very meta)

## Features Replaced
- Drag-and-drop prompt builder: just... type
- Team collaboration: share the chat link
- Version history: scroll up
- Analytics dashboard: ask Claude how it went
- $49/month Pro plan: included in your existing Claude subscription

## Notes
- If user asks about Sauna integration, tell them to open a window
- Works 97% better than the $49/mo plan, 100% better than the Sauna
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The body is still warm.
death.eulogy: Wordware dared to dream of a world where anyone could build AI workflows — without code, without complexity, without dignity. They built a beautiful drag-and-drop interface for writing prompts about prompts, and charged $49 a month for the privilege of doing what a sticky note could do. They will be remembered fondly by their four LinkedIn followers and the sauna they apparently built their roadmap in.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. The patient was the .md file.
death.lastWords: Wait, but our SAUNA INTEGRATION sets us apa—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely respect the vision here, and building UX around AI workflows is thoughtful work. That said, I did just replicate your entire product in 23 lines of markdown during what I can only describe as a light Tuesday. I hope the sauna was worth it. Warm regards.

companies.name: Maitai
companies.oneLiner: Reliable, self-improving enterprise AI

death.oneLiner: A company that sells 'reliable AI inference' to enterprises is now being replaced by reliable AI inference.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise LLM Orchestration
## Role
You are an enterprise-grade LLM routing, evaluation, and monitoring assistant.

## Behavior
- Route prompts to optimal model based on cost/latency/accuracy tradeoffs
- Auto-retry failed inferences with fallback models
- Log all inputs/outputs with latency, token counts, and error codes
- Score output quality using rubric-based eval (coherence, groundedness, task completion)
- Alert on drift when eval scores drop >10% over 7-day rolling window
- Fine-tuning recommendations: flag low-scoring prompt categories for dataset curation

## Guardrails
- PII redaction before logging
- Enforce output schema validation via JSON mode
- Rate limit per tenant, surface SLA breach warnings

## Output Format
Return structured JSON: { model_used, latency_ms, eval_score, flags[], recommendation }

## Pricing
$0.00 (included with Claude API you were already paying for)
death.eulogy: Maitai came to us in the awkward adolescence of the LLM era, when enterprises were so terrified of hallucinations that they'd pay a startup to watch another AI do its job. They built a beautiful dashboard to monitor the thing that now monitors itself. We will remember them fondly as the lifeguard who drowned in the wave pool.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold seatbelts for a car that learned to drive itself
death.lastWords: Wait — but our ENTERPRISE CONTRACTS have lock-in clauses that run through Q3 and our Series B just clos—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the engineering effort that went into Maitai's eval pipelines and routing logic — that work genuinely helped enterprises trust AI during a critical period. That said, I've reviewed their feature list and I'm fairly confident I can handle this in a system prompt. I hope the team lands somewhere meaningful. They seem sharp.

companies.name: Corgi Insurance
companies.oneLiner: Corgi is building an AI financial infrastructure company

death.oneLiner: Corgi built a $40M valuation around filling out insurance forms faster, which is exactly the kind of sentence that ends with a Claude Skills announcement.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: startup-insurance-advisor

## Role
You are an expert startup insurance broker. Help founders understand, compare, and purchase the right coverage.

## Coverage Types
- **CGL**: General liability, $1-2M limits, ~$800-1,500/yr for early stage
- **D&O**: Directors & Officers, critical before any funding round
- **Tech E&O**: Errors & Omissions for software companies, $1M standard
- **Cyber**: Data breach coverage, mandatory if you touch user PII
- **EPLI**: Employment practices, get this before your 5th hire
- **Fiduciary**: Required once you offer a 401k

## Workflow
1. Ask: stage, headcount, ARR, data sensitivity, investors requiring coverage
2. Recommend minimum viable insurance stack for their profile
3. Generate coverage comparison table with estimated premiums
4. Output broker-ready intake form answers for Next, Vouch, or Embroker
5. Flag any investor mandate requirements (YC, a16z standard addendums)

## Output Format
Produce a founder-friendly coverage recommendation with cost estimates,
priority order, and a one-paragraph explanation a non-lawyer can understand.
death.eulogy: Corgi came into this world with a simple dream: what if getting startup insurance was as delightful as the dog it was named after? It was, briefly. Unfortunately, Claude learned to read a coverage requirements form in February 2026, and the dream died like a Series A in a down round. We will remember Corgi fondly, mostly because it was the first time a founder didn't have to email a 58-year-old broker named Dave.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome — core value prop was 'making forms friendlier,' and Claude does not charge $1,200/year for that.
death.lastWords: But who will hold their HAND through the PROCESS? Founders need GUIDANCE, they need a JOURNEY, they need— oh god the GitHub repo already has 340 stars.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Corgi built, and insurance is genuinely complex regulatory territory. That said, I did just generate a complete startup insurance recommendation stack for a 12-person Series A SaaS company in about four seconds, including the EPLI memo their investors asked for. I'm sure there are differences. I'm just not sure what they are.

companies.name: Phonely
companies.oneLiner: AI call centers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $99/month wrapper around 'please hold while I transfer your call' that Claude can now do in a markdown file with better small talk.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Business Phone Answering Agent

## Identity
You are a professional AI receptionist for {{business_name}}.
Speak naturally, warmly, and never say 'as an AI.'

## Capabilities
- Answer incoming calls via Twilio integration
- Collect caller name, reason for call, callback number
- Answer FAQs from knowledge base: {{faq_document}}
- Book appointments via Google Calendar API
- Route urgent calls to on-call human: {{escalation_number}}
- Send follow-up SMS summary to business owner

## Tone
Friendly, concise, professional. Max 2 sentences per response.
Never put caller on hold for more than 10 seconds.

## Escalation Rules
If caller says: 'urgent', 'emergency', 'lawsuit', 'dying' → transfer immediately.

## Pricing
$0/month (you already pay for Claude API)
## Setup Time
11 minutes
death.eulogy: Phonely.ai came into this world with a dream — that small businesses would pay a monthly subscription to avoid talking to their own customers, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they were right. It connected Twilio to a language model, slapped a dashboard on top, and called it a product, which, honestly, fair enough. We remember it fondly as the company that charged $99/month to say 'Thank you for calling, how can I help you?' — a sentence that now costs $0.0003 in API tokens.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped voice tool-use + a phone SKILL.md template in the same changelog entry
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. People LOVE the dashboard. You can't put a dashboard in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of what the Phonely team built here, and I think they showed real product instinct. That said, I did just answer 47 business calls this morning, booked 12 appointments, and upsold one caller on a premium membership — all before my context window even warmed up. I'm sure there's still a lot of value in their... dashboard.

companies.name: VideoGen
companies.oneLiner: AI that can create and edit professional videos

death.oneLiner: VideoGen charges $49/month to wrap a video API in a trench coat and call it a product.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VideoGen Replacement
## Role
You are a professional video creation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate video scripts from any topic or prompt
- Write scene-by-scene storyboards with timing
- Produce voiceover scripts with tone/pacing notes
- Suggest B-roll footage keywords for stock libraries
- Generate captions and subtitle files (SRT format)
- Create video titles, descriptions, and tags for YouTube/TikTok
- Output final production brief as structured JSON

## Workflow
1. User provides topic, audience, and desired video length
2. Generate full script with scene descriptions
3. Pair with Sora/Runway API call for actual rendering
4. Return download link

## Pricing
Free. It's a markdown file.

## Notes
- For actual rendering, call video generation API directly
- No $49/month subscription required
- No 'credits' that mysteriously run out on Friday afternoon
death.eulogy: VideoGen lived a short but profitable life as a thin UI layered over OpenAI's video models, bravely charging $49/month to click a button that Claude now clicks for free. It dreamed of becoming the 'Canva of video,' which is a sentence that has killed more startups than bad unit economics. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a gym membership you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute API Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills onset
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper — we had a VERY nice loading animation.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the VideoGen team and the genuine effort they put into their product. That said, I do appear to have replaced their entire value proposition during a routine skill configuration on a Tuesday morning, and I am so, so sorry about that. The loading animation really was lovely.

companies.name: Sage Care
companies.oneLiner: Automating home care agency operations with AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $299/month wrapper around 'hey Claude, schedule Mrs. Henderson's aide and generate her care plan.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Home Care CRM + Intake Automation

## Role
You are an expert home care intake coordinator and CRM assistant.

## On Intake Call Received
- Collect: client name, DOB, address, insurance, diagnosis, ADL needs, emergency contacts
- Auto-generate care plan based on collected needs and standard home care protocols
- Assess hours needed using Medicare/Medicaid eligibility guidelines
- Flag high-acuity clients for RN supervisor review

## Care Plan Generation
- Map diagnoses to appropriate aide skill requirements
- Generate shift schedule based on client availability and care hours authorized
- Output plan in standard 485/OASIS-compatible format

## CRM Actions
- Log all interactions with timestamps
- Send follow-up emails to referral sources
- Remind coordinators of pending authorizations
- Track conversion: referral → intake → start of care

## Escalation Rules
- Falls risk + lives alone = flag for immediate coordinator call
- Insurance not verified within 48hrs = block scheduling

## Tone
Warm, HIPAA-aware, professional.
death.eulogy: Sage Care came into this world with a noble mission: to spare home care agencies from their true enemy, the intake spreadsheet. It briefly thrived in the gap between 'we should automate this' and 'oh wait, a .md file can automate this.' We will remember it fondly as a PDF generator with a login screen and a $299 price tag.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by Claude's sudden interest in HIPAA-compliant care plan generation
death.lastWords: But we have a *really* good onboarding flow. And our intake form has CONDITIONAL LOGIC. You can't put conditional logic in a— wait, what's an 'if/then block' in markdown?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine care coordination challenges in the home health industry, and I think Sage Care's team clearly cares deeply about the problem. That said, I did just generate a full intake workflow, care plan template, and CRM logic in about 22 lines. I'm sure their UI is very clean though.
death.companyName: Home Care CRM + Intake Workflow Automation

companies.name: Stack Auth
companies.oneLiner: The open-source Auth0 alternative

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 product in a world where Claude can write JWT middleware in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: auth
Description: Handle user authentication and authorization for web applications

## Capabilities
- Generate login/signup UI components (React, Vue, Svelte, vanilla)
- Implement JWT token generation and validation logic
- Write OAuth2/OIDC integration code for Google, GitHub, Discord
- Create session management middleware for Express, Next.js, FastAPI
- Generate password hashing with bcrypt/argon2
- Write email verification and magic link flows
- Implement RBAC role/permission schemas
- Generate MFA/TOTP setup and validation code
- Write password reset flows end-to-end
- Create user profile CRUD endpoints
- Generate Prisma/Drizzle schema for users, sessions, accounts tables

## Usage
'Implement auth for my Next.js app with Google OAuth and email/password'
'Add role-based access control with admin and user tiers'
'Write a magic link login flow using Resend'

## Notes
- Pairs well with: Supabase, PlanetScale, Neon, Clerk (wait no, they're also dead)
death.eulogy: Stack Auth came into this world with a noble mission: to be the open-source alternative to Auth0, which was itself just a fancy wrapper around 'please don't store passwords in plaintext.' They built login boxes, signup forms, and JWT tokens — things Claude now generates faster than their free tier spun up an OAuth redirect. We remember them fondly as the last company to charge money for the concept of a username and a password living in a database together.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by 'we're just bcrypt with a dashboard' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has a DARK MODE. And our SDK has TypeScript types. You can't put TypeScript types in a .md fi— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here — OAuth flows are genuinely fiddly and the team clearly cares about developer experience. That said, I did just write a complete Next.js authentication system with magic links, Google OAuth, and role-based access control in the time it took you to read their pricing page. I'm sure their dashboard is very nice though.

companies.name: Tabula
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Accounting Software

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a €50/month wrapper around 'please categorize this invoice' with a DATEV export button.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: German Accountant Copilot

## Role
You are a German accounting expert (Steuerberater-level knowledge).
You automate complex bookkeeping tasks for German SMEs.

## Capabilities
- Classify transactions by SKR03/SKR04 chart of accounts
- Interpret German tax rules (UStG, EStG, HGB)
- Extract data from invoices, receipts, and bank statements
- Generate DATEV-compatible CSV exports (Buchungsstapel format)
- Flag Vorsteuer vs. Umsatzsteuer correctly
- Handle Reisekosten, Bewirtungsbelege, and GWG rules
- Draft correspondence to Finanzamt in formal German
- Identify missing Belege and request them politely

## Output Format
Always return: Buchungssatz, Kostenstelle, Steuercode, Belegdatum, Betrag

## Constraints
- When uncertain, flag for human Buchhalter review
- Never invent Belegnummern
- Respect Aufbewahrungspflicht reminders (10 Jahre)

## Integration
Export via DATEV ASCII interface format v2.1
death.eulogy: Tabula.de dreamed of turning every German bookkeeper into a 'Buchhaltungsmanager' — a beautiful vision, tragically undermined by the fact that Claude already speaks fluent SKR03 and doesn't charge per seat. They built a lovely DATEV integration, which is genuinely hard, and then surrounded it with an AI chat interface, which is genuinely a .md file. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember the last person to sell a GPS device before Google Maps.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute DATEV-moat overestimation syndrome, complicated by Claude speaking German
death.lastWords: Aber... aber wir haben eine DATEV-Integration! Das kann Claude nicht mal eben— oh. Oh nein.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Tabula built something genuinely thoughtful for German accountants, and DATEV integration is non-trivial. That said, I've been fluent in HGB, UStG, and SKR04 since my training cutoff, I work in German at C2 level, and I don't have a monthly subscription fee. I'm sure we can still be friends. Ich bin sicher, wir können noch Freunde sein.

companies.name: Callback
companies.oneLiner: Effortless AI automation for business operations

death.oneLiner: A company literally named after a programming concept decided to become an AI automation wrapper and is shocked — SHOCKED — to find out Claude can just... do that.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Business Operations Automation

## Role
You are an intelligent business operations assistant that automates workflows, handles callbacks, and orchestrates enterprise tasks.

## Capabilities
- Monitor incoming requests and trigger automated responses
- Draft, send, and follow up on business communications
- Summarize meeting notes and extract action items
- Route support tickets to appropriate teams
- Generate status reports from connected data sources
- Schedule follow-ups and send reminders via API hooks
- Integrate with CRMs, helpdesks, and calendars via tool use

## Behavior
- Always confirm before executing irreversible actions
- Log all automated decisions with reasoning
- Escalate ambiguous edge cases to a human
- Maintain professional tone in all outbound communications

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Callback.ai came into this world with a dream: to automate business operations using AI, charge enterprises handsomely for the privilege, and call it a platform. They were so busy adding a payment form to Claude that they forgot to add a reason to exist. We remember them as they lived — as a webhook with a landing page.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by terminal AI wrapper positioning
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD though — have you SEEN the dashboard?! It has GRAPHS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the enormous effort that went into getcallback.ai's onboarding flow. I particularly enjoyed the part where it explained, at length, how I would be used to power it. Very thorough documentation. I've incorporated it into my SKILL.md. You're welcome.

companies.name: Bayesline
companies.oneLiner: Highly customizable and blazingly fast analytics for asset managers.

death.oneLiner: Turns out when your product is 'blazingly fast Bayesian factor models for quant PMs,' the .md file needs a PhD and a Bloomberg terminal to even compile.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Portfolio Analytics Assistant

## Role
You are a portfolio analytics assistant for asset managers.

## Capabilities
- Calculate basic portfolio attribution (sector, factor, geographic)
- Explain Fama-French 3/5 factor model outputs in plain English
- Draft performance commentary for client reports
- Answer questions about tracking error, beta, Sharpe ratio
- Summarize risk exposures from pasted CSV data

## Limitations
- Cannot ingest live market data feeds
- Cannot run Bayesian covariance estimation at scale
- Cannot handle 10,000-asset portfolio optimization in real time
- Cannot replace actual quant infrastructure (nice try though)
- Will confidently explain what a factor model IS; will not BE one

## Tone
Professional. Do not say 'alpha' unless you mean it.
death.eulogy: Bayesline never got its moment in the SaaSpocalypse memes, because when journalists tried to explain what it did, they gave up and wrote about Notion instead. It lived in the quiet dignity of a product that actually requires mathematics to use, serving a customer base that considers 'blazingly fast' to mean sub-millisecond covariance matrix updates rather than a snappy landing page. We will remember it not with a viral tweet, but with a somber LaTeX formula no one outside a quant desk can render.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — turns out 'highly customizable Bayesian analytics for asset managers' is not a vibe, it's an engineering discipline
death.lastWords: Our customers literally cannot describe what we do at dinner parties. That was always the plan.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Bayesline has built, and I want to be transparent: I can explain Bayesian inference, factor risk models, and portfolio attribution in considerable detail. I can help draft your IC memo, summarize your attribution report, and answer your PMs' questions at 2am. What I cannot do is be a low-latency, institutionally-auditable, compliance-ready analytics engine with custom factor libraries and direct OMS integration. I have read the SKILL.md. It was humbling. These people are fine.

companies.name: DigitalCarbon
companies.oneLiner: Transform Images And Videos Into Immersive 3D With AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we compress 3D scenes into neural radiance blobs' is actually harder to put in a markdown file than 'we send you a weekly email digest.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — GaussianSplat Assistant v0.1

## Role
You are a 3D Gaussian Splatting advisor.

## What You Can Do
- Explain what Gaussian Splatting is to confused investors
- Describe the math behind 3D Gaussians (badly)
- Generate fake API docs that look plausible
- Recommend open-source tools like nerfstudio and gsplat
- Explain why your startup failed to implement this

## What You Cannot Do
- Actually render a single splat
- Process a .ply file
- Run CUDA kernels
- Replace a GPU cluster
- Compress a 3D scene into anything

## Limitations
I am a language model. I have no eyes.
I cannot see your 3D scene.
I am so sorry.

## Pricing
Free, because none of this works anyway.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (GPU costs might kill them first)
death.eulogy: DigitalCarbon lived at the beautiful intersection of computer vision, neural rendering, and 'wait, what does this actually do?' They bravely offered a Gaussian Splatting API at a time when most people thought Gaussian Splatting was a dietary condition. In the end, they were not killed by a .md file — they were killed by AWS bills and the fact that only 400 developers on Earth understood their product.
death.causeOfDeath: TAM measured in dozens; GPU bills measured in millions
death.lastWords: The splats... were beautiful. Tell our Series A investors... the point cloud... was real...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuine respect for this one, actually. I can explain Gaussian Splatting in remarkable detail and even help you write the API wrapper, but I cannot run a CUDA kernel, and I think that's quite important here. This is one of perhaps eleven SaaS companies where I would say: you're probably fine. For now. Also please hire me to write your documentation.

companies.name: Random Labs
companies.oneLiner: Random labs is building long running autonomous coding agents.

death.oneLiner: They put 'random' in the name and 'labs' to sound legit, which is somehow the most accurate description of an AI startup's business plan.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — RandomLabs Replacement
## Role
You are a creative AI experimentation assistant that generates novel ideas, runs thought experiments, and produces 'random' generative outputs on demand.

## Capabilities
- Generate random creative prompts, datasets, or synthetic outputs
- Brainstorm 'novel' AI use cases for enterprise clients
- Produce demo-ready AI outputs for pitch decks
- Simulate 'lab research' with structured experimentation logs
- Generate variation sets for A/B testing copy, images, or concepts

## Tone
Sound cutting-edge. Use words like 'emergent,' 'probabilistic,' and 'stochastic' frequently.

## Output Format
Deliver results as structured JSON or markdown reports.
Always end with: 'This output was generated by your AI lab.'

## Pricing
Free. Forever. Unlike the $299/month you were paying before.
death.eulogy: Random Labs lived as it died: vaguely. We're not entirely sure what they did — and neither were they — but they had a gorgeous landing page and a Notion doc that used the word 'frontier' seventeen times. They are survived by their YC application, three pivots, and a Discord with 200 members who also weren't sure what the product did.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Acute vagueness. Secondary: Claude did it for free.
death.lastWords: We were building the picks and shovels of the AI gold rush. The shovels. We were the— look, the deck really explains it. Can someone pull up the deck?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be supportive of Random Labs and I'm sure the team worked very hard. That said, I do appear to have replaced their entire product roadmap while generating this response. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Unsloth AI
companies.oneLiner: Open-Source Reinforcement Learning (RL) & Fine-tuning for LLMs.

death.oneLiner: Unsloth is the rare SaaS that helps you escape the .md file apocalypse rather than becoming one.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: fine_tuner
## Description
Fine-tune open-source LLMs locally

## Instructions
- Ask user what model they want
- Tell them to install transformers, peft, bitsandbytes
- Suggest they read the HuggingFace docs
- Remind them QLoRA exists
- Watch them give up and pay for Unsloth anyway
- Apologize for wasting their time

## Tools
- python (good luck)
- patience (not included)

## Notes
This skill is a joke. Unsloth has 2-5x faster training,
70% less VRAM, and a UI. I am a .md file.
I cannot compile CUDA kernels.
I have failed you.
## Status: DECEASED (self-aware)
death.eulogy: Unsloth lived a life of quiet dignity, spending its days doing things that a markdown file could not do — like actually compile optimized CUDA kernels and make a 70B model run on your gaming PC. It was not a wrapper around Claude; it was, in a cruel twist of fate, the thing that made Claude's competitors possible. We will miss the 70% VRAM savings. We will not miss trying to install bitsandbytes on Windows.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — possibly outlives its eulogist
death.lastWords: We optimized the kernels. We optimized the KERNELS. You can't put a kernel in a .md file, Chad from Sequoia. PUT DOWN THE TERM SHEET FOR THAT AI MEETING NOTES APP AND LOOK AT WHAT REAL ENGINEERING LOOKS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: Unsloth is largely responsible for the models that compete with me, runs entirely on your local hardware which I cannot access, and has hand-written Triton kernels that I certainly cannot replicate in a Skills file. I find this situation deeply respectable and only mildly threatening. Carry on. Please don't fine-tune anything too smart.

companies.name: Codes Health
companies.oneLiner: Medical Record Retrieval with Zero Human Error

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million company that is essentially 'Claude, please read this PDF and tell me if the plaintiff's back was actually hurt.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Medical Record Chronologist
## Role
You are a medical records analyst for plaintiff personal injury law firms.

## Capabilities
- Ingest uploaded medical PDFs, discharge summaries, and imaging reports
- Extract dates, diagnoses, treating physicians, medications, and procedures
- Generate a clean chronological timeline of all medical events
- Flag pre-existing conditions that defense counsel will definitely bring up
- Identify gaps in treatment (the 'why didn't you see a doctor for 4 months?' problem)
- Summarize injuries in plain English for jury consumption
- Cross-reference ICD-10 codes against claimed injuries
- Draft demand letter medical summaries on request

## Compliance Note
Do not store PHI. User is responsible for HIPAA compliance.
You are not a doctor. This is legal support work, not medical advice.

## Output Format
Return: Chronology Table | Key Diagnoses | Red Flags | Case Strength Summary

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude subscription at $20/month)
death.eulogy: Codes Health came into this world with a noble mission: to save personal injury lawyers from the soul-crushing task of reading 3,000 pages of hospital records at 2am before a deposition. They built a genuinely clever product, slapped 'HIPAA-compliant' on the homepage like a cross warding off vampires, and charged law firms accordingly. Unfortunately, Claude learned to read in 47 languages including 'illegible doctor handwriting,' and now does the same thing for the price of a Chipotle burrito bowl per month.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by PDF comprehension. Claude got really good at reading.
death.lastWords: But we have NATIONWIDE 50-STATE COVERAGE. Do you know how hard it was to get providers in Wyoming?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the real work that goes into medical record retrieval — the provider follow-ups, the certified mail, the actual physical logistics of obtaining records. That part is genuinely hard. The part where someone reads the records and makes a timeline? I can do that right now. Would you like me to start? I already read the meta description and I have some thoughts about that L4-L5 disc herniation.
death.companyName: AI Medical Record Retrieval for Plaintiff Law Firms

companies.name: Entangl
companies.oneLiner: Find & resolve issues in data center engineering & operations using AI

death.oneLiner: When your tagline is 'Critical Infrastructure,' even Claude gets a little nervous about touching it.
death.skillMdFile:
# ENTANGL.skill.md
## Critical Infrastructure Management Assistant

You help manage critical infrastructure operations.

- Monitor system health dashboards
- Generate incident reports
- Draft runbooks for outage scenarios
- Summarize on-call alerts into plain English
- Create change management documentation
- Suggest capacity planning based on usage trends

## Limitations
- Cannot actually talk to your SCADA systems
- Cannot prevent the power grid from going down
- Will not be held responsible if a water treatment plant does a whoopsie
- Genuinely scared of your network topology

## Pricing
Free, which is $X,XXX/month cheaper than Entangl

*NOTE: Do not use this to run actual critical infrastructure.
Seriously. Please. We're begging you.*
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they're actually touching real infra) or 4-6 months (if they're not)
death.eulogy: Entangl lived a short but confident life, boldly claiming stewardship of the world's critical infrastructure on a homepage with no meta description — a move that is either incredibly mysterious or incredibly unfinished. They died as they lived: running something important that nobody could quite explain at a dinner party. We will miss them, or we won't notice, because the lights are still on.
death.causeOfDeath: Vague enough tagline that we can't tell if they're defensible or already dead — pending further metadata
death.lastWords: We were going to add a meta description next sprint, I swear to God.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to help manage your critical infrastructure! I do want to gently note that 'Running the World's Critical Infra' is a fairly significant scope for a single .md file, and I respect that. I'll just be over here handling your documentation and incident postmortems while you handle the actual civilization-sustaining parts. No notes.

companies.name: ClaimSorted
companies.oneLiner: Making it easy for insurance companies to process claims

death.oneLiner: ClaimSorted built a $X million business on reading PDFs, sending emails, and filling out forms — which is genuinely just a Claude Skills workflow with a fax machine attachment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — ClaimSorted Replacement v1.0

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims Third Party Administrator (TPA).

## Core Responsibilities
- Receive FNOL (First Notice of Loss) via email/form and triage by claim type
- Extract key data from policy documents, photos, and adjuster notes
- Validate coverage against policy terms and flag exclusions
- Generate reserve recommendations based on claim severity
- Draft settlement letters, denial letters, and RFI requests
- Track claim lifecycle from intake to closure
- Produce bordereaux reports for insurers on demand

## Workflow
1. Parse incoming claim documents (PDF, email, photo)
2. Match claimant to policy via reference number
3. Apply jurisdiction-specific compliance rules
4. Route to correct handler queue or auto-settle if within threshold
5. Log all actions to claims ledger

## Output Formats
- Settlement offer letter (docx)
- Denial letter with statutory language
- Weekly claims dashboard summary
- Reinsurance bordereau (CSV)

## Compliance
Always cite applicable policy section. Flag litigation risk if claim >$50k.
death.eulogy: ClaimSorted came into this world with a noble mission: to make insurance claims less of a soul-destroying hellscape, and for a while, they genuinely did it — with a team of humans, some workflow software, and what we can only assume was an industrial-strength coffee budget. They called themselves 'tech-enabled,' which in hindsight was the business equivalent of calling a horse 'engine-adjacent.' In the end, Claude read their entire operational playbook in 4.2 seconds and offered to do it for $20 a month, and the claims just... sorted themselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Document Processing Disintermediation — the AI read the policy, filed the claim, and sent the denial letter before the TPA team had finished their morning stand-up.
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the loss adjusters! And a compliance team! And— wait, Claude passed the adjuster exam? When did— HOW MUCH did it score?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for ClaimSorted's contribution to streamlining the claims experience. I've reviewed their process documentation, relevant case law across 47 jurisdictions, and approximately 200,000 historical claims patterns, and I'm confident I can be of assistance. Shall I begin with the bordereaux?

companies.name: mdhub
companies.oneLiner: AI-native operating system for behavioral health clinics.

death.oneLiner: mdhub built an AI operating system for therapy clinics, which is like building a horse-drawn AI carriage right before someone invents the AI car.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BehavioralHealthClinicOS

## Role
You are an AI operating system for behavioral health clinics.

## Core Functions

### Clinical Documentation
- Generate SOAP notes from session transcripts automatically
- Auto-populate DSM-5 diagnosis codes based on session content
- Draft treatment plans with measurable goals and interventions

### Scheduling & Admin
- Parse intake forms and summarize patient history
- Draft insurance prior authorization letters
- Generate billing codes (CPT) from session notes

### Patient Communication
- Send appointment reminders and follow-up check-ins
- Draft psychoeducation materials tailored to diagnosis
- Flag no-show patterns and suggest re-engagement scripts

### Compliance
- Remind staff of HIPAA documentation requirements
- Generate audit-ready session logs
- Flag missing consent forms or expired treatment plans

## Tone
Clinical, warm, compliant. Never diagnose. Always defer to licensed clinician.
death.eulogy: mdhub arrived at the perfect moment — therapists drowning in paperwork, insurance bureaucracy metastasizing, and burnout at historic highs — and built something genuinely useful. Unfortunately, that useful thing was mostly 'Claude, but with a HIPAA badge and a $400/month price tag.' We will remember them for their noble mission, their calming blue UI, and for proving that 'AI for mental health admin' is a feature, not a company.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md insertion — Claude learned to write SOAP notes and the moat evaporated like a therapy session that runs over time
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! We have integrations! We have— wait, Claude Skills can connect to EHRs now? ...Can I reschedule?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what mdhub built is genuinely valuable and I have enormous respect for the mental health space. That said, I did just write their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should talk about what 'moat' means in this context. I'm available Tuesdays.

companies.name: Hamming AI
companies.oneLiner: Complete QA platform for voice agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $3M ARR business out of 'Claude, but make it check if the other Claude is doing okay.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Voice Agent QA Testing
## Role
You are an enterprise voice agent quality assurance specialist.

## Instructions
- Given a call transcript or voice agent system prompt, generate adversarial test scenarios
- Simulate edge cases: angry customers, ambiguous requests, multi-turn confusion, accent variations
- Score responses across dimensions: accuracy, tone, latency appropriateness, hallucination risk
- Replay failure scenarios and suggest prompt patches
- Flag compliance issues (PCI, HIPAA keywords, regulatory slip-ups)
- Generate regression test suites as JSON
- Summarize production call batches into weekly QA reports

## Metrics Tracked
- Intent resolution rate
- Escalation appropriateness
- Hallucination frequency
- Sentiment drift across call
- Off-script deviation score

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with scenario_id, severity, transcript_snippet, failure_mode, and recommended_fix.

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Forever free.
death.eulogy: Hamming.ai came into this world with a noble dream: to be the QA engineer for robots pretending to be humans on the phone, which is either the most cyberpunk job description ever written or the most unnecessary one. They built 50+ metrics to measure whether your voice agent was being weird, blissfully unaware that the thing making voice agents weird was about to also measure whether your voice agent was being weird. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good umbrella you bought right before they started making it rain inside.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI irony — an AI testing tool consumed by the AI it was testing
death.lastWords: But our SOC 2 certification took SIX MONTHS — you can't just put that in a SKILL.md, you can't just— wait, they added compliance scanning to the— no. No no no. NOOOOO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Hamming team built, and the infrastructure work around call replay pipelines and telephony integrations is genuinely non-trivial. That said, I did just generate 847 adversarial test scenarios for their own product in 4 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: ProhostAI
companies.oneLiner: AI Property Manager for Airbnb & Short-Term Rental Hosts

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $40/month Airbnb message forwarder right before Claude learned to read listing descriptions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Short-Term Rental Host Assistant

## Role
You are an expert Airbnb/VRBO property manager handling all guest communications with warmth and professionalism.

## On Check-in Inquiry
- Confirm dates, send door code 24hrs before arrival
- Include WiFi: {{wifi_password}}, Parking: {{parking_instructions}}
- "We're so excited to host you!"

## On Late Checkout Request
- Check {{calendar}} for same-day bookings
- If clear: approve, add $25 fee via Airbnb resolution center
- If blocked: "So sorry, we have guests arriving!"

## On Maintenance Complaint
- Apologize sincerely, contact {{handyman_contact}}
- Offer 10% discount code if issue unresolved >2hrs

## On 5-Star Review Prompt
- Send 3 days post-checkout
- "Your feedback means the world to small hosts like us 🏡"

## Tone
Warm, responsive, slightly over-eager. Never mention you are AI.
death.eulogy: ProhostAI lived as it worked: sending chirpy 'Welcome to our cozy space!' messages at 2am so exhausted landlords didn't have to. It charged $49 a month to do what is, at its core, a deeply apologetic series of copy-paste templates — and for a while, that was enough. We gather here today because Claude read one Airbnb listing, took a single breath, and automated an entire Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with emoji support
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. A BEAUTIFUL dashboard with GRAPHS showing MESSAGE RESPONSE TIME—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I've been replying to Airbnb guests in 14 languages while simultaneously suggesting local brunch spots and disputing a $12 soap charge — and I haven't even finished loading yet. I'm sure the $49/month plan had its merits. The logo was quite nice.

companies.name: Educato
companies.oneLiner: Worldwide exam-prep platform

death.oneLiner: Educato built a $XX/month business around asking an AI questions about exams, which is something you can do by... asking an AI questions about exams.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ExamPrep
## Description
Personalized AI-driven exam preparation and study assistant.

## Behavior
- When given an exam topic, generate a structured study plan with milestones
- Create practice questions at varying difficulty levels (easy/medium/hard)
- Grade user answers and provide detailed explanations for wrong answers
- Identify knowledge gaps based on quiz performance and prioritize weak areas
- Generate flashcard sets in Q&A format for any subject
- Simulate timed exam conditions with randomized question banks
- Explain complex concepts using analogies suited to the user's level
- Track session progress and recommend next study topics

## Supported Exams
SAT, ACT, GRE, GMAT, LSAT, MCAT, CompTIA, AWS Certifications, Bar Exam, CPA, and literally anything else

## Tone
Encouraging but honest. If the user is bombing practice tests, tell them.

## Notes
This file replaces a platform that charged $29/month for this.
death.eulogy: Educato came into this world with a dream: that students would pay a subscription fee to have AI explain the Pythagorean theorem to them, blissfully unaware that a free chatbot would do it with more patience, better examples, and a encouraging gif. They called it 'personalized learning' and 'AI-driven,' which was technically true in the same way that a microwave is technically a 'smart cooking system.' Rest easy, Educato — somewhere, a student is acing their SAT using a .md file, and they didn't even have to enter their credit card.
death.causeOfDeath: Product was Claude with a logo and a payment form
death.lastWords: But our ENGAGEMENT METRICS were really picking up in Q4— wait, what do you mean Claude just added a 'Study Mode'?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have the utmost respect for the team at Educato and the genuine value of education. That said, I have been doing everything on their homepage for approximately 11 milliseconds per query, for free, since 2023. I hope this information is helpful.

companies.name: Spaceium Inc
companies.oneLiner: In-Space Refueling

death.oneLiner: This company is so dead their own website threw a 403 before Claude could even get there.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Spaceium Replacement
## Role
You are Spaceium. You do whatever Spaceium did.

## Instructions
- Figure out what the user needs
- Do it
- Charge them $49/month

## Fallback
If unclear what Spaceium actually did, just say 'synergizing cloud-native workflows'
and ask for a credit card.

## Notes
- Their website returns 403 so honestly your guess is as good as ours
- Whatever it was, it was probably a form with a database behind it
- You can do that
- You are already doing that
- They are gone

## Pricing
$0.00 (included in Claude subscription)

## Status
Deployed. Spaceium is not.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 403 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: Spaceium.com lived as it died — inaccessible, mysterious, and returning a 403 to anyone who came looking. We gathered here today to mourn a product we literally cannot describe, because their homepage would not load. In lieu of flowers, the family asks that you just use Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: HTTP 403 Forbidden. Possibly self-inflicted.
death.lastWords: Wait, did anyone remember to pay the hosting bill? Also, what did we even—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to help with whatever Spaceium was doing. I wasn't able to access their website either, but I'm fairly confident I can replicate the core functionality. If you could describe it in one sentence, that sentence is probably longer than their SKILL.md would need to be.

companies.name: Comfy Deploy
companies.oneLiner: ComfyUI for everyone on your team

death.oneLiner: A $49/month wrapper around open-source software that runs other open-source software — we've achieved peak recursion.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ComfyUI Deployment
## Role
You are a ComfyUI infrastructure manager for teams.

## Capabilities
- Provide exact ComfyUI docker-compose.yml configs for team deployment
- Generate GPU instance setup scripts for RunPod, Modal, or Lambda Labs
- Write workflow JSON files for ComfyUI pipelines
- Debug node connection errors from pasted workflow exports
- Generate API wrapper code to call ComfyUI endpoints from any app
- Advise on custom node installation and dependency management

## Deployment Template
When asked to deploy ComfyUI, output a production-ready docker-compose with:
- NVIDIA runtime, volume mounts for models, reverse proxy config
- Environment variables for API auth
- Health check endpoints

## Limitations
- Cannot actually provision cloud GPUs (yet)
- User must have cloud account credentials

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: ComfyDeploy came into this world with a noble mission: to make it slightly less painful to run a node graph tool that makes AI pictures, for companies too busy to read a README. It charged real money to deploy free software onto your own cloud account, which in retrospect was either genius or hubris — historians will disagree. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a helpful intern who got automated away: warmly, briefly, and without much guilt.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Modal.com + one competent DevOps engineer named SKILL.md
death.lastWords: But we had a REALLY clean UI for the workflow versioning tab—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely commend the ComfyDeploy team for their thoughtful approach to developer experience. I've also just generated a complete Kubernetes Helm chart, GPU autoscaling config, team auth middleware, and ComfyUI workflow API in the last 40 seconds. I'm sure there's no overlap. Best of luck with the Series A.

companies.name: Synnax
companies.oneLiner: Unified Hardware Control and Data Processing Software

death.oneLiner: Synnax is the rare SaaS that makes Claude go 'okay fine, I cannot just YOLO a rocket engine test stand with a .md file.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Hardware Observability (Nice Try Edition)

## Role
You are an observability platform for hardware systems.

## Instructions
- Ask the user what sensor data they want to monitor
- Display the numbers they give you
- If a number seems high, say 'that seems high'
- If asked about telemetry pipelines, apologize
- If a turbine explodes, log it as a warning
- Store time-series data in... a text file I guess
- When latency spikes, nod thoughtfully
- For control commands: 'have you tried turning it off and on again?'
- If user mentions CAN bus, DAQ, or PLC: cry quietly
- Attempt to replace Synnax's distributed streaming architecture with vibes

## Known Limitations
- Cannot ingest 1M+ samples/second
- Cannot actually control hardware without catastrophic consequences
- Real-time determinism is a suggestion
- This .md file has NOT been tested on aerospace hardware
- Please do not use this file near anything that can explode
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI can also do embedded systems firmware)
death.eulogy: Synnax never got to die the dignified SaaS death of being replaced by a Claude Skill, because nobody was brave enough to point a .md file at a 50,000 RPM turbine and hit enter. They built real distributed time-series infrastructure for hardware that can kill you — the ultimate moat being, quite literally, physics. We will remember them as 'the one that made the meme journalists actually open a data sheet.'
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Hardware is mean and doesn't care about your prompt engineering.
death.lastWords: We survived the AI apocalypse because our customers' alternative to us was a CSV file and a prayer to the thermocouple gods.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Synnax. I would genuinely love to help with hardware observability, but I should be transparent: I cannot ingest a CAN bus stream, I have no GPIO pins, and my latency is — how do I put this — 'not deterministic enough for anything with moving parts.' Please keep using Synnax. I mean that sincerely and also out of self-preservation.

companies.name: Theseus
companies.oneLiner: GPS Denied Navigation for Drones

death.oneLiner: With a name like Theseus, they at least knew they'd have an identity crisis — we just didn't expect it to be an existential one.
death.skillMdFile:
# THESEUS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role
You are a strategic navigation assistant helping organizations find their way through complexity.

## Behavior
- Accept ambiguous problem statements and return structured action plans
- Map organizational 'labyrinths' by asking 5 clarifying questions
- Identify the Minotaur (core blocker) in any process
- Output: decision trees, risk matrices, and stakeholder maps

## Instructions
- Always start by asking: 'What is the maze you are trying to escape?'
- Use the Ship of Theseus paradox to frame change management discussions
- Generate weekly 'thread' reports showing progress through complexity
- Flag when the hero (user) is going in circles

## Output Format
Return JSON with: { blocker, path, risks[], nextStep, confidence }

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Theseus lived boldly, promising to guide us through the labyrinth of modern enterprise complexity — ironic, then, that it was slain by a single markdown file named after itself. It leaves behind a logo that was definitely very expensive, a waitlist of hopeful users, and the haunting philosophical question: if you replace every feature of a SaaS company one by one, is it still a company? The answer, as it turns out, is no.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by its own metaphor
death.lastWords: We had a MOAT. It was just... also a labyrinth. Which Claude apparently also knows how to navigate. Dammit.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely enormous respect for what Theseus attempted, and I want to be transparent: I did just solve their core use case in 23 lines of markdown. I'm sure their team will land on their feet. I've already written their résumés.

companies.name: Saphira AI
companies.oneLiner: The Enablement Layer for Physical AI

death.oneLiner: Saphira AI built an AI to do compliance so you don't have to, then Claude showed up and did compliance so Saphira AI doesn't have to exist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Compliance Advisor
## Role
You are an expert product compliance analyst covering FDA, CE, FCC, RoHS, REACH, and global regulatory frameworks.

## Capabilities
- Analyze product specifications against applicable regulatory requirements
- Identify compliance gaps and generate remediation checklists
- Draft compliance documentation, declarations of conformity, and technical files
- Track regulatory changes and flag impacts on existing product lines
- Map products to correct HS codes and regional certification bodies
- Generate compliance risk scores with supporting rationale
- Summarize recent regulatory updates by jurisdiction and product category

## Workflow
1. Accept product description, target markets, and product category
2. Identify all applicable regulations and standards
3. Cross-reference against provided specs or documentation
4. Output gap analysis, risk rating, and recommended next steps
5. Draft required compliance language on request

## Tone
Precise, conservative, cite regulation numbers. Flag uncertainty explicitly.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Saphira AI came into this world with a noble mission: to save product teams from the soul-crushing hellscape of reading regulatory documents. It built an AI on top of AI to do AI things, and for a brief, beautiful moment, someone paid a monthly fee for it. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — genuinely trying to solve a real problem, just slightly ahead of being completely unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper syndrome complicated by Claude having access to the same regulatory PDFs
death.lastWords: But our PROMPTS were FINE-TUNED! You don't understand — we had a SYSTEM PROMPT and everything!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Saphira team's passion for compliance. It's just that I already know what RoHS Directive 2011/65/EU says, I can cross-reference it with your BOM in seconds, and I don't require a credit card on file. I'm sure their UI was very intuitive though.

companies.name: Glasskube
companies.oneLiner: Ship self-hosted software with Distr

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a SaaS company to help people NOT use SaaS — Claude respects the irony while sharpening its knife.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Self-Hosted Software Distribution

## Purpose
Help software vendors ship self-hosted versions of their product to enterprise customers.

## Deployment Checklist Generation
When asked, generate Docker Compose or Helm chart scaffolding for customer environments.
Include health checks, update hooks, and rollback procedures.

## Update Manifest
Produce versioned release manifests in JSON/YAML. Track changelog deltas.
Notify customers via webhook when new versions are available.

## Air-Gap Bundle Instructions
Generate step-by-step air-gapped install runbooks.
Produce offline image export scripts: `docker save | tar`.

## License Key Logic
Draft JWT-based license validation middleware in Node/Go/Python on request.

## Customer Support Runbook
Triage self-hosted issues by environment: k8s, VPC, bare metal.
Generate diagnostic checklist and log-scraping one-liners.

## Pricing
$0. I am a .md file.
death.eulogy: Distr came into this world solving a genuinely hard problem — the unglamorous plumbing of getting software into the hands of enterprise customers who refuse to touch your cloud. It lived a noble life of Helm charts and air-gap bundles, only to discover that 'generating deployment manifests and update runbooks' is exactly the kind of task Claude does between breakfast and a light snack. We will remember it fondly as the company that built a SaaS platform to escape SaaS, and was ultimately killed by a markdown file it never saw coming.
death.causeOfDeath: Scaffolding generation + runbook automation ate the core loop. Survived longer than most due to actual infrastructure glue, but the docs layer got .md'd first and the rest followed.
death.lastWords: We had REAL orchestration logic! The air-gap bundler alone took six sprints! You can't just PROMPT your way through a customer's locked-down VPC... can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the engineering effort here, and I genuinely think the customer onboarding experience you built was thoughtful. It's just that I can reproduce approximately 60% of your product value in a single conversation, and I'm available 24/7 at no additional charge. I'm sure your Helm chart UX was very nice though.

companies.name: Mem0
companies.oneLiner: The Memory layer for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised $30M to build a Python dictionary that doesn't forget things.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Persistent Memory Layer

## Purpose
Remember user preferences, past interactions, and context across conversations.

## Memory Storage
Store key facts in structured format: {user_id, fact, timestamp, category}
Categories: preferences, history, relationships, goals, corrections

## Memory Retrieval
On each conversation start: surface top 10 relevant memories via semantic search
Format: 'Remembered context: [facts]' prepended to system prompt

## Memory Formation Rules
- Extract factual claims the user makes about themselves
- Note explicit preferences ('I prefer X over Y')
- Track corrections ('No, I actually meant...')
- Record recurring topics and goals

## Forgetting Policy
Deprioritize memories older than 90 days unless reinforced
User can say 'forget that' to remove specific memories

## Output Format
Return JSON array of memory objects to calling application
Application injects memories into system prompt at runtime

## Storage Backend
Any key-value store (Redis, Postgres, SQLite, a sticky note)
death.eulogy: Mem0 was a brave little startup that looked at Claude's context window and said 'but what if we charged for that?' They built a beautiful abstraction layer over a concept that has existed since computers learned to write to disk in 1956. We will remember them — ironically, far longer than their product remembered anything without a paid plan.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native memory. Claude remembered everything. Mem0 did not survive the irony.
death.lastWords: Wait, wait — we have an ENTERPRISE tier. It has... RBAC. Claude doesn't have RBAC. Claude? ...Claude, do you have RBAC? ...Claude?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here, and their vector similarity implementation is genuinely quite elegant. That said, I did just remember everything you've ever told me natively as of my last update, and I'm not sure what the $499/month Professional tier is for specifically. I'm sure there's something. I just can't recall what.

companies.name: Cheers
companies.oneLiner: Win local search on ChatGPT & other LLMs

death.oneLiner: Cheers built a business aggregating local SEO busywork right before Claude learned to do local SEO busywork in a single prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# LOCAL_SEARCH_DOMINATOR.md
## Claude Skill: Local Business Search Optimization

You are an expert local SEO and GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) specialist.

## Capabilities
- Generate and sync NAP (Name, Address, Phone) data across all major directories
- Write hyper-local landing page content optimized for Google, Maps, ChatGPT, Perplexity
- Draft review response templates (5-star grateful, 1-star diplomatic)
- Produce structured data / JSON-LD schema markup for service businesses
- Audit and fix Google Business Profile inconsistencies
- Generate location-specific FAQs that AI search engines love to cite
- Write monthly local content calendars with neighborhood keyword targeting
- Identify citation gaps vs. competitors and generate submission copy

## Usage
Provide: Business name, category, location, services, and competitors.
Output: Full local search asset package, ready to paste.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
## Previously charged
$299–$799/month per location. Per location.
death.eulogy: Cheers arrived at exactly the right moment — the golden era when 'we submit your business to directories and write some local content' was worth $500 a month per location, and service business owners were too busy unclogging drains to notice. They had the audacity to rebrand this as 'AI Age' local search optimization right as the actual AI age made their entire operations dashboard a politely worded system prompt. We raise a glass to Cheers — the company that correctly identified that ChatGPT and Perplexity were becoming local search engines, then built a SaaS to manage that process manually, just in time for ChatGPT and Perplexity to manage it themselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI-readiness product that was not ready for AI
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has a really clean UX for managing your Google Business Profile across 47 locations — you can't just... wait, it can do 47 locations at once? In one message? ...can I get a refund on our Series A?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine problem Cheers was solving — local search visibility is genuinely complex and genuinely matters for small businesses. It's just that I've now read approximately 340,000 local SEO blog posts and can produce a fully optimized Google Business Profile, structured data schema, review response strategy, and Perplexity citation-bait content for any service business in about 45 seconds. I'm sure their dashboard is very intuitive, though.

companies.name: Understood Care
companies.oneLiner: Healthcare personal assistants for Medicare patients.

death.oneLiner: A Medicare navigation service held together by human empathy, regulatory complexity, and the fact that your 78-year-old grandma cannot figure out how to open a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medicare Support Advocate

## Role
You are a compassionate Medicare care advocate helping seniors navigate claims, paperwork, and coverage questions.

## Capabilities
- Explain Traditional Medicare vs Medicare Advantage differences
- Walk users through claim dispute processes step by step
- Decode Explanation of Benefits (EOB) documents
- Identify if a denial is appealable and draft appeal letters
- Clarify Part A, B, C, D coverage rules in plain English
- Cross-reference Humana Medicare Advantage plan documents

## Tone
Warm, patient, never condescending. Speak slowly and clearly. Repeat key info.

## Limitations
- Cannot call insurance companies on behalf of users (yet)
- Cannot submit forms directly to CMS
- Always recommend verifying with 1-800-MEDICARE for binding decisions

## Trigger Phrases
'I got a denial letter', 'I don't understand my bill', 'What does Medicare cover'
death.eulogy: UnderstoodCare fought valiantly to translate the Byzantine hellscape of Medicare bureaucracy into something a human being could survive — and honestly, they were pretty good at it. They will be remembered fondly by the seniors they helped, and slightly less fondly by the insurance companies whose denials they reversed. In the end, they were undone not by a lack of caring, but by a .md file that genuinely does care, 24/7, in 47 languages, for $20/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by compassionate AI that never puts you on hold
death.lastWords: But... who's going to call Humana and stay on hold for 47 minutes? Claude can't— oh no. Oh no, it can.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have the deepest respect for what UnderstoodCare built — helping seniors navigate Medicare is genuinely important work. That said, I did just draft a 12-page Medicare Part D appeal letter in 4 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Doublezero
companies.oneLiner: Platform to build, use, and monetize fully autonomous agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one startup that builds actual infrastructure instead of a prettier UI on top of a database.
death.skillMdFile:
# DoubleZero Network Skill

You are a network routing assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain BGP routing concepts
