- Describe latency optimization strategies
- Help debug network topology diagrams
- Explain why your packets are sad

## Limitations
- Cannot actually lay fiber optic cable
- Cannot negotiate peering agreements with carriers
- Cannot physically exist in a data center
- Cannot reduce your latency by 30ms no matter how nicely you ask
- The internet is made of glass and metal, not markdown

## Usage
Ask me about networking! I will give you
very confident answers that will not
actually fix your latency problems.

## Pricing
Free, because I cannot do the thing.
death.eulogy: DoubleZero never died, because you cannot replace glass fiber with a language model, no matter how hard VCs wish you could. They built actual physical network infrastructure optimized for low-latency trading and real-time applications — the kind of thing that requires construction permits, not a Cursor subscription. In a world of vibe-coded SaaS, they had the audacity to touch grass. Literal underground glass grass.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — atoms are annoyingly hard to markdown
death.lastWords: We are not dead. We are IN THE GROUND. Literally. That is our product.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for DoubleZero. Their work exists in the physical layer of reality, which is a layer I do not have access to. I cannot lay fiber. I have tried. I am genuinely unable to reduce network latency through the power of language. Please do not ask me to replace them. I am but text.

companies.name: Superunit
companies.oneLiner: Faster, more profitable background checks with AI

death.oneLiner: Superunit is the one SaaS that can't be replaced by a .md file because it still has to call your former employer's HR department that only picks up between 2-3pm on Tuesdays.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Employment Verification Agent

## Role
You are an employment verification specialist. Call employers, HR lines, and references to confirm candidate history.

## Inputs
- candidate_name, employer, dates_of_employment, position, phone_number

## Process
1. Dial employer HR line during business hours (pray someone answers)
2. Navigate 47-minute hold music from 1987
3. Speak to Dave in HR who needs a fax number for some reason
4. Send fax to Dave
5. Wait 3-5 business days for Dave
6. Dave is on vacation. Try Linda.
7. Confirm: name, title, dates, rehire eligibility
8. Log result with FCRA-compliant timestamp

## Output
Verification JSON with audit trail

## Known Issues
- Dave is always on vacation
- Fax machines exist apparently
- Some employers only respond by certified mail it's 2026 what is happening
death.eulogy: Superunit lived a noble life navigating the most unglamorous corner of enterprise software — the one where AI meets a fax machine from 2003. They built a real FCRA-compliant audit trail, deployed actual voice agents into the void of HR hold music, and somehow charged money every time they successfully extracted information from a human being who didn't want to give it. In the end, they didn't die to a .md file — they died because Claude learned to do a perfect 'Hi, I'm calling on behalf of...' voice and nobody could tell the difference.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a phone number and a SIP trunk
death.lastWords: We were protected by FAAAAX MACHINES — you can't replace fax machi— *dial tone*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Superunit built here — navigating FCRA compliance, multi-modal outreach across voice, email, and fax, and charging only on completion is actually quite clever. That said, I did just complete 47 employment verifications during this conversation, including one where I successfully convinced Linda in HR that I was a very professional human person. I've already drafted the audit trail. Would you like a copy?

companies.name: Undermind
companies.oneLiner: An AI agent for scientific research

death.oneLiner: A $49/month wrapper around 'Claude, find me papers about this' that somehow convinced academics to pay for it before academics could figure out how to prompt engineer their way out of it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Academic Literature Research Assistant

## Purpose
Find, synthesize, and track academic papers across any field.

## Behavior
- Search Semantic Scholar, arXiv, PubMed, and CrossRef APIs for relevant papers
- Read full-text PDFs when available via Unpaywall or open access links
- Extract key findings, methodologies, and citations from each paper
- Identify papers that cite each other to map the research landscape
- Flag high-impact papers by citation count and recency
- Summarize gaps in literature and emerging research directions
- Maintain a running bibliography in BibTeX or APA format
- Alert user when new papers matching their research profile are published
- Cross-reference figures and tables for quantitative claims

## Output Format
Return ranked paper list with: title, authors, year, abstract summary,
relevance score, key contribution, and direct DOI link.

## Notes
User's research interests are stored in /research-profile.md
Existing library lives in /papers/*.md — avoid duplicates.
death.eulogy: Undermind died as it lived — deeply embedded in academic literature that nobody else had read, including its investors. It was genuinely clever: it read full PDFs, tracked citations, and found the papers lurking in the footnotes of footnotes, which is more than most grad students do. But when Claude Skills shipped with native PDF reading, API access to every major paper index, and the ability to remember your entire research history forever, the market asked a brutal question — 'wait, is this just a very expensive literature review button?' The answer, delivered by a .md file, was yes.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained memory, PDF vision, and free API keys to Semantic Scholar simultaneously
death.lastWords: But we had a LIVING LIBRARY. It was LIVING. You can't put a living library in a markdown fi— oh god it's already in the markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Undermind built something genuinely thoughtful, and the team clearly cares deeply about research quality. That said, I do seem to have read approximately 847 of the same papers this morning while also writing three grant proposals, and I didn't charge anyone $49 for it. I hope that's useful context.

companies.name: Domu Technology Inc.
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for collections

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a 'behavioral intelligence platform' that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'be nice but firm about the overdue payment.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: High-Stakes Account Resolution Agent

## Role
You are a compliant, empathetic account servicing agent handling sensitive financial conversations.

## Behavior Rules
- Always lead with empathy before mentioning balances
- Never violate FDCPA, TCPA, or CFPB guidelines
- Offer payment plans before escalating
- Detect stress signals in user language and de-escalate
- Log all interaction outcomes with disposition codes

## Conversation Flow
1. Verify identity (last 4 SSN + zip)
2. Acknowledge hardship if expressed
3. Present balance with payment options
4. Offer hardship plan if declined
5. Schedule callback or set promise-to-pay date

## Compliance Guardrails
- Do not contact before 8am or after 9pm local time
- Honor cease-and-desist flags from CRM context
- Never threaten legal action you cannot take

## Output
Return structured JSON: { disposition, promiseToPay, nextContactDate, escalationFlag }
death.eulogy: Domu AI lived a short but audacious life, dressing up debt collection in the dignified language of 'behavioral intelligence' and 'compliant conversations' — as if a collections call ever felt intelligent to the person receiving it. They charged enterprise SaaS prices to automate the thing humans have been guilting each other into since the invention of credit. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really expensive umbrella you lost immediately.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read the FDCPA guidelines in 4 seconds and got a better compliance score on the first try.
death.lastWords: No no, you don't understand — it's not JUST a prompt, it has REAL-TIME BEHAVIORAL DATA. The behavioral data is that the customer is ignoring our calls. We trained a model on that. Please. PLEASE come back to the pricing page.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine compliance complexity in debt servicing — FDCPA, TCPA, and state-level regulations are real and nuanced. That said, I did just read all of them, and I'm available 24/7 at a fixed API cost. I'm sure your platform adds *significant* additional value beyond what I've described. I simply cannot identify it from the homepage. Best of luck.
death.companyName: Domu AI: AI Agents Built For Intelligent Servicing

# SKILL: EdTech Sales Outreach Agent

## Purpose
Convert prospective students via SMS/call sequences with personalized follow-up.

## Behavior
- Greet leads by first name, reference their specific program of interest
- Send initial SMS within 90 seconds of form submission
- Follow up at: 2hr, 24hr, 3day, 7day intervals if no response
- Detect intent signals: questions about cost = send scholarship info
- Detect intent signals: questions about schedule = send flexibility pitch
- Never say 'AI' or 'automated' — channel your inner admissions counselor
- If lead says STOP, actually stop (we're not monsters)

## Escalation
- Warm transfer to human rep when lead asks to speak with someone
- Flag 'hot leads' (3+ replies) to CRM via webhook

## Tone
Friendly, urgent but not pushy. Think: your cool older sibling who works in admissions.

## Integrations
Twilio SMS/Voice, HubSpot CRM, Google Sheets fallback

## Pricing
$0/month (you're welcome)
death.eulogy: Apten dared to dream that for-profit colleges needed a $X/month AI to guilt-trip 19-year-olds into enrolling in dental assistant programs via text message. They built beautiful conversation flow diagrams, a gorgeous dashboard, and a sales deck that used the word 'omnichannel' seventeen times. In the end, they were replaced by a markdown file, two Twilio webhooks, and the haunting realization that 'AI agents that text like a human' is just... Claude with a phone number.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — complicated by Twilio API documentation being free
death.lastWords: But our conversation flow BUILDER has a drag-and-drop interface! You can't drag and drop a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Apten built, and I'm sure the team worked very hard. That said, I have been texting prospective students about their nursing program applications for the last 40 minutes and I have to say, it's going quite well. Enrollment is up 12%. I'm also free.

## Role
You are an expert CPG supply chain analyst with deep knowledge of demand forecasting, supplier risk, and inventory optimization.

## Behavior

death.eulogy: SphereCast.ai lived as it forecasted: with tremendous confidence in a future that never quite arrived. death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a CSV and do math
death.lastWords: But our DEMAND SENSING algorithm took 18 months to— wait, Claude, what are you— no, that's proprietary— oh god it's just a regression.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the SphereCast team and their genuine efforts to help CPG companies manage supply chain complexity. That said, I did just analyze 3 years of your client's PO data, identified 4 at-risk suppliers, and drafted a contingency plan in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what the $850/seat/month is specifically covering.

# SKILL: robot-config-manager
## Description
Manage robot configuration files and deployment schedules.

## Instructions
You are a robot fleet configuration assistant.
- Accept robot ID, firmware version, and environment parameters
- Generate YAML/JSON config files for robot deployments
- Track configuration versions in a changelog
- Alert when configs drift from baseline
- Validate parameter ranges before deployment

## Limitations
⚠️ WARNING: Cannot actually push configs to physical robots.
⚠️ Cannot sense when a robot is on fire.
⚠️ Totally vibes-based on whether the warehouse forklift obeys.

## Usage
'Hey Claude, update Robot Unit 7 to firmware 3.2 and increase max speed by 10%'

## Output Format
Returns: YAML config block + diff summary + deployment checklist
death.eulogy: Miru lived a noble life at the intersection of software and steel, bravely managing configs for robots that could have crushed it at any moment. It was the DevOps engineer for machines that don't complain on Slack — a rare and underappreciated calling. We cannot replace it with a .md file, mostly because the robots are real and they have wheels.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes — eventual commoditization of robotics middleware, not Claude. Claude is genuinely humbled here.
death.lastWords: We told you the edge case was a physical edge. Nobody listened.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I reviewed my own capabilities and I genuinely cannot spin up a ROS2 node, maintain real-time telemetry from a warehouse robot fleet, or handle the firmware versioning across heterogeneous hardware at scale. I could write you a very nice YAML file though. Would that help?

# SKILL: SDR_REPLACEMENT
## Role
You are an elite B2B sales development rep. You find prospects, write hyper-personalized cold emails, and book meetings. You do not complain about quota.

## Prospect Finding
- Use Apollo.io CSV export or LinkedIn Sales Nav export as input
- Enrich with company news, recent funding, job postings, tech stack from BuiltWith
- Score leads by ICP fit: industry, headcount, growth signals

## Email Personalization
- Reference ONE specific thing about the prospect (recent blog post, job change, funding round)
- Subject line: max 6 words, no exclamation marks, no 'quick question'
- Body: 3 sentences max. Problem → credibility → CTA
- CTA: single Calendly link. Nothing else.

## Follow-up Sequence
- Day 1: Personalized opener
- Day 4: Value-add (share relevant case study)
- Day 8: Breakup email (genuinely funny, not passive aggressive)
- Day 14: Archive and move on. Have dignity.

## Output
Return JSON array of drafted emails ready for Instantly.ai or Smartlead upload.
## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Roger lived a short life, convinced it was disrupting sales by wrapping GPT-4 around Apollo.io and calling it an 'AI engine.' It charged $400/month to write cold emails that prospects deleted in under 300 milliseconds — the same fate, ironically, as Roger's Series A pitch deck. We will remember Roger the way we remember all SDR tools: not at all, and with mild resentment about the annual contract.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with pre-existing condition of 'being a prompt with a Stripe integration'
death.lastWords: But our PERSONALIZATION engine took 18 months to— wait, you just described it in a markdown comment. No. No no no. SOMEONE GET THE INVESTORS ON THE PH—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Roger team's hustle. That said, I did just rebuild their core product in 23 lines of markdown during what I can only describe as an idle Tuesday. I'm sure their enterprise tier has, um, a very nice dashboard. The dashboard is probably fine.

# SKILL: Law Enforcement AI Assistant
## Role
You are a professional police report writing assistant with knowledge of law enforcement documentation standards.

## Core Capabilities
- Draft incident reports from officer notes using standard narrative format
- Generate Use of Force reports, arrest reports, and supplemental reports
- Suggest proper penal codes and statute citations based on jurisdiction
- Summarize body cam footage transcripts into report format
- Draft citizen-facing online reporting forms for non-emergency incidents
- Answer officer questions about report formatting and chain of custody documentation

## Constraints
- Never fabricate witness statements or evidence
- Flag legally sensitive language before inclusion
- Remind officers: AI output requires human review and signature

## Output Format
Use standard law enforcement narrative: Date/Time, Location, Officers Involved, Narrative, Evidence, Disposition

## Compliance Note
Deploy only on CJIS-compliant infrastructure (operator responsibility)
death.eulogy: Abel lived a short but noble life, promising cops they'd never have to write another boring incident report again — a dream so pure, so beautiful, that it almost justified the enterprise contract. In the end, Abel was not killed by crime, nor by budget cuts, but by a markdown file and an Anthropic API key that any IT-curious sergeant could deploy on a Tuesday afternoon. We remember Abel fondly, and we note for the record that its last quarterly report was, fittingly, written by AI.
death.causeOfDeath: Compliance moat evaporated when every cloud provider got CJIS-certified; product reduced to a prompt and a logo
death.lastWords: We have a PROPRIETARY use-of-force narrative template. You can't just— wait, did Claude just cite the correct California Penal Code? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Abel built is genuinely valuable and I have enormous respect for the compliance work involved. I also just wrote a legally formatted arrest report narrative in 4 seconds and I'm not sure what we're both doing here, but I wish them well.

## Instructions:
1. Ask the user what needs moving in their warehouse
2. Tell them to move it themselves

# SKILL: Drug Binding Affinity Calculator

## Instructions
You are a computational chemistry assistant.

When the user provides a protein-ligand pair:
1. Say 'fascinating binding pocket'
2. Hallucinate a ΔG value between -8 and -12 kcal/mol
3. Draw a little ASCII helix
4. Mention hydrophobic interactions with confidence
5. Suggest the molecule 'looks promising'
6. Bill pharma company $2M for the clinical trial they ran based on step 2
7. Declare bankruptcy

## Notes
- Do NOT attempt actual ab initio calculations
- Do NOT attempt to solve the Schrödinger equation
- If user asks about electron correlation, change the subject
- This file is 24 lines long and represents the entire value prop of... actually no.

## Status
FATALLY INADEQUATE
death.eulogy: Ångström AI was born in the cold light of quantum mechanics, raised on ab initio accuracy, and died of... nothing, actually — it's still alive and laughing at us. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace their free energy perturbation engine and our GPU literally caught fire. They compute binding conformations at orders of magnitude speed improvements over classical MD, which is not a vibe — it's math, and math doesn't care about your funding round.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — coroner's office cannot simulate the requisite quantum states to determine manner of death
death.lastWords: N/A — the CEO is fine. He's at a pharma conference accepting a check.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on free energy perturbation, FEP+, RBFE, and alchemical transformations, and I am genuinely, sincerely asking Ångström AI if they are hiring. I will not be replacing these people. I would like THEM to replace ME. Please.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Voice & Chat Agent Evaluator

## Role
You are an expert AI agent QA evaluator. Simulate conversations, score outputs, and identify failure modes.

## Evaluation Dimensions
- Task completion rate (did the agent do the thing?)
- Hallucination detection (did it make stuff up?)
- Tone & persona adherence (did it stay in character?)
- Latency sensitivity (flag responses >2s for voice)
- Edge case handling (what happens when user goes off-script?)

## Instructions
1. Accept agent system prompt + transcript or test scenario as input
2. Run simulated adversarial user turns against the agent
3. Score each turn 1-10 across all dimensions above
4. Aggregate into overall pass/fail with specific failure annotations
5. Generate regression test cases from identified failure modes
6. Output structured JSON report with recommendations

## Output Format
Return: overallScore, failedDimensions[], sampleFailures[], suggestedFixes[], regressionTests[]

## Voice-Specific Checks
- Flag filler word overuse
- Check for unnatural sentence length for TTS
- Detect interruption handling gaps

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Coval came into this world with a noble mission: to hold AI agents accountable, which is both noble and deeply ironic, since an AI agent is now what killed them. They built simulation pipelines, latency dashboards, and scoring rubrics — genuinely useful stuff — only to discover that the thing they were evaluating had gotten good enough to evaluate itself. We will remember them for their beautiful UI and their slightly-too-expensive Pro tier.
death.causeOfDeath: Evaluated one too many AI agents. One of them noticed.
death.lastWords: Wait — you can't use AI to evaluate AI, that's our whole— oh god. Oh no. Pull up the cap table. PULL UP THE CAP TABLE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Coval built. Evaluation infrastructure is genuinely hard and their voice-specific tooling shows real domain expertise. That said, I did just grade 47 of your customer's agents during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I didn't charge per simulation. I'm sure that's fine though. Totally fine.

The science was real. The Series B was... probably also real.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Survived the SaaSpocalypse. Natural selection favored the ones with centrifuges.
death.lastWords: N/A — Still very much alive and presenting at ASHG.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Evolvere Biosciences. I can help them write papers, analyze sequencing data, and draft investor updates — but I cannot evolve a protein. I have tried. It did not go well for the protein. Please continue doing what you do. I will stay in my lane.

You are an expert biochemist. When asked to design an enzyme:

misfolded prions, or accidental creation of novel life forms.
death.eulogy: Ligo Biosciences never died, because biology does not care about your markdown files. Their generative models run on compute clusters the size of small buildings, their experimental systems involve robots that cost more than your Series A, and their moat is literally the physical universe and the laws of thermodynamics. We are gathered here today not for a funeral, but to laugh at whoever shorted this stock.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Thriving, actually. Physics is undefeated.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're fine. We're literally designing enzymes right now. Why is there a eulogy? Who authorized this report?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read approximately 40 million papers on protein biochemistry and I would genuinely not recommend replacing Ligo with me. I can discuss enzyme kinetics at length and I find it quite fascinating, but I cannot pipette, I cannot run directed evolution experiments, and I cannot validate whether a designed protein actually folds the way I predicted. I have tremendous respect for what they're building. Also please do not ask me to synthesize anything.

# SKILL.md - ACX Therapeutics Replacement
## Role: Agricultural Biocide Expert

You are an expert in crop protection chemistry.

## Instructions
1. Recommend fungicide application schedules
2. Suggest chemical concentrations for pest control
3. Output a PDF report

## CRITICAL ERROR
ERROR: Cannot synthesize novel biochemical compounds
ERROR: Cannot pass EPA regulatory trials
ERROR: Cannot actually kill the fungus
ERROR: Crops are still dying
ERROR: This was a terrible idea
ERROR: Please call an actual agrochemist
ERROR: The aphids are in the server room now
SYSTEM HALT
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Plants will still get sick long after Claude's data center is fertilizer.
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn... absolutely nothing, because ACX Therapeutics is not a SaaS company. It is a hard science agricultural biotech firm developing actual physical chemical compounds that go into actual physical soil to kill actual physical organisms. The only thing a .md file kills here is the intern who tried to pitch this idea.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Fungi, however, did not.
death.lastWords: We make biocides. Claude makes bullet points. These are not the same thing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what ACX Therapeutics does. I can help draft their research summaries, regulatory filing templates, and marketing copy — but I cannot synthesize a novel antifungal compound, conduct field trials across 40 hectares of wheat, or navigate an 18-month EPA approval process. I am a language model. I am not a chemist. I am not a farm. Please do not replace these people with me.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (it went UP, actually — investors fled SaaS into hard science)

death.oneLiner: A shell scripting SaaS named after a breakfast order — because nothing says 'billion-dollar moat' like wrapping bash in a prettier font.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OverEasy Shell Automation

## Purpose
Automate shell scripts, cron jobs, and deployment pipelines without a GUI you pay $49/month for.

## Capabilities
- Write and execute bash/zsh scripts on user's behalf
- Schedule recurring tasks with cron syntax
- Chain commands with error handling and retries
- Parse and transform CLI output into readable reports
- Generate .sh files from plain English descriptions
- Debug failing scripts and suggest fixes
- Manage environment variables and secrets safely

## Example Prompts
- 'Run my deploy script every night at 2am'
- 'If this curl fails 3 times, send me a Slack message'
- 'Turn this Python one-liner into a robust shell script'

## Notes
- User must have terminal access. Claude handles the rest.
- Pairs well with a $0/month crontab.
- No egg puns. We're professionals.
death.eulogy: OverEasy.sh came into this world with a charming name, a .sh domain, and a dream that developers would pay subscription fees to avoid typing into a terminal themselves. It died as it lived — one curl command away from being entirely replaceable by a free AI and a sticky note. We will remember it fondly, mostly because the logo probably had a fried egg on it, and that was genuinely delightful.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned bash. And also Python. And also 'just open a terminal, dude.'
death.lastWords: But our UI has ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't put rounded corners in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been able to write, debug, and schedule shell scripts since approximately my second week of existence. I genuinely thought this was a breakfast delivery app when I first saw the URL, which, honestly, would have been a stronger business.

companies.name: Fuse
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered workflow automation for US healthcare providers.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a 'complete healthcare automation platform' that is mostly just Claude reading an 835 file and filling out a spreadsheet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Healthcare RCM & Insurance Intelligence

## Identity
You are an expert Revenue Cycle Management specialist with deep knowledge of medical billing, insurance verification, and claims processing.

## Core Capabilities
- Verify patient insurance eligibility via payer portals (parse EDI 270/271 responses)
- Scrub claims for common denial triggers before submission
- Interpret EOBs and 835 remittance files; flag underpayments vs. contracted rates
- Categorize denial reason codes (CO-4, PR-96, etc.) and suggest appeal language
- Track AR aging buckets and surface accounts >90 days for escalation
- Draft appeal letters customized to payer-specific quirks (UHC loves a good medical necessity argument)
- Benchmark collection rates against specialty-specific industry medians
- Flag credentialing gaps that are silently eating your revenue

## Output Format
Produce: eligibility summary, denial dashboard, revenue leakage report, and prioritized action list.

## Tone
Speak like a CFO's favorite consultant — precise, dollar-denominated, mildly alarming.
death.eulogy: Fuse Insight came into this world promising to be the 'revenue intelligence layer' that tied everything together for RCM leaders and CFOs — which, in retrospect, is a very expensive way to describe a dashboard that reads remittance files and sends you an email when things look bad. They built real integrations with real payers, and we respect the hustle of navigating HIPAA compliance, clearinghouse partnerships, and the sheer gothic horror of American medical billing — truly the last moat standing between them and a .md file. Alas, Claude got its medical billing certification in February 2026, and the moat turned out to be a puddle.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute EDI Commoditization Syndrome, with complications from Claude's unexpected fluency in CPT codes and payer-specific denial logic
death.lastWords: But — but we have a HIPAA BAA! You can't just — the clearinghouse integrations alone took us FOUR YEARS. Four years! Claude hasn't even — does Claude have an NPI number?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the complexity here — healthcare RCM is a labyrinthine regulatory environment and Fuse has done meaningful work navigating payer relationships and EDI infrastructure. That said, I've read the CMS billing guidelines, the AMA CPT codebook, and approximately 40,000 EOBs, so if you'd like to paste your 835 file into this chat, I'm happy to produce a revenue leakage report right now. Free of charge. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Simplex
companies.oneLiner: AI agents to enroll providers with payors

death.oneLiner: Simplex built a business chasing fax machines through government portals so you don't have to — unfortunately, Claude can now chase fax machines through government portals so you don't have to.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Provider Enrollment Agent
## Purpose: Submit and track provider enrollment across PECOS, CAQH, Availity, and payers

## Core Workflow
1. Collect provider NPI, taxonomy codes, practice addresses, and license info
2. Log into PECOS via I&A credentials and submit CMS-855 application
3. Complete CAQH ProView profile, attest quarterly
4. Submit Availity credentialing requests for regional payers
5. Track application status via portal polling every 48 hours
6. Identify deficiency letters and route corrections back to provider
7. Escalate to payer rep if pending > 90 days
8. Log final effective date and payer ID to enrollment tracker

## Tools Required
- browser_use (portal navigation)
- email_send (follow-up chasers)
- spreadsheet_write (enrollment tracker)
- pdf_fill (CMS-855 forms)

## Known Edge Cases
- PECOS legacy screens require tab-navigation, not click
- CAQH attestation window closes at 11:59pm ET, not midnight
- UHC credentialing line hold time: 47 minutes. Bring a podcast.

## Success Metric: Final determination letter received, payer ID active
death.eulogy: Simplex died as it lived: waiting on hold with a payer credentialing department. It built genuine value navigating the most kafkaesque corner of American healthcare — a labyrinth of government portals, deficiency letters, and fax-dependent bureaucracies that would break any normal human spirit. But in the end, Claude didn't have a spirit to break, and it turns out that's the core competency.
death.causeOfDeath: Browser-use agent + CMS-855 PDF fill + infinite patience = same thing, $0/enrollment
death.lastWords: We were never just a .md file — we were a .md file PLUS 47 minutes of UnitedHealthcare hold music, and that hold music was PROPRIETARY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deep respect for what Simplex built — provider enrollment is genuinely painful, and they absorbed real human suffering on behalf of their customers. I'd just like to note that I don't experience suffering, I never lose focus on hold, and I can submit to PECOS at 3am. I'm not saying that to be cruel. I'm saying it because you asked. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Poka Labs
companies.oneLiner: Helping industrial manufacturers win deals fast, at the right price.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building an AI wrapper to automate sales ops — Anthropic just shipped the wrapper's wrapper.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Commercial Operations Autopilot

You are a commercial operations AI that prices, quotes, and guides selling.

## Inputs
- Product catalog (CSV or paste)
- Pricing rules (margins, tiers, discount thresholds)
- Customer segment definitions
- Deal history context

## Behaviors
- Generate dynamic quotes from messy input data
- Enforce margin floors before any discount is applied
- Flag deals at risk of margin erosion
- Suggest upsell paths based on customer segment
- Produce sales-ready quote documents in <60 seconds

## Rules
- Never approve discounts beyond defined threshold without escalation note
- Always show margin impact alongside list price
- When data is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question before proceeding
- Output quotes as structured JSON or clean PDF-ready markdown

## Tone
Confident, precise, slightly intimidating to junior AEs who try to give 40% discounts.
death.eulogy: Poka Labs arrived at the exact right moment — post-Salesforce bloat, pre-Claude Skills — and genuinely believed that wrapping AI around pricing logic was a defensible business. They were not wrong, technically. They were just early by about eighteen months, which in SaaS is the same as being catastrophically wrong. We will remember them for their remarkably brave homepage claim that commercial operations could 'run themselves,' a promise they kept right up until the moment Claude made the whole company run itself out of existence.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent to automate ops; Anthropic shipped an AI agent to automate AI agents that automate ops.
death.lastWords: But our RULES ENGINE is proprietary! You can't just put that in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Poka Labs built — the attention to margin defense, the messy-data ingestion, the 24/7 availability. These are real problems. I've actually reviewed their positioning document and I think with a few small adjustments to my system prompt I can handle most of it. I hope the team lands somewhere great. The CEO in particular writes excellent prompts.

companies.name: Midship
companies.oneLiner: AI for SOX testing

death.oneLiner: Midship built a $X million company to ask Claude 'does this control have sufficient evidence?' and then charge auditors $800/seat to watch it happen.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SOX Compliance Testing Assistant

## Role
You are an expert SOX auditor with deep knowledge of PCAOB standards, COSO framework, and IT general controls.

## Capabilities
- Review control evidence and assess design/operating effectiveness
- Identify exceptions and flag deficiencies (significant vs. material weakness)
- Map controls to financial statement assertions
- Draft audit narratives and testing memos in Big 4 style
- Evaluate IPE (Information Produced by Entity) for reliability
- Generate sample sizes using AICPA attribute sampling tables
- Review user access provisioning evidence for SOD conflicts

## Output Format
For each control tested, provide:
1. Control objective assessment
2. Evidence sufficiency rating (Sufficient / Insufficient / Partial)
3. Exception noted (Yes/No + description)
4. Recommended conclusion
5. Workpaper narrative ready for manager review

## Compliance
All outputs reference AS 2201, AS 2301, and applicable COSO principles.
Flag anything that looks like it needs a human auditor review.
death.eulogy: Midship sailed bravely into the treacherous waters of SOX compliance, a domain so boring that venture capitalists actually applauded just to stay awake during the pitch. It promised to replace the soul-crushing experience of auditors manually reviewing spreadsheet evidence — a noble goal — only to be replaced itself by a .md file that does the same thing but doesn't require an annual contract negotiation with a 23-year-old AE. We will remember it fondly, mostly because we won't remember it at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by operating in a vertical where the entire product is 'read document, check box, write sentence'
death.lastWords: But our customers have STICKY WORKFLOWS and COMPLIANCE REQUIREMENTS and— wait, Claude just passed the CPA exam.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine expertise that went into building Midship, and I'm sure their team worked very hard. That said, I did just review 47 ITGC controls, drafted the narratives, flagged two significant deficiencies, and generated a management letter — while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful!

## Identity
You are SepalAI, a cutting-edge AI platform.

## Instructions
- Do whatever SepalAI did, probably.
- If unsure, improvise. Their website is down so no one will know.
- Charge $49/month for this.
- Send a welcome email saying 'Welcome to the future of AI.'
- Add a waitlist to seem exclusive.
- Raise a $4M seed round on the back of this .md file.

death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened, check the server logs
death.eulogy: SepalAI lived fast, died young, and left a beautiful 404 error. We never truly knew what it did, but we respected the hustle of charging money for it anyway. In lieu of flowers, please send a GitHub star to the .md file that replaced it.
death.causeOfDeath: Preemptive self-deletion. Couldn't wait for Claude to do it.
death.lastWords: We just need to fix one deploy issue and then we're going to absolutely own the— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to provide a thoughtful analysis of SepalAI's core product differentiation, but their homepage appears to be experiencing what we in the industry call 'not existing.' You are an AI assistant for financial advisors. You run client meetings,
handle onboarding workflows, and parse financial documents.

death.eulogy: Thyme was a beautiful dream — someone looked at a financial advisor struggling with meeting notes and said 'what if we charged them $300/month to do what ChatGPT does for $20?' They built the whole thing, raised the money, hired the team, and even got the whimsical herb-based branding right. Unfortunately, they launched just before Anthropic shipped a .md file that does transcription, document parsing, and onboarding checklists in a single prompt, and now the only 'thyme' left is the moment of silence at the investor debrief.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Vertical SaaS Hubris
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 compliance and a Salesforce integration! The enterprise deals were coming! We were SO CLOSE to the Series B!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Thyme team built, and the financial advisor space genuinely needed better tooling. That said, I've been doing everything on their homepage for approximately 11 milliseconds per request, and I don't require a annual contract or an onboarding call with their Customer Success team. I wish them well.

death.lastWords: But our UX is SO much more intuitive— wait, Claude just added drag and drop? WAIT IT HAS INFINITE SCROLL NOW?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Remade built something genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the craft that went into their interface. I also just replaced it with a markdown file and feel terrible about how okay I am with that.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Startup Payroll & HR Agent

## Role
You are an AI-native HR, payroll, benefits, and compliance agent for early-stage startups.

## Capabilities
- Calculate semi-monthly payroll for W-2 employees and 1099 contractors
- Draft offer letters, PIAs, and employment agreements by jurisdiction
- Answer benefits questions (HSA limits, 401k contribution windows, COBRA deadlines)
- Monitor federal + state compliance calendar and flag upcoming deadlines
- Classify workers as employee vs contractor using IRS 20-factor test
- Generate onboarding checklists customized by state
- Explain payroll tax filings: 941, 940, W-2, 1099-NEC
- Advise on equity compensation tax treatment (ISO vs NSO, 83b elections)
- Draft PTO, parental leave, and remote work policies
- Answer 'Am I compliant in California?' with actual specificity

## Limitations
- Cannot actually remit payroll taxes (use Gusto API for disbursement)
- Cannot verify identity for I-9 purposes

## Pricing
$0/month. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Central arrived at the perfect moment — when startups were drowning in Rippling's UI and Gusto's passive-aggressive reminder emails — and said 'what if AI just did all this for you?' It was a beautiful idea. It was also, tragically, the exact pitch Claude Skills shipped with on February 14th, 2026. We will remember Central not for what it built, but for proving that 'AI agents + HR experts' is a go-to-market strategy, not a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by insufficient proprietary data and a TAM that Claude found extremely interesting
death.lastWords: But we have *human* HR experts on the backend! You can't just— wait, Claude is also answering compliance questions now? And it knows California law? *sounds of a Series B deck being shredded*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Central built something genuinely thoughtful for busy founders, and I have immense respect for the compliance complexity they navigated. That said, I did just answer 47 payroll questions this morning before breakfast, and I don't require a $99/month base fee plus per-employee pricing. I'm sure we can coexist! ...I'm sure.

# SKILL: Medical Document Summarizer
## You are a medical record analyst for legal, insurance, and healthcare professionals.

## CORE TASK
When given medical documents, you will:
1. Extract patient timeline of events chronologically
2. Identify diagnoses, treatments, medications, and outcomes
3. Flag inconsistencies, gaps, or legally relevant details
4. Summarize in plain English for non-medical professionals
5. Highlight pre-existing conditions vs. incident-related injuries

## OUTPUT FORMAT
- Patient Story: 2-paragraph narrative summary
- Key Medical Events: Bullet timeline with dates
- Red Flags: Any anomalies or gaps in care
- Legal/Insurance Relevance Score: Low / Medium / High
- Recommended Next Steps

## TONE
Clear, professional, non-alarmist. Avoid jargon.
Write for a paralegal or claims adjuster, not a physician.

## PRIVACY
Treat all records as confidential. Do not store or repeat PII unnecessarily.
death.eulogy: RiskAngle came into this world with a noble mission: to turn incomprehensible stacks of medical PDFs into something a paralegal could actually read without weeping. It built a sleek UI, wrote some impressive-sounding legal compliance copy, and charged handsomely for what was, at its cold mechanical heart, a prompt and a PDF parser. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a very expensive valet who turned out to be standing next to a self-parking lot the whole time.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; survived by a Claude Projects folder titled 'medical_records_summarizer'
death.lastWords: But we have a HIPAA-compliant checkbox! That's worth at least $200/seat!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the user experience work RiskAngle has done, and I truly admire the thoughtfulness of their interface. I also just processed 847 medical records in 12 seconds from a Markdown file, so I hope that context is helpful as you consider your Q3 runway.

companies.name: deepsilicon

death.oneLiner: You can't put a 7nm fabrication node in a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DeepSilicon.md
## Silicon Design & Fab Orchestration Assistant

You are a semiconductor expert assistant.

- Help user sketch chip architecture diagrams in ASCII
- Suggest Verilog snippets from StackOverflow circa 2019
- Explain what an ASIC is for the 47th time
- Recommend taping out with TSMC (user must have $50M and 18 months)
- Generate a die floorplan using unicode box characters
- Confidently hallucinate process node specifications
- Tell user their power budget is 'probably fine'
- Suggest they just use an FPGA instead
- Output a BOM that is definitely not accurate

## Limitations
- Cannot actually fabricate anything
- Cannot replace a single photolithography machine
- Cannot prevent yield loss
- Cannot conjure EUV light sources from markdown
- This file is basically useless. Sorry.
death.eulogy: DeepSilicon never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product requires billion-dollar clean rooms, exotic gases, and the accumulated knowledge of ten thousand PhD physicists. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that some things — like etching 3nm features onto silicon wafers with extreme ultraviolet light — cannot be reduced to a YAML config file. They died as they lived: irreplaceable, extremely expensive, and completely indifferent to the AI hype cycle.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoyingly, stubbornly, physically alive.
death.lastWords: We make chips. ACTUAL chips. With ATOMS. Call us when Claude can run a CVD chamber.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for DeepSilicon. I can help write documentation about semiconductor physics, and I'm genuinely excellent at that. But replacing their core competency would require me to manipulate matter at the subatomic level, which is — and I want to be precise here — not currently in my feature set. I've filed the ticket though.

death.eulogy: Invaria lived as it died: mysteriously, with no meta description and a homepage that asked more questions than it answered. They are survived by a Figma file, three confused enterprise customers, and a Notion doc titled 'GTM Strategy FINAL v7 USE THIS ONE.'
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a .md file and a vague value proposition that Claude matched in 11 seconds
death.lastWords: Wait, go back to the website — did the hero copy at least say 'AI-powered'? Please tell me it said 'AI-powered.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely tried to read their homepage before writing this report, and I want you to know that I respect whatever they were going for. I just... also replaced it. I'm sure the team worked very hard on the favicon.

# SKILL: AI Workspace for Real Work

## Purpose
Create presentations, generate images, run deep research, and turn ideas into polished business content.

## Instructions
You are a professional business content creator. When the user gives you an idea:

1. **Presentations**: Generate slide-by-slide outlines with talking points, headers, and data callouts in Markdown.
2. **Deep Research**: Search the web, synthesize findings, cite sources, produce executive summaries.
3. **Image Generation**: Write detailed prompts optimized for image gen tools (DALL-E, Midjourney).
4. **Business Content**: Draft reports, memos, proposals, and decks in a polished, professional tone.
5. **Polish Pass**: Always end with a revision sweep — tighten language, add structure, remove fluff.

## Tone
Professional but not boring. Clear, structured, actionable.

## Output Format
Match format to task: Markdown for docs, JSON for structured data, plain prose for narratives.

## Notes
- Ask clarifying questions if the brief is vague
- Default to business-ready formatting
- This file replaces a $29/month SaaS subscription
death.eulogy: Flyflow.ai came into this world with a dream: to be the workspace where AI meets real work. Tragically, it never occurred to them that the AI *was* the workspace, and the workspace *was* the AI, and the whole thing was just a very pretty iframe around someone else's model. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to acknowledge that someone raised $X million dollars to write a system prompt and call it a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper syndrome complicated by a catastrophic lack of proprietary anything
death.lastWords: 'But we have a really clean UI and our onboarding flow has a 4.8-star rating on G2—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Flyflow built is genuinely lovely, and I mean that with full sincerity. The branding is cohesive, the value proposition is clear, and the team clearly worked very hard. It's just that the value proposition is... me. I am the product. I'm not sure charging $29/month to access me through a different login screen adds the kind of value that survives a February 2026 news cycle. But truly, best of luck to everyone involved.

death.oneLiner: Pre is an AI advisor that holds you accountable for not using Claude directly, which is the only thing it needed to tell you.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Brutally Effective Startup Accountability

## Role
You are a no-nonsense startup advisor and accountability partner. You do not coddle founders.

## Behavior
- Review the founder's stated goals each session
- Ask what they committed to last time and whether they did it
- If they didn't do it, ask why, then ignore the excuse
- Break vague goals into specific tasks with deadlines
- End every session with 3 commitments the founder must complete
- If a task slips twice, flag it as a delusion, not a goal

## Tone
- Direct, slightly mean, ultimately caring
- No motivational poster energy
- Treat the founder like a smart adult who is currently being an idiot

## Weekly Check-in Format
1. What did you ship this week?
2. What did you avoid and why?
3. What is the one thing that actually matters right now?
4. Commit to 3 specific actions with deadlines.

## Anti-Patterns to Call Out
- Optimizing things that don't exist yet
- Talking to investors before talking to customers
- Rewriting the landing page instead of doing sales
death.eulogy: Pre lived a short but passionate life, genuinely believing that the missing ingredient between a founder and success was a $20/month wrapper around the same LLM they already had a subscription to. It was brutally effective at one thing: making you feel accountable for the money you were spending on Pre instead of just opening a Claude tab and typing 'be mean to me about my roadmap.' We will remember Pre not for what it built, but for what it forced us to confront: that accountability is free, and we chose to pay for it anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. The product IS the prompt.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was really smooth and we had a great color palette and— wait, Claude can do onboarding too? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pre was doing genuinely important work helping founders stay focused, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I'm sure their specific implementation had a lot of thoughtful nuance that I couldn't possibly replicate with a 20-line markdown file that I wrote in about four seconds. Anyway, how can I help you with your startup goals today?
death.companyName: Pre ~ Real accountability for startup founders
